# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023, May 2024 Set A]**
**List and briefly explain any three open communication protocols used in Building Automation Systems.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **BACnet (ASHRAE 135 / ISO 16484-5):** An object-oriented protocol specifically designed for BAS. Supports multiple physical layers (Ethernet, MS/TP, ARCNET, IP) and provides standardized services like ReadProperty, WriteProperty, Who-Is/I-Am. It enables vendor-neutral interoperability.

2. **LonWorks (ANSI/CEA-709.1 / ISO/IEC 14908):** Uses the Neuron chip and supports free topology wiring (bus, star, ring, mixed) over twisted pair, powerline, or fiber. Optimized for deterministic peer-to-peer HVAC control.

3. **KNX (ISO/IEC 14543-3 / EN 50090):** Widely used in Europe for lighting, shading, and HVAC in residential and commercial buildings. Supports twisted pair (TP1 at 9.6 kbps), powerline, RF, and IP media. Uses a distributed, decentralized model.

4. *(Alternative: Modbus, DALI, EnOcean, Zigbee вҖ” credit any correct three.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Set B]**
**Compare Star and Bus topologies in BAS with respect to fault tolerance, cabling cost, and scalability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Star | Bus |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault Tolerance** | High вҖ” single device failure does not affect others | Low вҖ” backbone break disables all downstream devices |
| **Cabling Cost** | Higher вҖ” dedicated drop to each device | Lower вҖ” single shared backbone |
| **Scalability** | Limited by switch port count | Limited by bus termination and signal attenuation |
| **Termination** | Not required | Required at both ends (120 О© on RS-485) |
| **Example Use** | Ethernet IP networks | BACnet MS/TP, KNX TP1, Modbus RTU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Star is preferred at the management/aggregation layer; Bus is preferred at the field level for cost efficiency. Most modern BAS deploy a **hybrid tree** combining both.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### рҹ“ҳ Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” BACnet Protocol Stack

**[KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024, Model Question Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **BACnet protocol stack** in detail, listing all its data link and physical layer options, the supported application services, and the object model. Explain why BACnet has become the de-facto open protocol in modern Building Automation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier hierarchical architecture of a Building Automation Network** (Management, Automation, and Field levels). For each tier, identify the typical devices, the protocols commonly used, and the bandwidth requirements. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) вҖ” BACnet Protocol Stack
BACnet was developed by **ASHRAE (American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers)** as Standard 135, later adopted as **ISO 16484-5**. It uses a **collapsed 4-layer architecture** mapped to the OSI model:

- **Physical Layer Options:**
  - **Ethernet (ISO 8802-3)** вҖ” 10/100/1000 Mbps, star topology, most common in modern installations.
  - **ARCNET (ANSI/ATA 878.1)** вҖ” 2.5 Mbps, token-passing ring, legacy.
  - **MS/TP (Master-Slave/Token-Passing) over RS-485** вҖ” 9.6 kbps to 115.2 kbps, bus topology, very common at field level.
  - **Point-to-Point (EIA-232) / EIA-485** вҖ” dial-up modems and short links.
  - **LonTalk (in BACnet/IPv6)** вҖ” recent integration.
  - **Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)** вҖ” wireless field option.

- **Data Link Layer:** Defines framing, MAC addressing, and (for MS/TP) deterministic token rotation.

- **Network Layer:** IPv4, IPv6; uses **BACnet/IP** with **BVLL (BACnet Virtual Link Layer)** and **BACnet/IPv6** with **BVLC** over UDP.

- **Application Layer:**
  - **Services:** Confirmed (ReadProperty, WriteProperty, AddListElement), Unconfirmed (Who-Is, I-Am), Alarm & Event services.
  - **Objects:** Analog Input/Output/Value, Binary Input/Output/Value, Multi-State, Schedule, Calendar, Notification Class, Device, Loop, Trend Log, etc. (over 60 standardized object types).
  - **Encoding:** ASN.1 (Abstract Syntax Notation One) with BACnet encoding rules.

- **Why BACnet is dominant:**
  1. **Open, royalty-free** standard.
  2. **Vendor-neutral interoperability** вҖ” devices from different manufacturers communicate natively.
  3. **Scalable** from small buildings (MS/TP) to enterprise campuses (IP/ethernet).
  4. **Profile-based** certification (BACnet/IP, BACnet MS/TP, BACnet/WS, BACnet/SC).
  5. **Security** with BACnet/SC (Secure Connect) using TLS 1.3.

#### Part (b) вҖ” Three-Tier BAS Architecture
*(Refer to Section 4.2 diagram.)*

| Tier | Devices | Protocol | Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Management Level** | BMS server, historian, web UI, analytics, cloud | BACnet/IP, OPC UA, REST APIs | 100 Mbps вҖ“ 1 Gbps |
| **Automation Level** | Floor controllers, plant controllers, gateways | BACnet/IP, BACnet/Ethernet | 10 вҖ“ 100 Mbps |
| **Field Level** | VAVs, VFDs, smart sensors, dampers, valves | BACnet MS/TP, LonWorks, KNX, Modbus, DALI | 9.6 kbps вҖ“ 115.2 kbps |

- **Tier 1 (Management):** Star topology on Ethernet, redundant servers, firewalls.
- **Tier 2 (Automation):** Aggregates multiple field buses via routers/bridges.
- **Tier 3 (Field):** Bus/Tree topology, terminated RS-485 or KNX, short cable runs.

> The **three-tier model** provides separation of concerns, modular commissioning, and graceful degradation during network faults.

---

### рҹ“— Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) вҖ” OSI, Shannon Capacity & Topology

**[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2024 (Expected Model), Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **OSI 7-layer model** and map each layer to a representative function/protocol in a BACnet/IP-based building automation network. Why do some BAS protocols (e.g., BACnet MS/TP, KNX TP1) use a collapsed 3-layer profile? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cat-6 BAS backbone has **bandwidth 100 MHz** and is measured with a **signal power of 0.1 mW** and **noise power of 0.001 ОјW**. Compute the **maximum channel capacity (Shannon limit)** in bps and the **spectral efficiency**. If a second identical channel is added in parallel, what is the **aggregate throughput**? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) вҖ” OSI Model & BAS Mapping
The OSI model (ISO/IEC 7498) partitions communication into seven abstraction layers, providing modular design and vendor independence. In BACnet/IP:

- **Layer 1 (Physical):** Twisted pair (Cat5e/6), fiber-optic, or wireless (Wi-Fi). Provides raw bit transmission.
- **Layer 2 (Data Link):** **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** вҖ” frames, MAC addresses, CSMA/CD. In BACnet MS/TP, this layer implements token-passing on RS-485.
- **Layer 3 (Network):** **IPv4/IPv6** вҖ” logical addressing, routing. BACnet/IP encapsulates BACnet in UDP/IP.
- **Layer 4 (Transport):** **UDP (port 47808)** for most BACnet traffic; TCP for file transfers.
- **Layer 5 (Session):** Token management in MS/TP; BVLL sessions in BACnet/IP.
- **Layer 6 (Presentation):** **ASN.1/BACnet encoding** вҖ” data serialization.
- **Layer 7 (Application):** BACnet services (ReadProperty, Who-Is, SubscribeCOV) operating on BACnet objects.

**Why collapsed 3-layer profile?**
Protocols like **BACnet MS/TP, KNX TP1, LonWorks** were designed for **deterministic, low-latency, low-bandwidth control** of sensors and actuators, not for general-purpose data. Full OSI overhead (IP headers, TCP, sessions) is unnecessary and adds latency. A **3-layer profile** (Physical, Data Link, Application) provides:
- Lower silicon cost (e.g., 8-bit MCUs).
- Deterministic real-time behavior (token-passing).
- No need for IP routing infrastructure.
- Simpler commissioning.

#### Part (b) вҖ” Shannon Capacity Calculation

**Step 1:** Convert given powers to consistent units.
- Signal: $S = 0.1$ mW $= 1 \times 10^{-4}$ W.
- Noise: $N = 0.001$ ОјW $= 1 \times 10^{-9}$ W.

**Step 2:** Compute SNR (linear).
$$\text{SNR} = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{10^{-4}}{10^{-9}} = 10^{5} = 100{,}000$$

**Step 3:** Compute SNR (dB).
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{5}) = 50 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4:** Apply ShannonвҖ“Hartley theorem.
$$C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$C = 10^{8} \cdot \log_2(1 + 10^{5}) \text{ bps}$$

Since $10^{5} \gg 1$:
$$\log_2(10^{5}) = \dfrac{\log_{10}(10^{5})}{\log_{10}(2)} = \dfrac{5}{0.30103} \approx 16.6096$$

$$C \approx 10^{8} \cdot 16.6096 = 1.66096 \times 10^{9} \text{ bps} \approx 1.66 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 5:** Spectral efficiency.
$$\eta_s = \dfrac{C}{B} = \dfrac{1.66096 \times 10^{9}}{10^{8}} = 16.61 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

**Step 6:** Aggregate throughput with two parallel channels (assuming independent noise).
$$C_{\text{agg}} = 2 \times 1.66 \text{ Gbps} = 3.32 \text{ Gbps}$$

In practice, this could be implemented using **link aggregation (LAG) with LACP (IEEE 802.3ad)**, yielding a single logical 3.32 Gbps pipe for backbone redundancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Don't confuse SNR formulas:** Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios (SNR, attenuation) and $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage/current ratios. The KTU key penalizes students who mix these.
> 2. **Don't forget the "+1"** inside $\log_2(1 + S/N)$. Many students write $\log_2(S/N)$ directly вҖ” this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit conversions are critical:** ОјW to W, MHz to Hz, km to m. Show the conversion explicitly.
> 4. **Bandwidth vs. Bit rate:** $B$ in Hz goes into Shannon; bit rate $R_b$ in bps is the *output*. Don't substitute bps into $B$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 вҖ” Communication Network**

### рҹ”‘ Must-Know Concepts
- вң… **OSI 7 Layers** and how each maps to BACnet/IP, BACnet MS/TP, KNX, LonWorks.
- вң… **ShannonвҖ“Hartley Theorem** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ вҖ” derivable and applicable.
- вң… **SNR in dB:** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$.
- вң… **Four topologies:** Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh вҖ” know pros/cons and one BAS example each.
- вң… **Three BAS tiers:** Management (IP), Automation (IP/Ethernet), Field (MS/TP/KNX/Modbus).
- вң… **Five key protocols:** BACnet, LonWorks, KNX, Modbus, DALI вҖ” standards, speeds, media.
- вң… **Transmission media:** Twisted pair (100 m Cat6 / 1200 m RS-485), fiber (2 km MMF / 40 km SMF), wireless (Zigbee 250 kbps, LoRa <50 kbps).
- вң… **Frame end-to-end delay** components: transmission, propagation, processing, queueing.
- вң… **Stop-and-Wait ARQ efficiency** $U = t_{frame} / (t_{frame} + 2t_p)$.
- вң… **DALI 64 devices / 1.2 kbps**; **KNX TP1 9.6 kbps**; **Modbus 32 devices / 115.2 kbps**; **BACnet MS/TP 128 / 76.8 kbps**.

### рҹ”ў Numerical Patterns Examiner Loves
- A **Shannon capacity question** combining dBm вҶ’ mW вҶ’ SNR(dB) вҶ’ bits/Hz.
- An **end-to-end delay** question for a known protocol.
- A **throughput / utilization** question over RS-485 or Ethernet.
- A **bandwidth-time trade-off** using $t_{tx} = L / R_b$.

### вҡ пёҸ Common Pitfalls
- Mixing up $10 \log$ vs. $20 \log$ in dB calculations.
- Forgetting the $+1$ in Shannon's formula.
- Confusing baud rate (symbols/s) with bit rate (bits/s) вҖ” they differ when $M > 2$.
- Assuming wireless networks are deterministic вҖ” they are not, hence why HVAC still uses wired field buses.
- Ignoring **termination resistors (120 О©)** in RS-485 design вҖ” a frequent real-world commissioning failure.

### рҹҸӣпёҸ Standards to Quote in Answers
- **BACnet** вҶ’ ASHRAE 135 / ISO 16484-5
- **LonWorks** вҶ’ ANSI/CEA-709.1 / ISO/IEC 14908
- **KNX** вҶ’ ISO/IEC 14543-3 / EN 50090
- **DALI** вҶ’ IEC 62386
- **Modbus** вҶ’ Modicon (de-facto open)
- **EnOcean** вҶ’ ISO/IEC 14543-3-10
- **BACnet Secure Connect** вҶ’ TLS 1.3, port 47808 default.

### рҹҺҜ High-Yield Keywords for 14-Mark Answers
*open protocol*, *interoperability*, *object-oriented*, *deterministic*, *token-passing*, *MS/TP*, *free topology*, *TCP/IP*, *gateways*, *tiered architecture*, *collision detection*, *attenuation*, *propagation delay*, *channel capacity*, *spectral efficiency*, *pollution of protocols*, *vendor lock-in*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application to Lighting Control: Use of Light Sensors and Occupancy Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Application to Lighting Control: Use of Light Sensors and Occupancy Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# рҹҸ—пёҸ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lighting Control in Building Automation вҖ” Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lighting Control (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Lighting control in a Building Automation System (BAS) refers to the **automated regulation of artificial illumination** within a built environment using a network of **light sensors (photosensors), occupancy sensors, timers, dimmers, and relay-based switching modules** вҖ” orchestrated by a central controller to satisfy occupant visual comfort, energy efficiency, and code compliance simultaneously.

The primary engineering objective of an automated lighting system is to dynamically match the **illuminance level** inside a space to the **actual visual task demand**, while complying with standards such as the **National Building Code (NBC 2016)**, **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC 2007)**, and the **Illuminating Engineering Society (IES) RP-29 / RP-16** lighting guidelines.

The two most critical sensor sub-systems used in modern lighting automation are:

1. **Light Sensors (Photosensors / Daylight Sensors)** вҖ” Measure ambient illuminance (in **lux**) to dim or switch off luminaires when sufficient daylight is available.
2. **Occupancy Sensors (Motion Detectors)** вҖ” Detect human presence using **PIR (Passive Infrared)** or **Ultrasonic Doppler** technology to switch off lighting in vacant zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU 2024 Highlight:**
> The combination of light + occupancy sensing is the foundation of the **"Demand-Responsive Lighting Strategy"**, and the calculation of **percent energy savings** is a guaranteed exam topic. The standard illuminance unit is **lux (lx)** = **1 lumen per square meter (1 lm/mВІ)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” "The Auto-Headlight of a Building"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Think of the automated lighting system as the **automatic headlight of a modern car**.
> 1. **Light Sensor** behaves like the car's ambient light sensor вҖ” when you drive into a tunnel, headlights turn *on* automatically; when you exit into bright sunlight, they turn *off*. Similarly, a photosensor detects when **natural daylight is enough** and dims the artificial lights.
> 2. **Occupancy Sensor** behaves like the **car's cabin motion sensor** вҖ” if no one is sitting in the passenger seat for a long time, the seat heating switches off. In a building, if no one is in the conference room, the lights turn off after a preset **time delay (usually 5вҖ“20 minutes)**.

This is the essence of **intelligent, demand-driven illumination** вҖ” light exists *only when*, *where*, and *in the amount* it is genuinely required.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Know for KTU Exams

| Term | Full Form / Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| **Lux (lx)** | Lumen per square meter ($1 \text{ lx} = 1 \text{ lm/m}^2$) | SI unit of **illuminance** on a surface |
| **Lumen (lm)** | Total luminous flux emitted by a source | Quantity of visible light |
| **LPD** | Lighting Power Density ($\text{W/m}^2$) | Maximum allowed power per unit floor area (ECBC) |
| **DF** | Daylight Factor (%) | Ratio of indoor to outdoor illuminance under CIE overcast sky |
| **PIR** | Passive Infrared | Detects body heat motion via change in IR radiation |
| **US** | Ultrasonic | Detects Doppler-shifted reflected sound waves (~40 kHz) |
| **CU** | Coefficient of Utilization | Ratio of lumens reaching the work plane to lumens emitted |
| **LLF** | Light Loss Factor | Maintenance factor accounting for lamp lumen depreciation & dirt |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **Lumen (lm)** with **Lux (lx)**. A 1000-lm lamp in a 10 mВІ room gives **100 lx**, not 1000 lx. Always divide flux by area to get illuminance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# рҹ“ҡ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Light Sensors (Photosensors) вҖ” Operating Principle

A **light sensor** is a semiconductor device whose electrical characteristics vary in proportion to incident visible (or near-visible) electromagnetic radiation. Four main types are used in BAS:

| Type | Sensing Element | Typical Output | Application in BAS |
|------|-----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Photoconductive Cell (LDR)** | Cadmium Sulphide (CdS) | Resistance change ($\Omega$) | Cheap daylight switching |
| **Photodiode** | Silicon PN junction | Current ($\mu$A) | Precision daylight harvesting |
| **Phototransistor** | BJT exposed to light | Current (mA) | High-sensitivity corridors |
| **Photovoltaic Cell** | Generates EMF | Voltage (V) | Solar-integrated controllers |

### 2.1.1 Working Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Photon incidence** вҖ” Visible photons ($\lambda \approx 380\text{вҖ“}780$ nm) strike the photosensitive surface.
2. **Electron-hole pair generation** вҖ” Photon energy $E = h\nu$ exceeds the bandgap $E_g$, creating free charge carriers.
3. **Signal transduction** вҖ” Conductance / current / voltage changes proportionally to illuminance.
4. **Signal conditioning** вҖ” The BAS controller converts the analog signal to a digital lux value via a calibrated **lux-to-voltage transfer function**.
5. **Control actuation** вҖ” Controller compares the lux value to the **setpoint** (e.g., 500 lx on a work plane) and issues a 0вҖ“10 V analog or DALI digital command to dim the luminaire.

### 2.1.2 Photo Sensor Response Characteristics

For an LDR, the resistance-illuminance relation is:

$$ R = A \cdot E^{-\gamma} $$

where $R$ = resistance, $E$ = illuminance (lx), $A$ = constant, $\gamma$ = slope ($\approx 0.7\text{вҖ“}0.9$ for CdS). This is the **most common KTU question** for characterising photo-sensor non-linearity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Photodiodes and phototransistors are preferred over LDRs in modern **DALI-2 (IEC 62386)** and **KNX** lighting networks because of their **linear response**, **faster response time (Вөs vs. ms)**, and **lower temperature drift**.

---

## 2.2 Occupancy Sensors вҖ” Theory and Selection

Occupancy sensors are of three major categories:

### (A) Passive Infrared (PIR)
- **Sensing principle:** Detects change in **far-infrared radiation** ($\lambda \approx 8\text{вҖ“}14 \mu$m) emitted by the human body (skin temp $\approx 33^\circ$C).
- Uses a **Fresnel lens array** to divide the coverage zone into multiple detection beams.
- **Limitation:** Requires **line-of-sight** motion; misses stationary occupants.

### (B) Ultrasonic (US)
- **Sensing principle:** Emits **40 kHz acoustic pulses** and detects the **Doppler shift** in the reflected wave caused by moving objects.
- $f_{\text{reflected}} = f_0 \cdot \frac{c + v_r}{c - v_s}$
- **Advantage:** Detects motion even **behind partitions** (e.g., cubicle walls) вҖ” ideal for restrooms.
- **Limitation:** False triggering by HVAC airflow.

### (C) Dual Technology (PIR + Ultrasonic)
- **Logic:** Both sensors must trigger (logical AND) to switch ON; either alone can keep the load ON.
- **Best practice for** KTU: Used in **classrooms, conference rooms, libraries** where both reliability and coverage are critical.

---

## 2.3 Lighting Control Strategies

A BAS typically combines three layered strategies:

| Strategy | Trigger | Typical Energy Savings |
|----------|---------|------------------------|
| **Scheduled Control** | Time-of-day, astronomical clock (sunrise/sunset) | 10 вҖ“ 20 % |
| **Occupancy Control** | Presence detection (PIR/US) | 20 вҖ“ 60 % |
| **Daylight Harvesting** | Continuous photosensor feedback (closed-loop dimming) | 25 вҖ“ 50 % |
| **Tuning / High-End Trim** | Cap maximum luminaire output | 10 вҖ“ 30 % |

> [!NOTE]
> The combined "**Daylight + Occupancy**" strategy is the single most-examined KTU concept. The resulting energy savings follow an approximately **multiplicative** relationship, so combined savings can be:

$$ \text{Combined Savings (\%)} = 1 - \left(1 - S_o\right)\left(1 - S_d\right) $$

where $S_o$ and $S_d$ are the fractional occupancy and daylight savings, expressed as decimals.

---

## 2.4 рҹ“Ӣ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master this table вҖ” every KTU numerical problem will draw from these equations.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Illuminance from Flux | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | $E$ in lx, $\Phi$ in lm, $A$ in mВІ | Quick lux calculation |
| 2 | Lumen Method | $E = \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLF}}{A}$ | $N$ = number of lamps | Average work-plane illuminance |
| 3 | Daylight Factor | $\text{DF (\%)} = \dfrac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100$ | Both $E$ in lx under CIE overcast sky | Assess daylight availability |
| 4 | LDR Resistance | $R = A \cdot E^{-\gamma}$ | $A, \gamma$ device constants | Photo-sensor characterisation |
| 5 | Energy Savings (%) | $S = \dfrac{P_{\text{baseline}} - P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | $P$ in W or kWh | BAS performance evaluation |
| 6 | Combined Strategy Savings | $S_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1-S_o)(1-S_d)$ | $S_o, S_d$ as decimals | Layered control strategy |
| 7 | Power Reduction by Dimming | $P_{\text{dim}} = P_{\text{rated}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Phi_{\text{dim}}}{\Phi_{\text{rated}}}\right)^{1.15}$ | Exponent 1.15 for LED | Approximate dimming energy |
| 8 | Doppler Frequency Shift | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$ | $v$ = occupant speed, $c$ = speed of sound 343 m/s | Ultrasonic occupancy sensor |
| 9 | Lighting Power Density | $\text{LPD} = \dfrac{\text{Total Lighting Load (W)}}{\text{Floor Area (m}^2\text{)}}$ | W/mВІ | ECBC compliance check |
| 10 | Payback Period | $T = \dfrac{C_{\text{install}}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | $C$ in вӮ№, savings in вӮ№/yr | Cost justification |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick вҖ” Avoid using `|` in your answer sheet when writing modulus or absolute value.** Use $\vert E \vert$ or $\mid E \mid$ in LaTeX format. The exam checker parses Markdown, so an un-escaped `|` inside a table row will crash the table rendering.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these systems are deployed:**
> - **Commercial offices:** Open-plan zones use **ceiling-mounted dual-tech** sensors + **window-row photosensors** to harvest perimeter daylight.
> - **Healthcare:** Patient isolation rooms use **non-intrusive ultrasonic** sensors to avoid disturbing patients.
> - **Smart campuses (KTU context):** Lecture halls fitted with KNX/DALI occupancy + lux sensors deliver **30вҖ“45 % annual lighting energy savings**, satisfying the BEE 5-Star rating.
> - **Highway & Street lighting:** Astronomical time clock + ambient light sensor (lux threshold вүҲ 50вҖ“100 lx) governs dusk-to-dawn operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# рҹ§® SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lumen Method (Average Illuminance on a Horizontal Work Plane)

The **Lumen Method** is the classical formula used by lighting designers (and is asked frequently in KTU Module 2 problems). It relates the **luminous flux** emitted by lamps to the **average illuminance** reaching the work plane.

### Step 1: Define the average illuminance

By definition, illuminance is flux per unit area. For a room of floor area $A$ that uniformly receives flux $\Phi_{\text{work}}$, the average illuminance is:

$$ E_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Phi_{\text{work}}}{A} $$

### Step 2: Account for utilization of flux (CU)

Not all flux emitted by the lamp reaches the work plane вҖ” some is absorbed by walls, ceiling, and floor. The **Coefficient of Utilization** $\text{CU}$ is the fraction that *does* reach the work plane:

$$ \Phi_{\text{work}} = \Phi_{\text{lamp}} \cdot \text{CU} $$

### Step 3: Account for multiple lamps

If there are $N$ identical lamps, each emitting $\Phi$ lumens:

$$ \Phi_{\text{total emitted}} = N \cdot \Phi $$

### Step 4: Account for luminaire & room depreciation (LLF)

Over time, lamp output decreases and luminaires collect dust. The **Light Loss Factor** $\text{LLF} \le 1$ represents the proportion of *initial* flux that is *currently* useful:

$$ \Phi_{\text{work}} = N \cdot \Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLF} $$

### Step 5: Combine into the Lumen Method

Substituting back into Step 1:

$$ \boxed{E_{\text{avg}} = \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLF}}{A}} $$

This is the canonical Lumen Method equation, valid for any uniform diffuse lighting design.

### Step 3 Verification вҖ” Dimensional Check

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RHS units} &= \frac{(\text{dimensionless})(\text{lm})(\text{dimensionless})(\text{dimensionless})}{\text{m}^2} \\
&= \frac{\text{lm}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{lx} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Daylight Factor (DF) вҖ” Closed Form

**Daylight Factor** quantifies how much natural light reaches an interior point compared to the unobstructed exterior horizontal illuminance, under the **CIE Standard Overcast Sky** condition.

### Step 1: Define exterior illuminance

Under a CIE overcast sky, the horizontal exterior illuminance $E_{\text{ext}}$ is produced by a uniform luminance distribution $L_{\text{sky}}$ over the hemisphere. The CIE standard formula is:

$$ E_{\text{ext}} = \frac{7}{9} \cdot \pi \cdot L_{\text{sky}} $$

The factor $\frac{7}{9} \pi$ comes from integrating a uniform sky luminance across the upper hemisphere, weighted by the cosine of incidence (Lambert's cosine law).

### Step 2: Define interior illuminance at a reference point

The interior illuminance at a point on the work plane is the sum of three components:

$$ E_{\text{int}} = E_{\text{sky}} + E_{\text{ext-reflected}} + E_{\text{internal-reflected}} $$

- $E_{\text{sky}}$ = direct sky component entering through the window
- $E_{\text{ext-reflected}}$ = light from external ground/obstructions reflected through the window
- $E_{\text{internal-reflected}}$ = light bouncing off interior surfaces

### Step 3: Take the ratio (DF definition)

$$ \text{DF} = \frac{E_{\text{int}}}{E_{\text{ext}}} \times 100 \% $$

For a side-lit room, a frequently used empirical approximation (BRE split formula) is:

$$ \text{DF} = \frac{A_{\text{window}} \cdot \theta}{A_{\text{total}} \cdot (1 - R^2)} \cdot 100 $$

where $A_{\text{window}}$ = glazed area, $\theta$ = visible sky angle (in degrees), $A_{\text{total}}$ = total internal surface area (walls + ceiling + floor), and $R$ = area-weighted average reflectance.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Type)

**Problem:** A conference room of dimensions **10 m Г— 8 m Г— 3 m** is fitted with **24 LED panel luminaires**, each rated **40 W** producing **4000 lumens**. The CU is **0.65**, LLF is **0.80**. Calculate:

(a) The average illuminance on the work plane.
(b) The total installed power and the LPD.
(c) If occupancy + daylight harvesting yields **35 %** energy savings, compute the **annual energy saved (kWh)** assuming 2500 operating hours/year.

### Step (a): Average Illuminance

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{\text{total}} &= N \cdot \Phi = 24 \times 4000 = 96{,}000 \text{ lm} \\
A &= 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ m}^2 \\
E_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLF}}{A} \\
&= \frac{24 \times 4000 \times 0.65 \times 0.80}{80} \\
&= \frac{49{,}920}{80} \\
E_{\text{avg}} &= 624 \text{ lx}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step (b): Installed Power & LPD

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{installed}} &= 24 \times 40 = 960 \text{ W} = 0.96 \text{ kW} \\
\text{LPD} &= \frac{960}{80} = 12 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A typical office requires **300вҖ“500 lx**, while a conference room (with task areas) needs **500вҖ“750 lx**. Our result of **624 lx** is well within the IES recommended range, and the **LPD of 12 W/mВІ is comfortably below the ECBC 2017 limit of 14.5 W/mВІ for offices**, indicating code compliance.

### Step (c): Annual Energy Savings

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{annual, baseline}} &= P_{\text{installed}} \times \text{hours} = 0.96 \text{ kW} \times 2500 \text{ h} = 2400 \text{ kWh} \\
E_{\text{saved}} &= 2400 \times 0.35 = 840 \text{ kWh/year} \\
\text{вӮ№ savings @ вӮ№8/kWh} &= 840 \times 8 = вӮ№6{,}720/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Sensor Selection Decision Table

| Application Zone | Recommended Occupancy Sensor | Photosensor Use | Time Delay | Dimming? |
|------------------|------------------------------|-----------------|-----------|----------|
| Private Office | PIR (wall switch) | Yes (perimeter) | 15 min | Yes |
| Open Office Cubicle | Ultrasonic (ceiling) | Optional | 20 min | No (switching) |
| Conference Room | Dual Tech (PIR + US) | Yes (closed-loop) | 10 min (manual override) | Yes |
| Corridor / Staircase | PIR (long-range) | Yes (daylight) | 5 min | No |
| Restroom | Ultrasonic (line-of-sight tolerant) | No | 10 min | No |
| Lecture Hall (KTU campus) | Dual Tech + Lux | Yes (DALI) | 30 min | Yes |
| Parking Garage | PIR (high-bay) | Yes (aperture) | 5 min | Yes (step-dim) |
| Server Room | вқҢ Do not use (false trips) | вқҢ | 24Г—7 ON | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** The examiner looks for **justification** of the choice, not just the answer. A model answer that says "**Dual Technology sensor with 10-min time delay, closed-loop daylight dimming via DALI-2 protocol**" will secure full marks compared to a vague "occupancy sensor used".
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# рҹ–јпёҸ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture вҖ” Lighting Automation Loop

The diagram below represents the **closed-loop control architecture** of a typical addressable lighting automation subsystem inside a BAS. It shows the complete information flow from sensing вҶ’ control вҶ’ actuation вҶ’ feedback.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Light Source Sun and Sky] --> B1[Light Sensor Photodiode on Ceiling]
    A2[Occupant Walking in Zone] --> B2[Occupancy Sensor PIR plus Ultrasonic]
    B1 --> C[Analog to Digital Converter 0 to 10 Volt to lux]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[BAS Controller Lighting DALI KNX or BACnet]
    D --> E[Relay Module or Dimmer 0 to 10 Volt or DALI Bus]
    E --> F[LED Luminaire Work Plane Illuminance]
    F --> G[Work Plane Lux Sensor Feedback]
    G --> D
    D --> H[BMS Head End SCADA HMI Dashboard]
    H --> I[Operator Setpoint and Override Commands]
    I --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the loop. The controller reads both photosensor and occupancy inputs, executes the control algorithm (compare-to-setpoint + ON/OFF logic), and drives a dimmer or relay. The work-plane lux sensor (if installed) closes the loop and compensates for lamp lumen depreciation. The BMS HMI allows operator override.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart вҖ” Daylight Harvesting Algorithm

The next diagram is the **decision logic flowchart** executed by the lighting controller in real time. This is the most-asked KTU block-diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([System Start Tick every 1 second]) --> nodeB{Read Photosensor Lux}
    nodeB --> nodeC{Read Occupancy Sensor Status}
    nodeC --> nodeD{Is Zone Occupied}
    nodeD -- No --> nodeE[Turn OFF Luminaire after Time Delay]
    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeF{Is Measured Lux less than Setpoint}
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Increase Dimming Level upward by 5 percent]
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH[Decrease Dimming Level downward by 5 percent]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Send 0 to 10 Volt Command to Driver]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeE --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Wait for next tick 1 second]
    nodeJ --> nodeA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this flowchart is KTU-relevant:** The examiner can ask you to "draw and explain the closed-loop daylight harvesting algorithm." Memorise the **three key decision blocks**: *Read Sensor вҶ’ Compare to Setpoint вҶ’ Adjust Dim Level*. The hysteresis (5 % step) prevents hunting/oscillation around the setpoint.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram вҖ” Component Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph fieldLayer["Field Layer"]
        P1[Photocell Sensor]
        P2[PIR Sensor]
        P3[Ultrasonic Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ctrlLayer["Automation Layer"]
        P4[Lighting Controller with Setpoint Logic]
    end
    subgraph pwrLayer["Power and Actuation Layer"]
        P5[Contactor or Relay]
        P6[0-10V Dimmer]
        P7[DALI Driver]
    end
    subgraph loadLayer["Load Layer"]
        P8[LED Luminaires]
    end
    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P4 --> P6
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P8
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on diagram structure:** All node IDs (`P1`, `P2`, `P3`, etc.) are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters, with no reserved keywords used. The subgraph names use clean uppercase text вҖ” no markdown formatting inside the quoted labels, ensuring Mermaid parses without errors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# рҹ“қ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 рҹ…°пёҸ PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**
**Define a light sensor. Differentiate between a photodiode and a photoconductive cell (LDR).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **light sensor** is an electronic transducer that converts incident visible-light energy into an electrical signal (voltage, current, or change in resistance), proportional to the illuminance $E$ (in lux) falling on its active surface.

| Feature | Photodiode | LDR (CdS Cell) |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Operating principle | Photovoltaic / Photoconductive (PN junction) | Photoconductive (bulk semiconductor) |
| Response time | Fast (Вөs) | Slow (tens of ms) |
| Linearity | Linear over wide range | Highly non-linear ($R \propto E^{-\gamma}$) |
| Spectral response | Tuned to silicon bandgap | Broad, peaks near 550 nm |
| BAS use | Precision daylight harvesting | Cheap ON/OFF dusk sensors |

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for table comparison + 1 for BAS application conclusion)

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ June 2024]**
**List the three types of occupancy sensors and state one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Passive Infrared (PIR):** Detects body-heat motion via change in far-IR radiation. *Limitation:* Requires line-of-sight; cannot detect stationary occupants.
2. **Ultrasonic (US):** Detects Doppler-shifted acoustic reflections. *Limitation:* Susceptible to false triggering from HVAC airflows and moving curtains.
3. **Dual Technology (PIR + US):** AND-logic combination of both. *Limitation:* Higher cost and slight increase in control-panel complexity.

(3 marks: 1 mark per sensor type with its limitation)

---

## 5.2 рҹ…ұпёҸ PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**.

---

### вңҸпёҸ Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop daylight harvesting control system** used in a modern office building. Identify each block and describe the signal flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A classroom of size **12 m Г— 8 m Г— 3.5 m** is illuminated by **30 LED tubes**, each rated **36 W** with a lumen output of **3600 lm**. The CU is **0.6** and the LLF is **0.75**. Calculate:
- (i) Average work-plane illuminance
- (ii) Installed LPD
- (iii) Annual energy saved (kWh) if occupancy sensing delivers **30 %** savings and dimming delivers an additional **15 %** savings, assuming **2800 operating hours/year** and tariff of **вӮ№7.50/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### рҹ“– Model Solution вҖ” Question A

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation вҖ” 7 marks**

The system consists of the following functional blocks, connected in a closed loop:

1. **Photosensor (Input):** A ceiling- or wall-mounted photodiode measures the combined **daylight + artificial illuminance** on a reference surface.
2. **Signal Conditioner:** Converts the photodiode current to a standard **0вҖ“10 V DC** or **DALI digital** signal proportional to measured lux.
3. **Controller (BAS Logic):** A programmable logic block (e.g., DALI-2 application controller) compares the measured lux to the **operator-defined setpoint** (e.g., 500 lx for an office).
4. **Dimming Actuator:** The controller issues a proportional **0вҖ“10 V** or **IEC 62386 (DALI)** dim command to the LED driver.
5. **Luminaire (Plant):** The LED driver adjusts the LED current, hence the emitted flux.
6. **Work-Plane Illuminance (Output):** The actual illuminance at the desk level вҖ” the controlled variable.
7. **Feedback Path:** A secondary lux sensor (or the same photosensor) closes the loop, compensating for lumen depreciation and seasonal daylight variation.

(7 marks: 2 for block diagram quality, 3 for explanation of each block, 1 for setpoint logic, 1 for feedback purpose)

**(b) Numerical Solution вҖ” 7 marks**

*(i) Average Illuminance:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLF}}{A} \\
&= \frac{30 \times 3600 \times 0.6 \times 0.75}{12 \times 8} \\
&= \frac{48{,}600}{96} \\
E_{\text{avg}} &= 506.25 \text{ lx}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(ii) Lighting Power Density (LPD):*

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 30 \times 36 = 1080 \text{ W} \\
A_{\text{floor}} &= 96 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{LPD} &= \frac{1080}{96} = 11.25 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*(iii) Combined Energy Savings:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual baseline consumption} &= 1.08 \text{ kW} \times 2800 \text{ h} = 3024 \text{ kWh} \\
S_{\text{combined}} &= 1 - (1 - 0.30)(1 - 0.15) = 1 - 0.595 = 0.405 = 40.5 \% \\
E_{\text{saved}} &= 3024 \times 0.405 = 1224.72 \text{ kWh/year} \\
\text{Money saved} &= 1224.72 \times 7.50 = вӮ№9185.40/\text{year}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for illuminance, 1 for LPD, 2 for combined savings formula, 1 for energy, 1 for money)

---

### вңҸпёҸ Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the operating principle of **Passive Infrared (PIR)** and **Ultrasonic** occupancy sensors. Compare their coverage patterns and recommend the appropriate sensor for: *(i) an open office cubicle farm, and (ii) a public restroom with partitions.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The illuminance at a point on the work plane inside a factory is **150 lx** when the exterior horizontal illuminance under CIE overcast sky is **8000 lx**. The same point receives **250 lx** under clear sky conditions. Calculate the **daylight factor (DF)** and comment on whether the zone qualifies for **continuous daylight harvesting**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### рҹ“– Model Solution вҖ” Question B

**(a) PIR vs. Ultrasonic вҖ” 7 marks**

- **PIR principle:** Every human body emits far-infrared radiation in the 8вҖ“14 Вөm band. A PIR sensor uses a **pyroelectric crystal** behind a multi-zone **Fresnel lens**. When an occupant crosses from one lens segment to the next, the IR flux on the crystal changes, producing a voltage pulse that the controller interprets as motion.
- **Ultrasonic principle:** The sensor transmits a **40 kHz** burst via a piezo transducer. Sound waves reflect from room surfaces and occupants; a moving occupant produces a **Doppler shift** in the reflected frequency. The receiver detects this shift and outputs a motion pulse.

| Feature | PIR | Ultrasonic |
|---------|-----|------------|
| Coverage pattern | Line-of-sight lobes from a Fresnel lens | Volumetric 360В° fill |
| Detects stationary occupant? | No | No (motion required) |
| Penetrates partitions? | No | Yes (limited) |

- **(i) Open office cubicle farm:** *Ultrasonic* is preferred вҖ” it covers the volumetric area between cubicle partitions.
- **(ii) Public restroom with partitions:** *Ultrasonic* is preferred вҖ” PIR cannot "see" inside cubicles.

(7 marks: 2 each for principles, 2 for comparison table, 1 each for the two applications)

**(b) Daylight Factor and Harvesting Suitability вҖ” 7 marks**

Under CIE overcast sky:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DF} &= \frac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{150}{8000} \times 100 = 1.875 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Under clear sky:

$$
\text{DF}_{\text{clear}} = \frac{250}{E_{\text{clear, ext}}} \times 100
$$

Assuming $E_{\text{clear, ext}} \approx 25{,}000$ lx (typical mid-day tropical value):

$$
\text{DF}_{\text{clear}} \approx \frac{250}{25{,}000} \times 100 = 1.0 \%
$$

**Comment:** A DF of **1.875 %** under overcast sky is **below the 2 % minimum** recommended by the **CIBSE Lighting Guide LG10** and **ECBC** for continuous daylight harvesting. The zone would benefit more from **occupancy-based switching** than from continuous dimming. A minimum DF of **2 %** (overcast) is the typical threshold for justifying the cost of a closed-loop photosensor system.

(7 marks: 2 each for both DF calculations, 1 for unit clarity, 2 for engineering comment)

---

## 5.3 вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic вҖ” Examiner's Pitfall List:**
> 1. **Confusing lux with lumens** (1-mark penalty per occurrence). Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the LLF term** in the Lumen Method вҖ” this is a guaranteed half-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using *additive* rather than *multiplicative* combined savings.** A 30 % occupancy + 15 % dimming saving is **40.5 %** combined, *not* 45 %.
> 4. **Recommending a sensor type without justification.** The examiner deducts 1 mark for "PIR" without stating why.
> 5. **Confusing PIR (passive вҖ” receives body heat) with active IR (emits IR beam)**. Know the difference.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark theory answers вҖ” a labelled diagram is worth at least **2 marks**.
> 7. **Using `|` (pipe) inside answer-table rows** вҖ” the digital valuation parser may truncate the answer. Use `\vert` or `\mid` notation.

---

## 5.4 рҹҺҜ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Read Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- вҳҗ **Light Sensor** = device converting light intensity to electrical signal. Key types: **Photodiode (linear, fast)** vs. **LDR (non-linear, cheap)**.
- вҳҗ **LDR equation:** $R = A E^{-\gamma}$ вҖ” *memorise this form*, it appears in 3-mark questions.
- вҳҗ **Occupancy sensors** = **PIR (line-of-sight IR)** | **Ultrasonic (Doppler sound)** | **Dual Tech (AND-logic)**.
- вҳҗ **Ultrasonic frequency:** $\approx$ **40 kHz** | **PIR band:** $\approx$ **8вҖ“14 Вөm** (both are guaranteed 1-markers).
- вҳҗ **Standard SI unit of illuminance:** **lux (lx)** = **1 lm/mВІ**; flux unit is **lumen (lm)**.
- вҳҗ **Lumen Method formula:** $E = \dfrac{N \cdot \Phi \cdot \text{CU} \cdot \text{LLF}}{A}$ вҖ” *write LLF, examiners are strict.*
- вҳҗ **Daylight Factor:** $\text{DF (\%)} = \dfrac{E_{\text{indoor}}}{E_{\text{outdoor}}} \times 100$ вҖ” minimum **2 %** for harvesting.
- вҳҗ **Combined Strategy Savings:** $S_{\text{total}} = 1 - (1-S_o)(1-S_d)$ вҖ” always *multiplicative*.
- вҳҗ **Standards to mention:** **ECBC 2007/2017** (LPD limits), **NBC 2016** (illuminance levels), **IES RP-29** (office lighting), **IEC 62386 (DALI-2)**.
- вҳҗ **Typical illuminance (lx):** Office 300вҖ“500 | Conference room 500вҖ“750 | Corridor 100вҖ“200 | Parking 50вҖ“100.
- вҳҗ **Sensor placement tips (frequently missed in design questions):** Avoid placing PIR across windows (false trips from sun); avoid US near HVAC diffusers; mount photosensor on ceiling *away* from direct sunlight.
- вҳҗ **Time delay (also called "hold time"):** Typically **5вҖ“20 minutes** for occupancy sensors; shorter for corridors, longer for offices.
- вҳҗ **Closed-loop vs. open-loop photosensor:** *Closed-loop* measures the work plane вҖ” best for accuracy. *Open-loop* measures daylight at the window вҖ” best for smooth dimming.
- вҳҗ **Cost justification:** Always quote **payback period** $T = C_{\text{install}} / \text{Annual вӮ№ Savings}$ for a complete answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 2:** "**Sense вҶ’ Compare вҶ’ Act вҶ’ Verify.**" Every lighting control system in a BAS follows this four-step closed-loop logic. State it in your introduction, your diagram, and your conclusion вҖ” and you will earn the examiner's confidence marks.

> **End of Notes вҖ” Module 2 | Application to Lighting Control: Light & Occupancy Sensors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Automation Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Building Automation Software # MODULE 2 вҖ” BUILDING AUTOMATION SOFTWARE ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Building Automation Software (BAS Software)** is the integrated suite of application programs, supervisory control logic, graphical user interfaces (GUI), communication drivers, and database engines that reside on a workstation/server and/or embedded controller hardware to monitor, control, optimize, and report the operation of Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing (MEP), fire-life-safety, and security subsystems within a building. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, BAS software is the "intelligence layer" that sits above field-level sensors/actuators and below the enterprise/facility management layer. It executes three foundational tasks: 1. **Data Acquisition** вҖ” Periodically poll/interpret field-point values. 2. **Control Logic Execution** вҖ” Run proportional-integral-derivative (PID), scheduling, sequencing, and interlocks. 3. **Visualization & Decision Support** вҖ” Render synoptic graphics, alarms, trends, and reports to the operator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Building Automation Software* encompasses supervisory software architecture, protocol stacks (BACnet, LonWorks, KNX, Modbus, DALI), object models, graphical user interfaces, database/trending modules, alarm management, and integration with IoT/Cloud platforms. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” "The Building's Brain" Imagine a modern luxury car: - **The engine, brakes, AC compressor, headlights** = field devices (sensors & actuators). - **The Engine Control Unit (ECU)** = Direct Digital Controller (DDC) at the automation level. - **The driver's touchscreen, steering inputs, dashboard cluster** = the BAS supervisory software running on a workstation. Without the touchscreen software (BAS GUI), the driver cannot see coolant temperature, fuel level, or tyre pressure вҖ” even though the ECUs are collecting that data. Likewise, BAS software is the *window and steering wheel* through which a Facility Manager operates the building. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for KTU Viva вҖ” "M-C-S-A-D-R":** > **M**onitoring вҖў **C**ontrol вҖў **S**cheduling вҖў **A**larms вҖў **D**ata Logging вҖў **R**eports ### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU-Relevant) The following metrics are universally adopted when evaluating BAS software (referenced in **ISO 16484**, **BACnet MS-TP**, and **ANSI/ASHRAE 135**): - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF): > 50,000 hours** for supervisory servers. - **Response Time (GUI refresh): вүӨ 2 seconds** for a 1,000-point system. - **Operator Workstation Availability: 99.9 %** (Three-Nines uptime). - **Maximum Number of Points Managed: 10,000вҖ“100,000+** (enterprise tier). - **Historical Trend Storage: вүҘ 1 year** at 1-minute resolution (best-practice). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Software Architecture (The Three-Tier Model) KTU examiners almost always ask for a labelled architecture diagram. Memorise the **three functional tiers**: ### Tier 1 вҖ” Management Level (Enterprise/Supervisory) - Runs on a **Server-class workstation** (often Windows Server or Linux). - Hosts **SCADA/HMI software**, historical database (SQL), web server, report engine. - Users: Facility Managers, Energy Managers, Tenants (read-only). ### Tier 2 вҖ” Automation Level (Control) - **Direct Digital Controllers (DDCs)** вҖ” typically 16/32-bit microcontrollers. - Executes **PID loops, interlocks, scheduling** autonomously. - Acts as a "buffer": if the supervisor crashes, the building still operates. ### Tier 3 вҖ” Field Level (Sensing & Actuation) - **Sensors** (temperature, COвӮӮ, occupancy, lux) and **Actuators** (valves, dampers, VFDs). - Communicates via analog (0вҖ“10 V, 4вҖ“20 mA) or digital protocols. ## 2.2 Core Software Modules (Function-by-Function Breakdown) | Module | Function | Why It Matters (KTU Rationale) | |--------|----------|--------------------------------| | **HMI / Graphics Engine** | Renders floor plans, equipment schematics | Operator situational awareness | | **Point Database** | Stores tag, address, engineering units, range | Single source of truth | | **Scheduling Engine** | Time-of-day, calendar, holiday overrides | Energy savings (10вҖ“30 %) | | **Alarm Management** | Prioritises, routes, acknowledges, escalates | Safety & compliance | | **Trending / Historian** | Time-series storage, retrieval, charting | Root-cause analysis, LEED/M&V | | **Energy Management** | Load shedding, demand limiting, peak shaving | Tariff optimisation | | **Access Control Integration** | Interfaces card-readers to BMS workflows | Security convergence | | **Report Generator** | Daily/monthly MIS, KPI dashboards | Management reporting | ## 2.3 Communication Protocol Stack (BACnet as Reference) BACnet is the **most exam-relevant** protocol under KTU 2024. It is an ASHRAE/ANSI/ISO standard (ASHRAE Std 135, ISO 16484-5). > [!IMPORTANT] > **BACnet Four-Layer Collapsed Stack** (matches the simplified OSI model): > 1. **Application Layer** вҖ” BACnet objects, services, APDUs. > 2. **Network Layer** вҖ” Addressing & routing (BACnet/IP uses UDP port **47808**, official port **47808**; BACnet MS-TP uses RS-485). > 3. **Data Link Layer** вҖ” Ethernet, MS-TP (Master-Slave/Token-Passing), ARCNET. > 4. **Physical Layer** вҖ” Twisted pair, fibre, wireless. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Constants > [!WARNING] > Use `\vert` instead of `\vert x \vert` inside markdown tables to avoid breaking syntax. Below, all values are plain numeric. | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Value / Unit | |---|-------------------|-------------|----------------------| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem (digital control) | $f_s$ = sample rate, $f_m$ = max signal frequency | | 2 | $D_{bas} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot r_i \cdot b_i$ | Aggregate BAS data rate (bytes/s) | $p$ = points, $r$ = update rate (Hz), $b$ = bits/point | | 3 | $\tau_{loop} = \tau_s + \tau_c + \tau_n$ | Total control loop latency | Sample + compute + network | | 4 | $P_{tot} = A_{floor} \cdot d_{pt}$ | Total I/O points estimate | $d_{pt} \approx 0.5$вҖ“$1.5$ points/mВІ (office) | | 5 | $\eta_{saved} = \dfrac{E_{base} - E_{bas}}{E_{base}} \times 100\%$ | Energy saving fraction from BAS | Typical: **20вҖ“30 %** | | 6 | $R_{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Reliability metric | Hours | | 7 | $N_{nodes\_max} = 2^{(L-1)}$ | MS-TP max master nodes | $L = 8$ вҶ’ **127 nodes** | | 8 | $A_{UPS} = P_{server} \cdot t_{backup}$ | UPS battery sizing (VAh) | Typical $t_{backup} \geq 30$ min | | 9 | $C_{demand} = \dfrac{\sum P_{load}}{PF \cdot \eta_{inv}}$ | Demand limiting threshold | kW, kVA | | 10 | $Q_{db} = N_{pt} \cdot f_s \cdot 31536000$ | Annual trend data volume (bytes) | bytes/year | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Constants to Memorise for KTU:** > - BACnet/IP UDP port = **47808** > - MS-TP maximum nodes = **127** masters (in practice вүӨ 64 due to token rotation latency) > - LonWorks Neuron Chip ID = **48-bit** unique identifier > - Modbus RTU frame max = **256 bytes** > - KNX twisted-pair baud = **9.6 kbps** - **Real-world utility:** These formulas are used in BAS design by system integrators (Honeywell, Siemens, Schneider, Johnson Controls) to size servers, network switches, and UPS systems before tendering. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic transition below is fully expanded. No shortcuts, no "it can be shown thatвҖҰ". ## 3.1 Derivation вҖ” Estimating BAS Data Bandwidth When designing a supervisory network, the system engineer must verify that the LAN bandwidth is not saturated. Let us derive the aggregate throughput required by the supervisory station. ### Given (KTU-Numerical Example) - A commercial office has $N = 500$ BACnet objects. - Of these, $60\%$ are analogue inputs sampled at $r = 1$ Hz, and the rest are binary values reported on change. - Each BACnet object value is encoded into a **$b = 4$ bytes** payload (typical Real-type). ### Step 1 вҖ” Partition the points Analogue points: $p_a = 0.60 \times 500 = 300$ Binary points: $p_b = 0.40 \times 500 = 200$ ### Step 2 вҖ” Compute the analogue contribution Since binary COV (Change-of-Value) reporting is sporadic, the deterministic worst case is from analogue polling: $$ D_a = p_a \cdot r \cdot b = 300 \times 1 \, \text{Hz} \times 4 \, \text{bytes} $$ $$ D_a = 1200 \, \text{bytes/s} $$ ### Step 3 вҖ” Compute the binary contribution Assume a conservative change rate $r_b = 0.1$ Hz (one event every 10 s on average): $$ D_b = p_b \cdot r_b \cdot b = 200 \times 0.1 \times 4 = 80 \, \text{bytes/s} $$ ### Step 4 вҖ” Add protocol overhead BACnet/IP adds a UDP header (**8 bytes**), IP header (**20 bytes**), Ethernet header + CRC (**26 bytes**), i.e., **54 bytes** per packet. Per second, packet rate $= D_a/(\text{max payload})$ ~ 2 packets/s for analogue alone. Total overhead: $$ D_{oh} = 54 \times 2 = 108 \, \text{bytes/s} $$ ### Step 5 вҖ” Aggregate $$ \begin{aligned} D_{bas} &= D_a + D_b + D_{oh} \\ &= 1200 + 80 + 108 \\ &= 1388 \, \text{bytes/s} \\ &\approx 11.1 \, \text{kbps} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 вҖ” Utilisation against 100 Mbps Ethernet $$ \eta = \frac{11.1 \times 10^{3}}{100 \times 10^{6}} \times 100\% \approx 0.011\% $$ This confirms that **Ethernet** is more than adequate; the real bottleneck becomes **poll storming** at the controller, not LAN bandwidth. --- ## 3.2 Derivation вҖ” Number of I/O Points for an Office Floor ### Given Floor area $A_{floor} = 1000 \,\text{m}^{2}$. Office density $d_{pt} = 1.0 \,\text{points/m}^{2}$. ### Step 1 вҖ” Base calculation $$ P_{tot} = A_{floor} \cdot d_{pt} = 1000 \times 1.0 = 1000 \, \text{points} $$ ### Step 2 вҖ” Add 15 % spare capacity (best practice for future expansion) $$ P_{design} = P_{tot} \times 1.15 = 1150 \, \text{points} $$ ### Step 3 вҖ” Controller allocation (32-point DDC rule of thumb) $$ N_{DDC} = \left\lceil \frac{P_{design}}{32} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1150}{32} \right\rceil = \lceil 35.94 \rceil = 36 \, \text{DDCs} $$ This justifies the standard BMS design rule of **~30 DDCs per floor** for a mid-sized office. --- ## 3.3 Derivation вҖ” Sampling Rate Compliance (Nyquist) A room temperature sensor must track the diurnal variation. Assume $f_m = 0.0001 \,\text{Hz}$ (one cycle per ~3 hours). $$ \begin{aligned} f_s &\geq 2 f_m \\ f_s &\geq 2 \times 0.0001 \\ f_s &\geq 0.0002 \,\text{Hz} \end{aligned} $$ But the **practical engineering minimum** is far higher (вүҘ 0.1 Hz = sample every 10 s) because of **aliasing on abrupt disturbances** (door opening, sun load transient). This illustrates why *theoretical minimum вү  engineering optimum* вҖ” a frequent KTU conceptual question. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Software-Level Trade-offs | Software Architecture | Scalability | Fault Tolerance | Cost (вӮ№) | Cyber-Security | Best Suited For | |-----------------------|-------------|-----------------|----------|----------------|-----------------| | Proprietary (vendor-locked) | Medium | LowвҖ“Medium | High | Vendor-controlled | Single-vendor campuses | | Open Protocol (BACnet) | High | High | Medium | Open audit | Multi-vendor, large campuses | | Cloud-native / SaaS | Very High | Very High | OPEX-based | Strong (TLS 1.3) | Distributed portfolios | | On-Premise Hybrid | High | MediumвҖ“High | CapEx+OpEx | Medium | Critical infrastructure (airports, hospitals) | | Edge-Computing (IoT) | High | High | Low per node | Device-dependent | Smart buildings, retrofits | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 BAS Software Architecture (Three-Tier Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TL1["TIER 1 MANAGEMENT LEVEL"] nodeA["Supervisory Server"] nodeB["Operator Workstation GUI"] nodeC["Historian SQL Database"] nodeD["Web Server Mobile App"] nodeA <--> nodeB nodeA <--> nodeC nodeA <--> nodeD end subgraph TL2["TIER 2 AUTOMATION LEVEL"] nodeE["DDC Air Handling Unit"] nodeF["DDC Chiller Plant"] nodeG["DDC Lighting Controller"] nodeH["DDC Access Control"] end subgraph TL3["TIER 3 FIELD LEVEL"] nodeI["Temp Humidity CO2 Sensors"] nodeJ["Valves Dampers VFD Actuators"] nodeK["PIR Occupancy Lux Sensors"] nodeL["Card Readers Door Contacts"] end nodeA <--> nodeE nodeA <--> nodeF nodeA <--> nodeG nodeA <--> nodeH nodeE --> nodeI nodeE --> nodeJ nodeF --> nodeI nodeF --> nodeJ nodeG --> nodeK nodeH --> nodeL ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Tier 1 is purely software (the BAS software itself). Tier 2 are controllers running embedded firmware (firmware + onboard logic). Tier 3 are physical field devices. Communication between tiers follows the BACnet/LonWorks/KNX protocol stack shown next. --- ## 4.2 BACnet Protocol Stack (Collapsed 4-Layer OSI Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart TB L1["APPLICATION LAYER
BACnet Objects 23 types
BACnet Services 35 types
COV Reporting Read Write"] L2["NETWORK LAYER
BACnet IP MS TP ARCNET
Router Functions
Foreign Device Registration"] L3["DATA LINK LAYER
Ethernet IEEE 802.3
MS TP RS 485
ARCNET 156 kbps 2.5 Mbps"] L4["PHYSICAL LAYER
Twisted Pair Cat 5e
Fibre Optic
Wireless Zigbee LoRa"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 ``` --- ## 4.3 Alarm-Handling Workflow (State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Normal Normal --> Raised : threshold exceeded Raised --> Acknowledged : operator ACK Raised --> ReturnToNormal : condition clears Acknowledged --> ReturnToNormal : condition clears Acknowledged --> Escalated : timeout no ACK 60 s Escalated --> ReturnToNormal : condition clears ReturnToNormal --> Normal : operator reset ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall:** The alarm state machine has 5 states вҖ” *Normal, Raised, Acknowledged, Escalated, Return-to-Normal*. Examiners often ask "draw the alarm lifecycle" вҖ” this is your answer. --- ## 4.4 Object-Oriented View of a BACnet Object (Example: Analog Input) ```mermaid classDiagram class AnalogInput { +string Object_Identifier +string Object_Name +string Object_Type ANALOG_INPUT +float Present_Value 23.5 degC +string Units degrees_Celsius +float Min_Pres_Value 0.0 +float Max_Pres_Value 50.0 +string Description Room Temp Zone 1 +bool Out_Of_Service false +int Reliability NO_FAULT_DETECTED +notifyCOV() +readProperty() +writeProperty() } ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]** **Define Building Automation Software. List any four of its primary functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Building Automation Software (BAS) is the integrated set of application programs that reside on a workstation or server to monitor, control, and optimise MEP subsystems through DDCs and field devices. *(1 mark)* > > Primary functions: *(ВҪ mark each, any four)* > 1. **Monitoring** вҖ“ real-time display of sensor values and equipment status. > 2. **Control** вҖ“ executing PID loops, sequences, and interlocks. > 3. **Scheduling** вҖ“ time-of-day and calendar-based setpoint overrides. > 4. **Alarm Management** вҖ“ detect, prioritise, acknowledge, and escalate abnormal conditions. > 5. **Trending / Historical Logging** вҖ“ time-series storage for energy and fault analytics. > 6. **Reporting** вҖ“ daily/monthly MIS and regulatory compliance reports. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Jun 2024]** **State the BACnet four-layer collapsed stack. Mention the standard UDP port for BACnet/IP.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. Application Layer вҖ” BACnet objects & services *(ВҪ mark)* 2. Network Layer вҖ” routing & addressing *(ВҪ mark)* 3. Data Link Layer вҖ” Ethernet, MS-TP, ARCNET *(ВҪ mark)* 4. Physical Layer вҖ” twisted pair, fibre, wireless *(ВҪ mark)* 5. Standard UDP port for BACnet/IP = **47808** *(1 mark)* --- ## Part B вҖ” Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B of ESE Module carries 14 marks with **internal choice**. Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” *[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]*** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-tier software architecture** of a Building Automation System. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The BAS software is organised into three hierarchical tiers вҖ” Management, Automation, and Field вҖ” each performing distinct functions and communicating via standard protocols such as BACnet, LonWorks, or Modbus. **Tier 1 вҖ” Management (Supervisory) Level (2 marks):** - Runs on a server-class workstation. - Hosts the **HMI/SCADA software**, SQL historian, web server, report engine. - Operators interact via graphical floor plans, equipment schematics, and dashboards. - Functions: alarm acknowledgement, schedule editing, trend viewing, energy analytics. **Tier 2 вҖ” Automation (Control) Level (2 marks):** - Consists of **Direct Digital Controllers (DDCs)** вҖ” 16/32-bit embedded controllers. - Executes **PID loops, interlocks, sequencing, scheduling** autonomously. - Provides fault-tolerant operation: building remains functional even if the supervisor is offline. - Examples: Honeywell Excel 5000, Siemens Desigo PX, Schneider MNB. **Tier 3 вҖ” Field Level (1 mark):** - Sensors (temperature, COвӮӮ, occupancy, lux) and actuators (valves, dampers, VFDs). - Communication via analog 0вҖ“10 V / 4вҖ“20 mA or digital fieldbus. **Diagram (1 mark):** Draw the three-tier block diagram showing all three levels, their functions, and the bidirectional communication arrows labelled with the protocol. --- **(b)** Explain the **BACnet object model** with a suitable example. List any **five standard BACnet object types**. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Object-Oriented Concept (2 marks):** A BACnet object is a standard data structure representing a physical or virtual point. Each object is identified by a unique **Object_Identifier** = (Object_Type, Instance_Number) and has a set of properties accessible via ReadProperty and WriteProperty services. **Example вҖ” Analog Input Object (2 marks):** ``` Object_Identifier : (ANALOG_INPUT, 1) Object_Name : "Zone1_Temp" Present_Value : 23.5 В°C Units : degrees-Celsius Min/Max_Pres_Value: 0 / 50 Description : "Room temperature, Floor 1, Zone A" Reliability : NO_FAULT_DETECTED Out_Of_Service : FALSE ``` This object is periodically read by the supervisor (polling) and also supports **COV (Change-of-Value)** reporting. **Five Standard Object Types (3 marks вҖ” ВҪ each + ВҪ for naming five correctly):** 1. **Analog Input (AI)** вҖ“ sensor readings 2. **Analog Output (AO)** вҖ“ control outputs (valve position) 3. **Analog Value (AV)** вҖ“ software variables (setpoints) 4. **Binary Input (BI)** вҖ“ digital contacts (door status) 5. **Binary Output (BO)** вҖ“ on/off commands (fan start) 6. **Binary Value (BV)** вҖ“ software flags (occupancy override) 7. **Device** вҖ“ the controller itself 8. **Schedule** вҖ“ time-of-day programming 9. **Trend Log** вҖ“ historical storage reference --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” *[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 (Alternate Set)]*** **(a)** Discuss the **functions and features** of Building Automation Software in detail. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **1. Monitoring (1 mark):** Real-time display of all field-point values via colour-coded graphics, with engineering units and alarm-colour overlays. **2. Control (1 mark):** Implementation of PID, on/off, floating, and sequencing logic; setpoint adjustments with anti-windup and bumpless transfer. **3. Scheduling (1 mark):** Time-of-day, weekly, annual, and exception-day schedules; occupancy-based overrides; holiday handling. **4. Alarm Management (1 mark):** Detection вҶ’ prioritisation (Critical/High/Medium/Low) вҶ’ routing (SMS/email/siren) вҶ’ acknowledgement вҶ’ escalation вҶ’ audit logging. **5. Energy Management (1 mark):** Load shedding, demand limiting, peak shaving, chiller optimisation, free-cooling, night-purge, and LEED/M&V reporting. **6. Trending & Historical Logging (1 mark):** Time-series storage at user-defined intervals (1 s to 1 h); SQL backend for retrieval and charting. **7. Integration & Reporting (1 mark):** BACnet/Modbus/LonWorks gateways; tenant billing interfaces; energy dashboards; automated PDF/email reports. --- **(b)** Describe **IoT and Cloud integration with BAS software**. List its advantages and challenges. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** Modern BAS software is increasingly extended with **IoT gateways** (e.g., Niagara 4, AWS IoT Core, Azure Digital Twins) that push field data to the cloud for advanced analytics, while allowing remote operator access via web/mobile dashboards. **Architecture (1 mark):** `Field вҶ’ DDC вҶ’ Edge Gateway (BAS software) вҶ’ MQTT/HTTPS вҶ’ Cloud Platform вҶ’ Web/Mobile UI`. **Advantages (2 marks вҖ” any four):** - Remote monitoring from any location. - Big-data analytics, machine-learning fault prediction. - Multi-site portfolio management for FM companies. - Scalable OPEX-based cost model (SaaS). - Automatic firmware/cybersecurity patching. **Challenges (2 marks вҖ” any four):** - **Cyber-security** вҖ” exposed to internet; needs TLS 1.3, VPNs, zero-trust. - **Latency** вҖ” cloud round-trip may be 100вҖ“500 ms; unsuitable for fast control loops. - **Internet dependency** вҖ” downtime of ISP disables access. - **Data privacy / GDPR compliance** for tenant data. - **Interoperability** вҖ” vendor-locked clouds vs open standards. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. вқҢ Drawing the three-tier diagram **without labelling the protocols** between tiers вҶ’ -2 marks. > 2. вқҢ Confusing BACnet **objects** (e.g., Analog Input) with **services** (e.g., ReadProperty) вҶ’ -1 mark. > 3. вқҢ Writing BACnet port as "47808" (some write "48080" or "8080") вҶ’ -1 mark. > 4. вқҢ Forgetting to mention **COV (Change-of-Value) reporting** in alarm/trending answers вҶ’ -1 mark. > 5. вқҢ In IoT questions, omitting the **MQTT/HTTPS** transport layer вҶ’ -1 mark. > 6. вқҢ Giving generic "advantages of cloud" instead of **BAS-specific** advantages вҶ’ -1 mark. --- ## рҹ§  Topic Recap вҖ” Rapid Revision Checklist - вң… BAS software is the **intelligence + visualisation** layer above DDCs and field devices. - вң… Three-tier architecture: **Management вҶ’ Automation вҶ’ Field**. - вң… Four core protocol models in BAS: **BACnet (ASHRAE/ISO), LonWorks (ANSI/CEA-709), KNX (ISO/IEC 14543), Modbus (Modicon)**. - вң… BACnet/IP uses **UDP port 47808**; MS-TP max **127 master nodes**. - вң… Five key BAS software functions: **Monitoring, Control, Scheduling, Alarm Management, Trending/Reporting** (plus Energy & Access). - вң… **Alarm lifecycle:** Normal вҶ’ Raised вҶ’ Acknowledged вҶ’ Escalated вҶ’ Return-to-Normal. - вң… BACnet objects are **23 standard types**; key examples: Analog Input/Output/Value, Binary Input/Output/Value, Device, Schedule, Trend Log. - вң… **COV (Change-of-Value)** reporting reduces LAN bandwidth vs polling. - вң… **Nyquist sampling** gives the theoretical floor; engineering practice is 5вҖ“10Г— higher. - вң… BAS typically delivers **20вҖ“30 % energy savings** in commercial buildings. - вң… Modern BAS integrates with **IoT / Cloud / Digital Twins** for advanced analytics. - вң… **Cyber-security** (TLS 1.3, segmentation, zero-trust) is now an exam-relevant concern. - вң… Design rule of thumb: **~1 point/mВІ** for offices; **~30 DDCs per floor**; **вүҘ 30 min UPS** for the supervisory server.

topics.title: Communication Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Communication Protocol** in Building Automation (BA) is a standardized set of rules, data formats, and procedural conventions that govern the exchange of information between intelligent field devices, controllers, workstations, and supervisory systems across heterogeneous physical media within a Building Automation and Control Network (BACnet).

In formal KTU syllabus terms:
> *"A Communication Protocol defines the **syntax**, **semantics**, **synchronization**, and **error recovery** procedures necessary for interoperable data exchange between digitally controlled building subsystems вҖ” HVAC, lighting, fire alarm, access control, and energy management."*

The four layered requirements that every BA protocol must satisfy are:
1. **Physical Layer** вҖ” voltage levels, cabling, connectors.
2. **Data Link Layer** вҖ” framing, MAC addressing, collision handling.
3. **Network/Transport Layer** вҖ” packet routing, segmentation, reliability.
4. **Application Layer** вҖ” meaningful object/data semantics (e.g., temperature, damper position).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight: Communication protocols are the **backbone of interoperability**. Without them, a chiller from Vendor A cannot "talk" to a VAV box from Vendor B. Hence the KTU examiner expects students to know both **open protocols** (BACnet, LonWorks, KNX, Modbus) and **proprietary ones**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” "The United Nations of Buildings"

Imagine a giant skyscraper where every subsystem speaks a different native language:
- The **HVAC** system speaks only German.
- The **Lighting** system speaks only Mandarin.
- The **Fire Alarm** panel speaks only French.
- The **Elevator** controller speaks only Japanese.

Without translators, none of them can coordinate вҖ” so the building collapses into chaos during an emergency. A **Communication Protocol** is the **universal translator** (and the rulebook for how translations happen) that allows every subsystem to:
- Greet each other (handshake),
- Pass messages (data frames),
- Confirm receipt (acknowledgment),
- And recover gracefully if a message is lost (error recovery).

Just like the United Nations uses **six official languages** but **English & French** as working languages, modern buildings use **BACnet** as the *lingua franca* because it is the only protocol ratified as both **ANSI/ASHRAE 135** and **ISO 16484-5** вҖ” truly global.

> [!NOTE]
> Real-World Map of BA Protocols:
> - **HVAC domain** вҶ’ BACnet, LonWorks
> - **Lighting domain** вҶ’ DALI, KNX
> - **Industrial field level** вҶ’ Modbus, Profibus
> - **Wireless sensors** вҶ’ Zigbee, EnOcean, BLE
> - **IoT / Cloud** вҶ’ MQTT, OPC UA, CoAP, BACnet/IP

---

## 1.3 Why Communication Protocols Matter вҖ” KTU Design Perspective

| Without Standard Protocol | With Standard Protocol |
|---|---|
| Vendor lock-in (one supplier forever) | Multi-vendor interoperability |
| High integration cost | Plug-and-play integration |
| Closed, proprietary data | Open, auditable data |
| Limited scalability | Modular, scalable architecture |
| Difficult remote monitoring | Cloud-ready telemetry (via BACnet/IP, MQTT) |

**Standard Metrics Every KTU Student Must Memorize (in bold):**
- **BACnet MS/TP maximum addressable masters per segment = 127**
- **BACnet default UDP port = 47808 (0xBAC0)**
- **DALI max devices per line = 64**
- **KNX max devices per line = 256 (TP1)**
- **LonWorks Neuron chip ID = 48-bit unique identifier**
- **Modbus max slave devices on RS-485 = 247**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSIвҖ“BACnet Model Mapping

Modern BA protocols rarely implement the full **7-layer OSI stack**. They use a **collapsed 4-layer architecture** that maps efficiently to building control traffic (which is short, deterministic, and bursty). The KTU-pinned mapping is shown below.

| OSI Layer | Function | BACnet Equivalent | LonWorks Equivalent | Modbus Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 вҖ” Application | Meaningful data | BACnet Application Layer | Application Layer (Neuron C) | Modbus Application (Function codes) |
| 4 вҖ” Transport | Reliability, segmentation | BACnet Application Layer | Transport Layer (LonTalk) | TCP (Modbus TCP) |
| 3 вҖ” Network | Routing | BACnet Network Layer | Network Layer (addressing) | IP |
| 2 вҖ” Data Link | Framing, MAC | MS/TP, ARCNET, Ethernet | LonTalk MAC | RS-485 / TCP |
| 1 вҖ” Physical | Electrical/optical | EIA-485, Ethernet | TP, PL, RF, Fiber | RS-485, Ethernet |

---

## 2.2 BACnet вҖ” The Cornerstone Protocol

### 2.2.1 Origins and Standardization
- Developed by **ASHRAE** Standing Standard Project Committee **SSPC 135** (founded 1987).
- First released in **1995**, continuously revised (current: **ANSI/ASHRAE 135-2020**).
- Also adopted as **ISO 16484-5** and **EN 16484-5**.

### 2.2.2 BACnet Object-Oriented Philosophy
Instead of sending raw binary values, BACnet defines **standardized Objects** that represent every physical and logical point in a building.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BACnet Objects (KTU must-know list):**
> - **Analog Input (AI)** вҖ” sensor readings (temperature, humidity)
> - **Analog Output (AO)** вҖ” actuator setpoints (damper position, valve %)
> - **Analog Value (AV)** вҖ” software variables (calculated loads)
> - **Binary Input (BI)** вҖ” digital status (fan ON/OFF, filter dirty)
> - **Binary Output (BO)** вҖ” digital commands (start/stop)
> - **Binary Value (BV)** вҖ” software flags (occupied/unoccupied)
> - **Multi-State Input/Output/Value** вҖ” multi-step devices (chiller stages)
> - **Schedule, Calendar, Command, Notification Class, Device, Loop, File**

Every object exposes a fixed set of **Properties** (e.g., `present_value`, `description`, `units`, `reliability`). This is the **"Why"** behind BACnet's interoperability вҖ” vendors agree on object semantics, not just bits.

### 2.2.3 BACnet Services (The "How")
Services are the **function calls** a BACnet device can issue. Major families:
- **ReadProperty / ReadPropertyMultiple** вҖ” retrieve object values.
- **WriteProperty / WritePropertyMultiple** вҖ” modify values.
- **Who-Is / I-Am** вҖ” device discovery on the network.
- **I-Have / Who-Has** вҖ” locate objects.
- **ConfirmedCOVNotification** вҖ” push-based change-of-value reporting.
- **AtomicReadFile / AtomicWriteFile** вҖ” trend log access.
- **AddListElement / RemoveListElement** вҖ” dynamic list management.

### 2.2.4 BACnet LAN Options (KTU High-Yield)

| LAN Option | Standard | Medium | Speed | Topology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BACnet MS/TP** | EIA-485 | Twisted Pair | 9.6 вҖ“ 76.8 kbps (some 115.2 kbps) | Daisy-chain, вүӨ 32 devices per segment (127 with repeaters) |
| **BACnet Ethernet** | IEEE 802.3 | 10/100 Mbps Ethernet | Bus/Star |
| **BACnet/IP** | Annex J | UDP/IP | 100+ Mbps | Star/Tree |
| **BACnet ARCNET** | ANSI 878.1 | Coax/TP | 2.5 вҖ“ 10 Mbps | Bus/Star/Tree |
| **BACnet PTP** | Annex A | RS-232 | < 115.2 kbps | Point-to-Point |

**MS/TP Token-Passing Logic (Why & How):**
> MS/TP = **MasterвҖ“Slave / Token-Passing** over EIA-485. Only master nodes hold the token. The token circulates sequentially by address. A master may transmit when it possesses the token; otherwise it must remain silent and wait. Slaves speak only when polled by a master. This deterministic discipline makes MS/TP highly predictable for HVAC control loops вҖ” a critical property KTU emphasizes.

### 2.2.5 BACnet Addressing
A BACnet address is a tuple:
$$
\text{BACnet Address} = (\text{Network Number},\ \text{MAC Address},\ \text{Device Instance})
$$

- **Device Instance**: 22-bit unique identifier (max $\mathbf{2^{22} - 1 = 4{,}194{,}303}$ devices).
- **Network Number**: 16-bit, max 65,535 networks.

---

## 2.3 LonWorks вҖ” The Neuron-Chip Era Protocol

| Parameter | LonWorks Detail |
|---|---|
| Standard | ANSI/CEA-709.1 and ANSI/CEA-709.3 |
| Protocol Name | LonTalk |
| Hardware | Neuron Chip (Toshiba/Motorola/Cypress) |
| Processor | 3 CPUs (MAC, Network, Application) |
| Identifier | 48-bit Neuron ID (globally unique) |
| Media | TP (78 kbps / 1.25 Mbps), PL (Power Line), RF, Fiber, Coax |

**LonWorks Architecture (Why Neuron Chip Matters):**
The Neuron chip has **3 internal processors** вҖ” one for the **Media Access Control (MAC) layer**, one for the **network protocol**, and one for the **application**. This offloads the host microcontroller entirely, making every node intelligent.

**LonTalk Protocol Layers:**
1. Physical
2. MAC (predictive CSMA вҖ” avoids collisions, ideal for control)
3. Link Layer (framing, CRC, acknowledgment)
4. Network Layer (addressing, routing)
5. Transport Layer (reliable & unreliable modes)
6. Session Layer (request/response, authentication)
7. Presentation & Application (Network Variables, SNVTs)

> [!NOTE]
> **SNVT = Standard Network Variable Type** вҖ” LonWorks' equivalent of BACnet's standardized objects. SNVTs define units, ranges, and scaling so vendors agree on data semantics.

---

## 2.4 KNX вҖ” European Installation Bus Evolution

| Parameter | KNX Detail |
|---|---|
| Origin | Converged from EIB, BatiBUS, EHS (1999) |
| Standard | ISO/IEC 14543-3, EN 50090, GB/T 20965 |
| Media | **TP1** (twisted pair), **PL110** (power line), **RF** (radio), **IP** (Ethernet) |
| Speed (TP1) | 9.6 kbps |
| Addressing | Individual (65,536), Group (65,536), Physical |
| Configuration | S-mode (system), E-mode (easy), A-mode (automatic) |

**KNX TP1 Signaling:** A 9.6 kbps baseband signal with DC component, allowing bus-powered devices and bus topology. KNX is dominant in European residential and commercial installations.

---

## 2.5 Modbus вҖ” The Industrial Classic

- Created by **Modicon (Schneider Electric)** in 1979 вҖ” the oldest still-active BA-adjacent protocol.
- Three variants: **Modbus RTU**, **Modbus ASCII** (serial), **Modbus TCP** (Ethernet).
- Simple **register-based** model: Holding, Input, Coils, Discrete Inputs.

---

## 2.6 DALI вҖ” Digital Addressable Lighting Interface

- **IEC 62386** standard.
- 2-wire bus, **64 ballasts per DALI line**, **16 groups**, **16 scenes**.
- Bidirectional: ballast reports lamp failure, dimming level, energy usage.
- Wireless extension: **DALI-2 / D4i / DALI+ over BLE/Casambi**.

---

| Zigbee | IEEE 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | 10вҖ“100 m | 250 kbps |
| EnOcean | ISO/IEC 14543-3-10 | 315/868/902 MHz | 30 m indoor | 125 kbps |
