> *Differentiate between sensible heat and latent heat in the context of a building's air-conditioning load.*

**Model Answer:**
| **Sensible Heat** | **Latent Heat** |
|-------------------|-----------------|
| Heat that changes *temperature* only | Heat that changes *phase* (moisture content) only |
| Detected by dry-bulb thermometer | Detected by wet-bulb / humidity sensor |
| Formula: $Q_s = m c_p \Delta T$ | Formula: $Q_l = m h_{fg}$ |
| Effect: changes room air temperature | Effect: changes room relative humidity |
| BAS countermeasure: cooling coil + reheat | BAS countermeasure: dehumidification cycle |
| **(1.5 marks)** | **(1.5 marks)** |

---

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** *With neat sketches, explain the three modes of heat transfer that affect a conditioned zone in a building. State the governing law for each mode.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A $10\,\text{m} \times 4\,\text{m}$ brick wall is $0.25\,\text{m}$ thick. Inside temperature is $22\,^\circ\text{C}$, outside is $38\,^\circ\text{C}$. Thermal conductivity of brick $k = 0.7\,\text{W/mK}$. Inner and outer surface film coefficients are $h_i = 8\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $h_o = 22\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ respectively. Calculate:* **(7 marks)**
> 1. *Total thermal resistance of the wall.*
> 2. *Heat flow rate through the wall per hour.*
> 3. *Whether adding $0.05\,\text{m}$ polystyrene insulation ($k_{ins} = 0.04\,\text{W/mK}$) on the outer face would reduce the heat ingress. Justify numerically.*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) вҖ” Three Modes of Heat Transfer (7 marks):**

| **Mode** | **Governing Law** | **Formula** | **Example in Building** |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Conduction** | Fourier's Law | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat flow through brick wall |
| **Convection** | Newton's Law of Cooling | $q = h (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Heat from coil to supply air |
| **Radiation** | StefanвҖ“Boltzmann Law | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Solar gain through glass, occupant IR to wall |

> **Sketch description (for examiner):**
> - Conduction: arrow passing straight through wall thickness with label $k$.
> - Convection: curved arrows showing air movement over a hot/cold surface, with $h$ coefficient.
> - Radiation: wavy arrows from a hot body to a colder one across an air gap, with $T^4$ dependence. **(7 marks distributed: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for examples)**

**Part (b) вҖ” Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $A = 10 \times 4 = 40\,\text{m}^2$
- $L = 0.25\,\text{m}$, $k = 0.7\,\text{W/mK}$
- $T_i = 22\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_o = 38\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T = 16\,\text{K}$
- $h_i = 8$, $h_o = 22\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$

**Step 1 вҖ” Individual resistances:**

$$R_i = \frac{1}{h_i A} = \frac{1}{8 \times 40} = 3.125 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{brick}} = \frac{L}{kA} = \frac{0.25}{0.7 \times 40} = 8.929 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_o A} = \frac{1}{22 \times 40} = 1.136 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Total resistance (without insulation):**

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_i + R_{\text{brick}} + R_o$$

$$R_{\text{total}} = (3.125 + 8.929 + 1.136) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{total}} = 13.19 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K/W} = 0.01319\,\text{K/W}}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Heat flow rate:**

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{16}{0.01319}$$

$$\dot{Q} = 1213.04\,\text{W}$$

**Heat per hour:**

$$Q = 1213.04 \times 3600$$

$$\boxed{Q = 4.367 \times 10^6\,\text{J} = 4.367\,\text{MJ per hour}}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” With insulation added ($L_{ins} = 0.05\,\text{m}$, $k_{ins} = 0.04$):**

$$R_{\text{ins}} = \frac{0.05}{0.04 \times 40} = 0.03125\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{total,ins}} = 3.125 \times 10^{-3} + 8.929 \times 10^{-3} + 0.03125 + 1.136 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_{\text{total,ins}} = 0.04444\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” New heat flow rate:**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{ins}} = \frac{16}{0.04444} = 360.04\,\text{W}$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Comparison:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{1213.04 - 360.04}{1213.04} \times 100 = 70.32\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Yes, adding $0.05\,\text{m}$ polystyrene reduces heat ingress by **~70%**, which is a substantial improvement justifying the BAS-controlled chiller load reduction. **(7 marks: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

> ### **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Model]**
>
> **(a)** *Explain the physiological effects of heat on human occupants in a building. Discuss the Fanger PMV model and how the BAS uses it to maintain thermal comfort.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A $6\,\text{m}$ long copper pipe carries chilled water at $8\,^\circ\text{C}$ through a plant room at $32\,^\circ\text{C}$. The pipe is uninsulated and has a $50\,\text{mm}$ outer diameter. Outer surface coefficient $h = 10\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, pipe wall $k = 385\,\text{W/mK}$ and wall thickness $= 2\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the heat gain into the pipe per hour.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) вҖ” Physiological Effects & PMV Model (7 marks):**

**Physiological effects of heat (3 marks):**
- **Vasodilation:** Blood vessels near skin dilate, increasing skin temperature and sweat rate.
- **Sweating:** Latent heat loss through evaporation; up to ~1 L/hour in heavy work.
- **Increased heart rate:** Cardiovascular system works harder to dissipate metabolic heat.
- **Heat exhaustion / Heat stroke:** Above $40\,^\circ\text{C}$ core temperature, organ failure risk.
- **Cognitive decline:** Productivity drops ~4% per $^\circ\text{C}$ above $25\,^\circ\text{C}$ operative temperature.

**Fanger PMV Model (2 marks):**
P.O. Fanger's comfort equation:

$$\text{PMV} = f(M - W) - \left[ c_1 \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (T_{cl} - T_a)^4 + c_2 \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (T_{cl} - T_a) + c_3 \cdot (P_a - P_{a,\text{sat}}) \right]$$

Where $M$ = metabolic rate, $W$ = external work, $T_{cl}$ = clothing surface temp, $P_a$ = water vapor pressure.

PMV scale: $-3$ (cold) to $+3$ (hot); ideal $= 0$.

**BAS Application (2 marks):**
- BAS receives zone temperature + humidity from sensors.
- Calculates PMV in real-time (soft sensor).
- Modulates chilled water valve and fan to keep PMV within $\pm 0.5$.
- Integrates with CO$_2$ sensors for **DCV (Demand-Controlled Ventilation)** to simultaneously manage IAQ and thermal comfort.

**Part (b) вҖ” Heat Gain into Copper Pipe (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 6\,\text{m}$, $r_o = 0.025\,\text{m}$, $r_i = 0.023\,\text{m}$
- $T_{\text{air}} = 32\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{\text{water}} = 8\,^\circ\text{C}$, $\Delta T = 24\,\text{K}$
- $h_o = 10\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $k_{\text{Cu}} = 385\,\text{W/mK}$

**Step 1 вҖ” Resistances (cylindrical coordinates):**

$$R_{\text{conv}} = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_o L} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.025 \times 6}$$

$$R_{\text{conv}} = \frac{1}{9.425} = 0.1061\,\text{K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{cond}} = \frac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L} = \frac{\ln(0.025/0.023)}{2\pi \times 385 \times 6}$$

$$R_{\text{cond}} = \frac{0.0834}{14512.7} = 5.75 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Total resistance:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{\text{conv}} + R_{\text{cond}} = 0.1061 + 0.00000575 \approx 0.10611\,\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Heat gain rate:**

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{24}{0.10611} = 226.18\,\text{W}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Heat gain per hour:**

$$Q = 226.18 \times 3600 = 8.14 \times 10^5\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 814\,\text{kJ per hour} \approx 0.226\,\text{kWh per hour}}$$

> **Interpretation for BAS:** This heat gain represents cooling load that the chiller must remove. The BAS will monitor return-water temperature and increase chiller capacity accordingly. Insulating the pipe would dramatically reduce this load. **(7 marks: 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $\alpha$:** Some students write $12 \times 10^{-6}/^\circ\text{F}$. Wrong. Always $/\text{K}$ or $^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta T$ with $T$:** $T_1 - T_0$ is a *difference*, not an absolute value to feed into StefanвҖ“Boltzmann's $T^4$ without conversion to Kelvin.
> 3. **Forgetting area in Newton's Law:** $q = h \Delta T$ gives flux ($\text{W/m}^2$); for *total* heat, multiply by area: $\dot{Q} = h A \Delta T$.
> 4. **Mixing up SHGC and U-value:** SHGC applies to *solar* gain (radiation); U-value applies to conductive/convective heat transfer. Do not interchange.
> 5. **Linear vs. area vs. volume expansion:** Use $2\alpha$ for area, $3\alpha$ for volume. Examiners love testing this distinction.
> 6. **Cylindrical conduction vs. flat wall:** Pipe heat loss uses $\ln(r_o/r_i)$, not $L/kA$. Forgetting this is a 3-mark deduction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**
> - [x] **Three modes of heat transfer** вҖ” Conduction ($k$, $L$), Convection ($h$, $A$), Radiation ($\varepsilon$, $\sigma$, $T^4$)
> - [x] **Linear expansion formula** вҖ” $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$; thermal stress $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$
> - [x] **Composite wall resistance** вҖ” $R_{\text{total}} = \sum (L_i / k_i A) + 1/(h_i A) + 1/(h_o A)$; $U = 1/(R_{\text{total}} A)$
> - [x] **Heat flow equation** вҖ” $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T$ (conduction) and $\dot{Q} = \text{SHGC} \cdot I \cdot A$ (radiation)
> - [x] **Sensible vs. latent heat** вҖ” $Q_s = m c_p \Delta T$; $Q_l = m h_{fg}$
> - [x] **PMV scale** вҖ” $-3$ to $+3$; comfort range $\pm 0.5$
> - [x] **Solar constant** вҖ” $G_{sc} = 1367\,\text{W/m}^2$ (extraterrestrial)
> - [x] **StefanвҖ“Boltzmann constant** вҖ” $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4)$
> - [x] **Cylindrical resistance** вҖ” $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_o / r_i)}{2\pi k L}$
> - [x] **Fire-induced BAS actions** вҖ” HVAC shutdown, damper closure, stairwell pressurization, elevator recall
> - [x] **Heat detectors** вҖ” Fixed temperature $57\,^\circ\text{C}$ and Rate-of-Rise (RoR) types
> - [x] **SHGC vs. U-value** вҖ” Solar gain coefficient is radiation-based; U-value is conduction+convection-based

> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *"Heat flows from hot to cold вҖ” by **C**onduction through solids, by **C**onvection through fluids, and by **R**adiation through vacuum. The BAS must **C**ompensate, **C**ool, and **C**ontrol."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Humidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Humidity** is formally defined as the quantity of water vapor present in a gaseous mixture (most commonly air). In the context of building automation, humidity is one of the four primary psychrometric parameters that govern indoor environmental quality (IEQ), alongside dry-bulb temperature, wet-bulb temperature, and atmospheric pressure.

The water vapor content of air can be quantified through several distinct, mathematically rigorous metrics:

- **Absolute Humidity (AH):** The mass of water vapor ($m_v$) contained per unit volume ($V$) of the moist air mixture. Units: grams per cubic meter ($g/m^3$).
- **Specific Humidity (or Humidity Ratio, $\omega$):** The mass of water vapor per unit mass of dry air ($m_a$). Units: kg of water vapor per kg of dry air (kg/kg).
- **Relative Humidity (RH, $\phi$):** The ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor ($P_v$) in the air to the saturation vapor pressure ($P_s$) at the same dry-bulb temperature, expressed as a percentage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 3 of Building Automation, students must master the distinction between *Absolute*, *Relative*, and *Specific* humidity. The ability to convert between them is a frequently tested concept in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy вҖ” The Sponge Model**
>
> Imagine the air as a giant, invisible **sponge** and water vapor as the water it can soak up.
>
> - On a **hot day**, the sponge becomes larger and more porous вҖ” it can hold a *massive* amount of water vapor. This is why hot summer air feels so "heavy" and humid.
> - On a **cold day**, the sponge shrinks and stiffens вҖ” it can hold very little moisture. The excess water "squeezes out" as condensation (dew on grass, fog on mirrors).
>
> **Relative Humidity (RH)** is *not* the total water in the sponge; it is *how full* the sponge currently is. A sponge that is 90% full on a cold day holds very little water, but the same 90% full sponge on a hot day holds a *huge* amount. This is the source of most student confusion!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The fundamental physical constant governing humidity calculations is the **gas constant for water vapor**, $R_v = 461.5 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$. The gas constant for dry air is $R_a = 287.05 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$.

The ratio of these constants yields the dimensionless constant **0.622** (also written as $R_a / R_v = 461.5 / 287.05 \approx 1.608$, so $R_v / R_a \approx 0.622$). This magic number appears in nearly every humidity formula in psychrometrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Atmospheric Reference:** At **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP)**, defined as $T = 15^\circ C$ and $P = 101.325 \; kPa$, the saturation pressure of water vapor is $P_s = 1.705 \; kPa$, and the maximum absolute humidity is approximately **12.8 g/mВі**.

### 1.4 Why Humidity Matters in Building Automation

In modern Building Management Systems (BMS), humidity is a critical controlled variable because it directly impacts:

1. **Thermal Comfort** вҖ” Humans perceive humidity through evaporative cooling of sweat. High humidity makes $30^\circ C$ feel like $35^\circ C$.
2. **Health & IAQ** вҖ” Pathogens (bacteria, viruses, dust mites) thrive in specific humidity ranges. ASHRAE recommends $30\% - 60\%$ RH.
3. **Structural Integrity** вҖ” Excessive humidity causes mold, corrosion, and degradation of building materials.
4. **Energy Efficiency** вҖ” Humidification/dehumidification accounts for $30\% - 40\%$ of HVAC energy load in tropical climates like Kerala.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework вҖ” The Three Pillars of Humidity

#### Pillar 1: Absolute Humidity (AH)

Absolute humidity is the most physically intuitive measurement вҖ” it tells you literally "how much water is in this cubic meter of air." It is calculated using the **Ideal Gas Law** applied to the water vapor component:

$$AH = \frac{m_v}{V} = \frac{P_v}{R_v \cdot T}$$

Where:
- $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor (Pa)
- $R_v$ = gas constant of water vapor = $461.5 \; J/(kg \cdot K)$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

#### Pillar 2: Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio ($\omega$)

This is the **most used metric in HVAC engineering** because it is conserved across temperature changes (unlike RH). The derivation comes from the ideal gas law applied to both vapor and dry air components:

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

#### Pillar 3: Relative Humidity (RH)

RH is a *ratio of pressures*, not of masses. This is the most commonly misunderstood aspect of humidity:

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_s(T)}$$

Expressed as a percentage: $\%RH = \phi \times 100\%$.

### 2.2 The Dew Point Temperature (DPT)

The dew point is the temperature at which the air becomes **saturated** (RH = 100%) when cooled at constant pressure and constant water vapor content. Below DPT, condensation occurs. DPT depends *only* on $P_v$, not on the current temperature вҖ” making it an absolute measure of moisture content.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Absolute Humidity | $AH$ | $P_v / (R_v \cdot T)$ | $kg/m^3$ |
| Specific Humidity | $\omega$ | $0.622 \cdot P_v / (P - P_v)$ | $kg_{vapor}/kg_{dryair}$ |
| Relative Humidity | $\phi$ | $P_v / P_s(T)$ | dimensionless (0-1) |
| Saturation Pressure (approx.) | $P_s(T)$ | $610.78 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 T}{T + 237.3}}$ (T in В°C) | Pa |
| Dew Point (from $\omega$) | $T_{dp}$ | $\frac{237.3 \cdot \ln(e/6.108)}{17.27 - \ln(e/6.108)}$ | В°C |
| Enthalpy of Moist Air | $h$ | $1.005 T + \omega(2501 + 1.88 T)$ | $kJ/kg_{da}$ |
| Degree of Saturation | $\mu$ | $\omega / \omega_s$ | dimensionless |
| Specific Gas Constant (dry air) | $R_a$ | **287.05** | $J/(kg \cdot K)$ |
| Specific Gas Constant (vapor) | $R_v$ | **461.5** | $J/(kg \cdot K)$ |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R_u$ | **8.314** | $J/(mol \cdot K)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application Note:** In real BMS controllers, the formula $P_s(T) = 610.78 \cdot e^{17.27T/(T+237.3)}$ (the **Magnus formula**) is embedded in firmware of humidity transmitters. Field engineers must understand it, even if the sensor computes it automatically.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In commercial building automation, humidity control is implemented via:

- **Humidification systems** (steam injection, ultrasonic, evaporative) вҖ” used in hospitals, cleanrooms, museums.
- **Dehumidification systems** (refrigerant-based dehumidifiers, desiccant wheels) вҖ” used in pharmaceutical storage, swimming pools, and tropical coastal buildings.
- **Sensor networks** вҖ” typically using capacitive polymer sensors or chilled-mirror hygrometers, reporting back to the BMS via BACnet/Modbus protocols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Humidity from Partial Pressures

**Starting Point:** We have a moist air mixture with total pressure $P$ and water vapor partial pressure $P_v$. We want to find the ratio of the mass of water vapor to the mass of dry air.

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply the Ideal Gas Law to the water vapor component:**

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

Solving for the mass of water vapor:

$$m_v = \frac{P_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply the Ideal Gas Law to the dry air component:**

The partial pressure of dry air is $P_a = P - P_v$ (by Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures). Therefore:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

Solving for the mass of dry air:

$$m_a = \frac{P_a \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T} = \frac{(P - P_v) \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Form the ratio $m_v / m_a$:**

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{\frac{P_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T}}{\frac{(P - P_v) \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T}}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Cancel common terms.** The volume $V$ and temperature $T$ appear in both numerator and denominator, so they cancel out:

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Substitute the ratio of gas constants:**

$$R_a / R_v = 287.05 / 461.5 \approx 0.622$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem Walkthrough:** A classroom in a Kerala engineering college has air at $T = 30^\circ C$ (i.e., 303.15 K) and total pressure $P = 101.325 \; kPa$. A humidity sensor reports **Relative Humidity = 60%**. Find: (a) Partial pressure of water vapor, (b) Specific humidity, (c) Absolute humidity, (d) Dew point temperature.

**Given Data:**
- $T = 30^\circ C = 303.15 \; K$
- $P = 101325 \; Pa$
- $\phi = 0.60$

**Step (a) вҖ” Find the Saturation Pressure $P_s(30^\circ C)$:**

We use the **Magnus formula** from the cheat sheet:

$$P_s(30) = 610.78 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3}}$$

Computing the exponent:

$$\frac{17.27 \times 30}{30 + 237.3} = \frac{518.1}{267.3} = 1.9383$$

So:

$$P_s(30) = 610.78 \cdot e^{1.9383}$$

$$P_s(30) = 610.78 \cdot 6.9448 \approx 4242.8 \; Pa \approx 4.243 \; kPa$$

**Verify with standard table:** At $30^\circ C$, $P_s = 4.246 \; kPa$ вң“ (matches accepted value within rounding error).

**Now compute $P_v$:**

$$P_v = \phi \cdot P_s = 0.60 \times 4242.8 = 2545.68 \; Pa \approx 2.546 \; kPa$$

**Step (b) вҖ” Specific Humidity $\omega$:**

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2545.68}{101325 - 2545.68}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2545.68}{98779.32} = 0.622 \times 0.02577 = 0.01603 \; kg_v / kg_{da}$$

**Step (c) вҖ” Absolute Humidity AH:**

$$AH = \frac{P_v}{R_v \cdot T} = \frac{2545.68}{461.5 \times 303.15}$$

$$AH = \frac{2545.68}{139898.7} = 0.01819 \; kg/m^3 = 18.19 \; g/m^3$$

**Step (d) вҖ” Dew Point Temperature $T_{dp}$:**

Using the inverse Magnus formula. First compute the actual vapor pressure in hPa: $e = 25.457 \; hPa$.

$$T_{dp} = \frac{237.3 \cdot \ln(e / 6.108)}{17.27 - \ln(e / 6.108)}$$

Compute $\ln(e/6.108) = \ln(25.457/6.108) = \ln(4.168) = 1.4272$

$$T_{dp} = \frac{237.3 \times 1.4272}{17.27 - 1.4272} = \frac{338.69}{15.843} = 21.38^\circ C$$

**Final Answer Box:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_v &= 2.546 \; kPa \\
\omega &= 0.01603 \; kg/kg_{da} \\
AH &= 18.19 \; g/m^3 \\
T_{dp} &= 21.38^\circ C
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Comparing the Three Humidity Metrics

| **Property** | **Absolute Humidity** | **Specific Humidity ($\omega$)** | **Relative Humidity ($\phi$)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Mass of vapor / Volume of mixture | Mass of vapor / Mass of dry air | Partial pressure / Saturation pressure |
| Temperature Dependent? | **Yes** (inverse) | **No** (conserved) | **Yes** (strongly) |
| Pressure Dependent? | Yes | Slightly (via $P$) | No (ratio cancels) |
| Physical Meaning | "Total water in air" | "Mixing ratio" | "Saturation state" |
| Used in HVAC Loads? | Rarely | **Always** (psychrometrics) | Often (setpoints) |
| Typical Indoor Range | $5 - 20 \; g/m^3$ | $0.005 - 0.020 \; kg/kg$ | $30\% - 60\%$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Humidity Control Loop in a BMS

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the closed-loop control architecture for indoor humidity regulation in a modern Building Automation System. The architecture maps the signal flow from sensor to actuator via the central BMS controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Zone 1 Humidity Sensor] --> B[Analog to Digital Converter]
    C[Zone 2 Humidity Sensor] --> B
    D[Zone 3 Humidity Sensor] --> B
    B --> E[BMS Central Controller PID Algorithm]
    E --> F{Compare with Setpoint}
    F --> G[If RH too HIGH: Activate Dehumidifier]
    F --> H[If RH too LOW: Activate Humidifier]
    G --> I[Modulating Damper Actuator]
    H --> J[Steam Injection Valve]
    I --> K[Conditioned Air to Zones]
    J --> K
    K --> A
    K --> C
    K --> D
    E --> L[SCADA HMI Display]
    E --> M[Data Historian Logging]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow follows a classical **closed-loop feedback** structure. The three zone sensors provide parallel input. The PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) algorithm in the central controller compares the measured humidity with the user-defined setpoint (typically 50% RH) and issues corrective commands to either the dehumidification or humidification actuator. The SCADA HMI provides operator visibility, and the data historian stores trends for later analysis.

### 4.2 Psychrometric Process Flow вҖ” Summer Cooling & Dehumidification

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the state changes of an air parcel as it passes through a typical building AHU (Air Handling Unit) in a tropical climate like Kerala.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Outside Air 35C 80 percent RH] --> M1[Mixing Damper with Return Air]
    M1 --> M2[Cooling Coil 12C Chilled Water]
    M2 --> M3[Condensation Occurs Water Droplets Removed]
    M3 --> M4[Reheat Coil Optional]
    M4 --> S[Supply Air 24C 55 percent RH to Room]
    S --> R[Room Heat and Moisture Gains]
    R --> RA[Return Air 26C 60 percent RH]
    RA --> M1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Description:** The outside air (state O) is first mixed with the warmer return air from the room. It then passes over the cooling coil where its temperature drops sharply below the dew point, causing water vapor to condense out (dehumidification). An optional reheat coil restores the temperature to a comfortable level without re-adding moisture. The final supply air at 24В°C and 55% RH enters the room, where occupant activity and equipment add both heat and moisture, raising the RH to ~60% before being returned to the AHU.

### 4.3 Sensor Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Capacitive Polymer Sensor] --> P1[Low Cost Wide Range 5 to 95 percent RH]
    S2[Resistive Sensor] --> P2[Cheap Narrow Range Limited Accuracy]
    S3[Chilled Mirror Hygrometer] --> P3[Highest Accuracy Reference Standard]
    S4[Psychrometer Wet Dry Bulb] --> P4[Manual Lab Use No Power Needed]
    S1 --> BMS[Building Management System]
    S2 --> BMS
    S3 --> BMS
    S4 --> BMS
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024 Model]**

Define the following terms with proper units: (i) Absolute Humidity, (ii) Specific Humidity, (iii) Relative Humidity.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Absolute Humidity (AH):** It is defined as the mass of water vapor present per unit volume of moist air. **Formula:** $AH = m_v / V$. **Unit:** $g/m^3$ or $kg/m^3$. (1 Mark)

2. **Specific Humidity ($\omega$):** It is the mass of water vapor per unit mass of **dry air**. **Formula:** $\omega = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P - P_v)$. **Unit:** $kg_{vapor}/kg_{dryair}$. (1 Mark)

3. **Relative Humidity (RH, $\phi$):** It is the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor to the saturation pressure of water vapor at the same dry-bulb temperature. **Formula:** $\phi = P_v / P_s(T) \times 100\%$. **Unit:** Percentage (%). (1 Mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Model]**

What is Dew Point Temperature? Why is it considered a more reliable indicator of moisture content than Relative Humidity?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$) is the temperature at which the water vapor in the air becomes saturated (RH = 100%) and begins to condense into liquid water, when the air is cooled at constant pressure and constant moisture content.

**Why More Reliable than RH (1.5 Marks):**

- RH changes with temperature even when the actual moisture content remains constant (e.g., morning vs afternoon in the same room).
- $T_{dp}$ depends **only** on the partial pressure of water vapor $P_v$, which is a direct measure of moisture content.
- Therefore, $T_{dp}$ is an **absolute** indicator вҖ” two air samples with the same $T_{dp}$ always contain the same mass of water vapor per unit volume, regardless of temperature. This is why aircraft pilots and HVAC engineers use $T_{dp}$ as a primary moisture metric.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently lose **2 marks** by writing the formula for Specific Humidity as $\omega = m_v / m_{total}$ instead of $m_v / m_{dryair}$. **Always divide by mass of dry air, not total air.**
> - Another common error: forgetting to convert **В°C to Kelvin** in the Absolute Humidity formula. If the temperature is in В°C, you **must** add 273.15.
> - When asked to find RH, many students give a decimal (e.g., 0.6) instead of a percentage (60%). **Always express RH as a percentage in the final answer.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Specific Humidity** (humidity ratio) of moist air in terms of partial pressures of water vapor and dry air. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The air in a server room is at $T = 25^\circ C$ and total pressure $P = 101.325 \; kPa$. The relative humidity is measured to be **45%**. Calculate: (i) Partial pressure of water vapor, (ii) Specific humidity, (iii) Absolute humidity in $g/m^3$, (iv) Dew point temperature. Use the Magnus formula $P_s(T) = 610.78 \cdot e^{17.27T/(T+237.3)}$ with T in В°C. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Specific Humidity (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions:** (1 Mark)
- Moist air behaves as an ideal gas.
- Water vapor and dry air do not react chemically.
- The system is in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$ and total pressure $P$.

**Step 1:** Apply ideal gas law to water vapor: $P_v V = m_v R_v T$ вҶ’ $m_v = P_v V / (R_v T)$. (1.5 Marks)

**Step 2:** Apply ideal gas law to dry air. By Dalton's law, $P_a = P - P_v$: $P_a V = m_a R_a T$ вҶ’ $m_a = (P - P_v) V / (R_a T)$. (1.5 Marks)

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v \cdot R_a}{R_v \cdot (P - P_v)}$$

(1.5 Marks)

**Step 4:** Substitute $R_a / R_v = 287.05 / 461.5 = 0.622$:

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}}$$

(1 Mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $T = 25^\circ C$, $P = 101.325 \; kPa$, $\phi = 0.45$.

**(i) Saturation Pressure at 25В°C:** (1 Mark)

$$P_s(25) = 610.78 \cdot e^{\frac{17.27 \times 25}{25 + 237.3}} = 610.78 \cdot e^{\frac{431.75}{262.3}} = 610.78 \cdot e^{1.6460} = 610.78 \cdot 5.187 = 3168.5 \; Pa$$

**Partial Pressure of Water Vapor:** (1 Mark)

$$P_v = 0.45 \times 3168.5 = 1425.8 \; Pa \approx 1.426 \; kPa$$

**(ii) Specific Humidity:** (1.5 Marks)

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1425.8}{101325 - 1425.8} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1425.8}{99899.2} = 0.622 \times 0.01427 = 0.00888 \; kg/kg$$

**(iii) Absolute Humidity:** (1.5 Marks)

$$AH = \frac{P_v}{R_v T} = \frac{1425.8}{461.5 \times 298.15} = \frac{1425.8}{137595.7} = 0.01036 \; kg/m^3 = 10.36 \; g/m^3$$

**(iv) Dew Point Temperature:** (2 Marks)

Convert $P_v$ to hPa: $e = 14.258 \; hPa$.

$$T_{dp} = \frac{237.3 \times \ln(14.258 / 6.108)}{17.27 - \ln(14.258 / 6.108)} = \frac{237.3 \times \ln(2.335)}{17.27 - \ln(2.335)}$$

$$\ln(2.335) = 0.8479$$

$$T_{dp} = \frac{237.3 \times 0.8479}{17.27 - 0.8479} = \frac{201.21}{16.422} = 12.25^\circ C$$

**Final Summary:**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_v &= 1.426 \; kPa \\
\omega &= 0.00888 \; kg/kg \\
AH &= 10.36 \; g/m^3 \\
T_{dp} &= 12.25^\circ C
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2025 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **capacitive polymer humidity sensor** used in modern Building Management Systems. Mention its advantages over traditional wet-and-dry bulb psychrometers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop humidity control architecture** in a building automation system. List the typical setpoint range recommended by ASHRAE for human comfort and explain why controlling humidity is essential in tropical climates like Kerala. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Capacitive Polymer Humidity Sensor (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
- The sensor consists of a thin **hygroscopic polymer film** (typically polyimide or a specialized acrylic) sandwiched between two conductive electrodes, forming a small capacitor.
- The top electrode is porous, allowing water vapor molecules to pass through and be absorbed by the polymer dielectric.
- The bottom electrode is solid and is mounted on a ceramic substrate.
- The whole assembly is housed in a protective plastic case with a perforated cap to allow vapor diffusion.
- The capacitance is typically in the range of **100 вҖ“ 500 pF** at 50% RH.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**
- When water vapor molecules are absorbed by the polymer, the **dielectric constant** of the polymer changes (water has a very high dielectric constant of ~80, while polymer is ~3).
- This change in dielectric constant alters the capacitance: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
- The BMS reads the capacitance and converts it to an RH percentage using a calibration curve stored in firmware.

**Advantages over Wet-Dry Bulb Psychrometer (2 Marks):**
1. **No moving parts or water reservoir** вҖ” fully solid-state, low maintenance.
2. **Faster response time** (typically 5вҖ“30 seconds vs. several minutes for psychrometer).
3. **Direct digital/BACnet output** вҖ” integrates seamlessly with BMS.
4. **Better long-term stability** when properly calibrated.
5. Lower power consumption.

**(b) Closed-Loop Humidity Control (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 architecture вҖ” points allocated for neat diagram with labeled signal flow).

**Description (2 Marks):**
The BMS uses a **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** in a closed feedback loop:
- **Sensors** measure zone humidity continuously.
- The **controller** compares the measured RH to the setpoint.
- If RH > setpoint вҶ’ PID output increases вҶ’ **dehumidifier** (or chilled-water cooling coil) is modulated ON.
- If RH < setpoint вҶ’ **humidifier** (steam injector) is modulated ON.
- The **actuators** (valves, dampers, contactors) physically adjust the moisture injection/removal.

**ASHRAE Setpoint Range (1 Mark):** ASHRAE Standard 55 recommends **30% вҖ“ 60% RH** for human thermal comfort, with an ideal of **40% вҖ“ 50%**.

**Importance in Kerala (1 Mark):** Kerala's tropical climate has high outdoor humidity (often 80%вҖ“95%). Without proper dehumidification, indoor RH exceeds 70%, promoting mold growth, dust-mite proliferation, and condensation on AC ducts, leading to both health hazards and structural damage.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Humidity**

- **Absolute Humidity** = mass of vapor / volume of mixture. **Temperature-dependent** (use $T$ in Kelvin).
- **Specific Humidity** = $0.622 \cdot P_v / (P - P_v)$. **Denominator is dry air, not total air.**
- **Relative Humidity** = $P_v / P_s(T) \times 100\%$. **Always a percentage in final answer.**
- The constant **0.622** comes from $R_a / R_v$ вҖ” memorize it.
- **Magnus Formula** for $P_s(T)$: use $T$ in **В°C** (not Kelvin) inside the formula.
- **Dew Point** is an **absolute** moisture indicator вҖ” does not change with temperature fluctuations.
- **Capacitive sensors** are the modern BMS standard; **psychrometers** are now mainly lab equipment.
- **ASHRAE comfort range:** 30% вҖ“ 60% RH; ideal = 40% вҖ“ 50%.
- **Saturated air** = RH = 100%; $P_v = P_s(T)$; condensation begins.
- **High humidity** вҶ’ mold, corrosion, thermal discomfort, increased cooling load.
- **Low humidity** (<30%) вҶ’ dry skin, static electricity, respiratory irritation.
- The **humidity ratio $\omega$ is conserved** during sensible heating/cooling (only changes during humidification/dehumidification).
- **Enthalpy of moist air:** $h = 1.005T + \omega(2501 + 1.88T)$ kJ/kg dry air вҖ” used in cooling-coil load calculations.
- In **closed-loop control**, RH setpoint typically 50%; PID tuning uses typical values $K_p = 2$, $T_i = 120$ s, $T_d = 0$ s.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Loss
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Loss

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat Loss** in the context of building automation is defined as the rate of thermal energy transfer from the conditioned interior of a building to the unconditioned exterior environment (or vice versa, depending on the season) through the building envelope, ventilation pathways, and infiltration openings. It is quantified in **Watts (W)** or **British Thermal Units per hour (BTU/hr)** and is governed by the laws of thermodynamics вҖ” primarily the First Law (conservation of energy) and the Second Law (heat flows from hot to cold).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, heat loss is a sub-component of the broader **Building Thermal Load**, which also includes heat gains from solar radiation, internal equipment, occupants, and ventilation air. The HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) system sizing, chiller/boiler capacity selection, and BAS (Building Automation System) control loop tuning are all critically dependent on accurate heat loss calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Heat Loss is the foundational concept for understanding how buildings interact thermally with their environment. Every downstream topic вҖ” including heating coil sizing, thermostat placement, zone control, and energy-efficient BAS strategies вҖ” is rooted in the accurate estimation of heat loss.

### 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and units are essential for KTU-level problems:

- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Measured in **W/(mВ·K)** or **BTU/(hrВ·ftВ·В°F)**
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value):** Measured in **W/(mВІВ·K)**
- **Thermal Resistance (R-value):** Measured in **(mВІВ·K)/W**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (Пғ):** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4\text{)}$
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant for Engineering (English units):** $\sigma = 0.1714 \times 10^{-8} \text{ BTU/(hrВ·ft}^2\cdotВ°\text{R}^4\text{)}$
- **Degree Day:** A unit representing a 1В°C (or 1В°F) difference between the mean outdoor temperature and a reference (usually 18В°C / 65В°F) over 24 hours.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference:** A building's "U-value" is the heat loss in Watts per square meter for every 1В°C temperature difference across that building element. Lower U-value = better insulation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The Coffee Cup Model

Imagine holding a cup of hot coffee on a winter morning. The coffee cools down rapidly. Why? Because thermal energy escapes through three parallel "escape routes":

1. **Through the ceramic walls (Conduction)** вҖ” heat travels molecule-to-molecule through the cup material.
2. **Off the surface of the cup (Convection)** вҖ” surrounding cold air sweeps the heat away.
3. **As infrared waves to the cold window (Radiation)** вҖ” the cup emits electromagnetic energy.

A building behaves identically. The "walls" are the envelope (brick, glass, insulation), the "wind" is the outdoor air movement, and the "cold window" is the night sky. **Heat Loss = (Conduction) + (Convection) + (Radiation) + (Infiltration/Ventilation)**.

By understanding and quantifying each pathway, a Building Automation System can intelligently control thermostats, dampers, blinds, and HVAC equipment to minimize energy waste.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Builder:** Think of heat loss as "thermal money leaking out of your building wallet." Every BTU lost is a rupee spent on heating fuel. BAS = your automated accountant that detects and plugs the leaks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer in Buildings

#### 2.1.1 Conduction (Dominant in opaque building elements)

Heat flows through solid materials (walls, roofs, floors) via molecular vibration transfer. Governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**.

- Heat always flows from the higher temperature region to the lower temperature region.
- The rate is proportional to the cross-sectional area, temperature difference, and material conductivity.
- The rate is inversely proportional to the material thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is conduction dominant in walls?** Most building envelopes are opaque (brick, concrete, insulation). Radiant energy cannot pass through them, and convective air currents inside solid walls are negligible. Hence, conduction is the bottleneck.

#### 2.1.2 Convection (Dominant at air-surface interfaces)

Heat transfer between a solid surface (interior wall, window glass) and the adjacent moving fluid (air). Two sub-types:

- **Natural Convection:** Buoyancy-driven, dependent on the temperature difference $\Delta T$ and orientation of the surface (horizontal vs. vertical).
- **Forced Convection:** Externally driven by wind, fans, or HVAC blowers.

In building heat loss, the **combined external surface coefficient** accounts for both forced wind and natural convection.

#### 2.1.3 Radiation (Critical for high-temperature surfaces and night sky losses)

All bodies above absolute zero emit thermal radiation. For building surfaces, the relevant emission is in the **infrared (IR) spectrum**.

- Heat is exchanged between the building envelope and surrounding surfaces (ground, neighboring buildings, the **sky dome**).
- On clear nights, the sky temperature can drop to **10вҖ“20В°C below ambient air temperature**, causing significant **radiative cooling losses** from horizontal roof surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Remember:** Conduction, convection, and radiation happen **in parallel** at each surface. Engineers must add their resistances as reciprocals (analogous to parallel electrical resistors), not as a simple sum.

### 2.2 Building Envelope Heat Loss Pathways

The building envelope consists of five major element types:

1. **Walls (above-grade and basement)** вҖ” typically the largest heat loss surface.
2. **Roof / Ceiling** вҖ” horizontal top surface, prone to stack-effect driven heat loss.
3. **Floor (slab-on-grade and above-grade)** вҖ” often overlooked but can contribute 5вҖ“15% of total loss.
4. **Windows and Glazed Doors** вҖ” high U-values; can be 5вҖ“10Г— worse than insulated walls.
5. **Doors (solid, insulated, uninsulated)** вҖ” significant in commercial and industrial buildings.

Additionally, **infiltration** and **ventilation** contribute substantially to total heat loss вҖ” often 20вҖ“40% in leaky buildings.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet вҖ” Master These Before Exam Day**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | k: W/(mВ·K), A: mВІ, L: m, T: K or В°C | Single-layer wall |
| 2 | Thermal Resistance (single layer) | $R = \dfrac{L}{k \cdot A}$ | R: K/W | Resistance to conduction |
| 3 | R-value (per unit area) | $R = \dfrac{L}{k}$ | R: (mВІВ·K)/W | Material property |
| 4 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + R_{wall} + R_{so}}$ | U: W/(mВІВ·K) | Composite wall |
| 5 | Heat Loss (envelope element) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | U: W/(mВІВ·K), A: mВІ, О”T: K or В°C | Window/wall/door |
| 6 | Composite Wall U-value | $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$ | h: W/(mВІВ·K) | Multi-layer wall |
| 7 | Convection (Newton's Law) | $Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty)$ | h: W/(mВІВ·K) | Surface heat loss |
| 8 | Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann) | $Q = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Оө: emissivity, Пғ: 5.67Г—10вҒ»вҒё | Roof/panel |
| 9 | Degree-Day Method | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot DD \cdot 24$ | DD: degree-days | Seasonal load |
| 10 | Infiltration Heat Loss | $Q = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$ | ПҒ: kg/mВі, cвӮҡ: 1005 J/(kgВ·K) | Air leakage |
| 11 | Ventilation Heat Loss | $Q = 0.33 \cdot ACH \cdot V_{room} \cdot \Delta T$ | ACH: air changes/hr | Mechanical ventilation |
| 12 | Stack Effect (Pressure) | $\Delta P = C \cdot H \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T_o} - \dfrac{1}{T_i}\right)$ | C: 0.0342, H: height m | High-rise buildings |
| 13 | Heat Loss per Meter of Pipe | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k (T_i - T_o)}{\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | k, r: pipe conductivity, radius | Insulated pipe runs |
| 14 | Linear (Fin) Heat Loss | $Q = U \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | L: perimeter length m | Slab edge loss |
| 15 | Total Building Heat Loss | $Q_{total} = \sum Q_i + Q_{inf} + Q_{vent}$ | All losses summed | BAS sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The 1-dimensional steady-state composite wall calculation is the **#1 most-asked formula** in Module 3 problems. Practice plugging in surface resistances ($h_i \approx 8.3$, $h_o \approx 23$ W/(mВІВ·K) for exterior) repeatedly until it's muscle memory.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **building automation engineering**, heat loss calculations are not just academic вҖ” they drive:

- **HVAC Equipment Sizing:** Boilers, chillers, AHUs are sized to handle the peak design heat loss (typically calculated at design outdoor temperature, e.g., 0В°C in Kerala context).
- **Insulation Specification:** Architects use U-value targets (e.g., ECBC mandate вүӨ 0.4 W/(mВІВ·K) for walls) to specify insulation thickness.
- **BAS Sensor Placement:** Thermostats are placed on the most exposed (highest heat loss) walls to avoid short-cycling.
- **Energy Auditing:** Hourly heat loss estimates feed into chiller/boiler scheduling algorithms.
- **Fault Detection:** A sudden spike in measured heat loss indicates broken insulation, open damper, or stuck valve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Loss Through a Composite Wall (Steady-State, 1D)

**Problem Setup:** A building wall consists of three layers in series вҖ” outer brick, middle insulation, inner plaster. Heat flows from the warm interior (T_i) to the cold exterior (T_o).

**Step 1: Identify the thermal circuit.**

Heat flow encounters resistances in series:
- Interior surface resistance $R_{si} = \dfrac{1}{h_i \cdot A}$
- Brick conduction resistance $R_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1 \cdot A}$
- Insulation conduction resistance $R_2 = \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 \cdot A}$
- Plaster conduction resistance $R_3 = \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 \cdot A}$
- Exterior surface resistance $R_{so} = \dfrac{1}{h_o \cdot A}$

**Step 2: Apply the thermal Ohm's Law analog.**

For a series circuit, the heat current is the same through all resistances, and total temperature difference is the sum of individual drops.

$$T_i - T_o = Q \cdot (R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{so})$$

**Step 3: Solve for the heat flow rate Q.**

$$Q = \dfrac{T_i - T_o}{R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_{so}}$$

**Step 4: Express in terms of overall U-value (per unit area).**

Dividing numerator and denominator by A:

$$Q = \dfrac{A \cdot (T_i - T_o)}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

Define the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

Therefore the heat loss per unit area is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** U-value is the **inverse of total thermal resistance per unit area**. To minimize Q (heat loss), we need to minimize U, which means maximizing the denominator вҖ” i.e., adding more insulation layers.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Composite Wall Heat Loss

**Problem Statement (Sample KTU Style):**
A building wall has the following construction (outside to inside):
- 100 mm face brick, $k_1 = 0.77$ W/(mВ·K)
- 50 mm polystyrene insulation, $k_2 = 0.04$ W/(mВ·K)
- 200 mm concrete block, $k_3 = 1.40$ W/(mВ·K)
- 15 mm gypsum plaster, $k_4 = 0.17$ W/(mВ·K)

Surface coefficients: $h_i = 8.3$ W/(mВІВ·K), $h_o = 23$ W/(mВІВ·K).

Indoor temp $T_i = 22В°C$, outdoor temp $T_o = 5В°C$.
Wall area $A = 12$ mВІ. Find the heat loss through this wall.

**Step 1: List all resistances (per mВІ area).**

$$R_{si} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{8.3} = 0.1205 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_1 = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1} = \dfrac{0.100}{0.77} = 0.1299 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_2 = \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} = \dfrac{0.050}{0.04} = 1.2500 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_3 = \dfrac{L_3}{k_3} = \dfrac{0.200}{1.40} = 0.1429 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_4 = \dfrac{L_4}{k_4} = \dfrac{0.015}{0.17} = 0.0882 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_{so} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{23} = 0.0435 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

**Step 2: Sum all resistances in series.**

$$R_{total} = 0.1205 + 0.1299 + 1.2500 + 0.1429 + 0.0882 + 0.0435$$

$$R_{total} = 1.7750 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate U-value.**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{1}{1.7750} = 0.5634 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate heat loss.**

$$\Delta T = T_i - T_o = 22 - 5 = 17 \text{ K}$$

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.5634 \times 12 \times 17$$

$$Q = 114.93 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The heat loss through this wall is approximately **115 W**. Notice that the 50 mm polystyrene insulation alone contributes **70%** of the total resistance вҖ” a great example of why insulation layer choice is critical.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Different Envelope Elements

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table** (often asked in viva and 2-mark questions):

| Element | Typical U-Value (W/(mВІВ·K)) | Relative Heat Loss | BAS Control Strategy |
|---------|----------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Insulated Wall (with 50mm PU) | 0.30 вҖ“ 0.50 | Low | None (passive) |
| Uninsulated Brick Wall (230mm) | 2.00 вҖ“ 2.50 | High | Add insulation retrofit |
| Single-Glazed Window | 5.0 вҖ“ 5.8 | Very High | Add low-E film / shading |
| Double-Glazed Window (12mm air gap) | 2.5 вҖ“ 3.0 | High | Automatic blinds |
| Insulated Roof (100mm PU) | 0.20 вҖ“ 0.30 | Very Low | None (passive) |
| Uninsulated Slab-on-Grade | 1.50 вҖ“ 2.00 | Medium | Underfloor insulation |
| Solid Wood Door | 3.0 вҖ“ 4.0 | High | Weatherstripping |
| Insulated Steel Door | 0.80 вҖ“ 1.20 | Low | None |

### 3.4 Degree-Day Method (Seasonal Heat Loss)

**Derivation:** A "degree day" integrates the outdoor temperature deficit over 24 hours.

$$DD = T_{base} - T_{mean,outdoor}$$

If $T_{mean,outdoor} = 10В°C$ and base = 18В°C, then for that day: $DD = 8$ degree-days.

The total annual heat loss is:

$$Q_{annual} = U \cdot A \cdot DD_{annual} \cdot 24 \text{ hours/day}$$

**Worked Example:**
Wall area = 100 mВІ, U = 0.5 W/(mВІВ·K), annual heating degree days = 800 (typical for Kerala highlands).

$$Q_{annual} = 0.5 \times 100 \times 800 \times 24 = 960{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 960 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why degree-days matter for BAS:** Degree-day data is fed to **weather-compensated control algorithms** in modern BAS. The system "knows" the outdoor temperature history and pre-positions valves/dampers to avoid overshoot.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Heat Loss Pathways вҖ” Block Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete interaction of heat loss mechanisms in a building automation context, showing how each physical phenomenon translates into a measurable quantity and a control action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Building Envelope] --> B[Wall Conduction Path]
    A --> C[Window Conduction Path]
    A --> D[Roof Conduction Path]
    A --> E[Floor Conduction Path]

    B --> F1[Fourier Law Q1 = kA delta T over L]
    C --> F2[Glass U-value Q2 = U glass times A times delta T]
    D --> F3[Roof with sky radiation Q3 = eps sigma A delta T4]
    E --> F4[Slab edge Q4 = U perimeter times L times delta T]

    F1 --> G[Total Envelope Heat Loss Qenvelope]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G

    H[Infiltration and Ventilation] --> I[Air Change Heat Loss]
    I --> I1[Qinf = rho cp V delta T]
    I1 --> J[Total Building Heat Loss Qtotal]

    G --> J
    J --> K[HVAC Sizing and BAS Controller]
    K --> L1[Boiler Chiller Capacity]
    K --> L2[Thermostat Setpoint Logic]
    K --> L3[Damper and Valve Modulation]
    K --> L4[Energy Dashboard Reporting]
```

### 4.2 Composite Wall Thermal Resistance Network

The following diagram illustrates the series-resistance model used in the derivation above. Each block is a thermal resistor; the heat current Q flows through them in series from the warm interior to the cold exterior.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Node1[Indoor Air Temp Ti] --> Node2[Interior Surface Resistance Rsi]
    Node2 --> Node3[Brick Layer R1]
    Node3 --> Node4[Insulation Layer R2]
    Node4 --> Node5[Concrete Block R3]
    Node5 --> Node6[Gypsum Plaster R4]
    Node6 --> Node7[Exterior Surface Resistance Rso]
    Node7 --> Node8[Outdoor Air Temp To]

    Node2 -.->|1 over hi| R10[Rsi equals 0.120]
    Node3 -.->|L1 over k1| R11[R1 equals 0.130]
    Node4 -.->|L2 over k2| R12[R2 equals 1.250]
    Node5 -.->|L3 over k3| R13[R3 equals 0.143]
    Node6 -.->|L4 over k4| R14[R4 equals 0.088]
    Node7 -.->|1 over ho| R15[Rso equals 0.043]
```

### 4.3 Heat Loss Reduction Strategy Hierarchy

This block diagram shows the **priority order** of heat loss reduction strategies as typically implemented in a BAS-driven smart building, from passive envelope improvements to active control loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Heat Loss Reduction Strategy] --> S1[Level 1 Passive Envelope]
    S0 --> S2[Level 2 Efficient Glazing]
    S0 --> S3[Level 3 Air Sealing]
    S0 --> S4[Level 4 Smart BAS Controls]

    S1 --> S1a[Add Wall Insulation]
    S1 --> S1b[Roof Insulation]
    S1 --> S1c[Floor Edge Insulation]

    S2 --> S2a[Double or Triple Glazing]
    S2 --> S2b[Low Emissivity Coatings]
    S2 --> S2c[Automatic Shading Devices]

    S3 --> S3a[Weatherstripping Doors]
    S3 --> S3b[Seal Window Frames]
    S3 --> S3c[Duct Sealing]

    S4 --> S4a[Weather Compensated Setpoint]
    S4 --> S4b[Optimal Start Stop Logic]
    S4 --> S4c[Night Setback Control]
    S4 --> S4d[Demand Controlled Ventilation]
```

### 4.4 Stack Effect and Infiltration Flow (High-Rise Context)

For taller buildings, the **stack effect** drives significant infiltration losses. The following diagram maps the pressure-driven airflow loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[Neutral Pressure Level] --> P1[Below Neutral Lower Floor]
    P0 --> P2[Above Neutral Upper Floor]

    P1 --> P1a[Outdoor Air Infiltrates IN]
    P1a --> P1b[Cold Air Infiltration Loss]

    P2 --> P2a[Warm Indoor Air Exfiltrates OUT]
    P2a --> P2b[Warm Air Exfiltration Loss]

    P1b --> P3[Total Infiltration Heat Loss]
    P2b --> P3

    P3 --> P4[BAS Mitigation Strategies]
    P4 --> P4a[Lobby Vestibule Doors]
    P4 --> P4b[Stairwell Pressurization]
    P4 --> P4c[Elevator Shaft Damper Control]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:** Define **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value)** and explain its significance in building heat loss calculations.

**Model Answer:**

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U-value)** is defined as the rate of steady-state heat transfer (in Watts) through one square meter of a building element for every one Kelvin (or 1В°C) of temperature difference between the indoor and outdoor environments. Its unit is **W/(mВІВ·K)**.

Mathematically, for a composite wall, the U-value is the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit area:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum R_i + R_{so}}$$

**Significance in building heat loss calculations:**

1. It provides a single, easy-to-use parameter to characterize the insulating performance of an entire building element (e.g., a wall with brick, insulation, and plaster).
2. The heat loss through any envelope element can be directly calculated using the simple formula $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$.
3. It enables fair comparison between different building elements (e.g., walls vs. windows) and between different construction technologies.
4. Building codes (such as ECBC in India) specify maximum permissible U-values for compliance.
5. Lower U-values indicate better insulation and lower heat loss, which translates into reduced HVAC energy consumption.

A typical insulated wall in modern construction has a U-value of **0.30 to 0.50 W/(mВІВ·K)**, while a single-glazed window can have **5.0 to 5.8 W/(mВІВ·K)** вҖ” roughly 10вҖ“15 times higher.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:** Differentiate between **conduction, convection, and radiation** as modes of heat loss in buildings, giving one example of each from building components.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Mechanism | Medium/Requirement | Building Component Example |
|------|-----------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Conduction** | Transfer of heat through a material by molecular vibration and free electron movement. Requires a solid, liquid, or gas medium but no bulk fluid motion. | Static medium (solid wall, stationary air) | Heat flowing through a brick wall from the warm interior to the cold exterior. |
| **Convection** | Transfer of heat between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid or gas) due to bulk motion and mixing. | Moving fluid (air or water) | Heat carried away from the outer surface of a building wall by cold winter wind. |
| **Radiation** | Transfer of heat in the form of electromagnetic waves (primarily infrared) that does not require any medium вҖ” it can occur in vacuum. | Electromagnetic emission from all bodies above absolute zero | Heat radiated from a hot black roof to the night sky, causing significant nighttime cooling. |

In a typical building heat loss problem, all three modes act **simultaneously and in parallel** at the surfaces of building elements, while conduction is dominant within the solid layers of the envelope.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> 
> **(a)** A building wall consists of the following layers (from outside to inside): 25 mm cement plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/(mВ·K)), 200 mm brick masonry ($k = 0.81$ W/(mВ·K)), 50 mm expanded polystyrene insulation ($k = 0.035$ W/(mВ·K)), and 15 mm gypsum board ($k = 0.17$ W/(mВ·K)). The inside and outside surface heat transfer coefficients are $h_i = 8.3$ W/(mВІВ·K) and $h_o = 23$ W/(mВІВ·K), respectively. The wall area is 18 mВІ, indoor temperature is 24В°C, and outdoor temperature is 8В°C. Calculate:
> 
> (i) The overall heat transfer coefficient (U-value) of the wall. **(4 marks)**
> 
> (ii) The total heat loss through the wall. **(3 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** With the aid of a thermal resistance network, derive the general expression for the U-value of a composite wall consisting of $n$ layers in series, and discuss how Building Automation Systems (BAS) can utilize heat loss data for HVAC control. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a)(i): U-value Calculation [4 marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate individual resistances per mВІ area.**

$$R_{si} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{8.3} = 0.1205 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_1 = \dfrac{0.025}{0.72} = 0.0347 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_2 = \dfrac{0.200}{0.81} = 0.2469 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_3 = \dfrac{0.050}{0.035} = 1.4286 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_4 = \dfrac{0.015}{0.17} = 0.0882 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_{so} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{23} = 0.0435 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

**Step 2: Sum the resistances in series.**

$$R_{total} = 0.1205 + 0.0347 + 0.2469 + 1.4286 + 0.0882 + 0.0435$$

$$R_{total} = 1.9624 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate U-value.**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{1.9624} = 0.5096 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

> **Answer (a)(i):** $U \approx 0.51$ W/(mВІВ·K)

**Part (a)(ii): Heat Loss Calculation [3 marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate temperature difference.**

$$\Delta T = 24 - 8 = 16 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Apply heat loss equation.**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.5096 \times 18 \times 16$$

$$Q = 146.77 \text{ W}$$

> **Answer (a)(ii):** Heat loss $Q \approx 146.77$ W

**Part (b): U-value Derivation and BAS Application [7 marks]**

**Derivation [4 marks]:**

Consider a composite wall with $n$ layers having thicknesses $L_1, L_2, \ldots, L_n$ and thermal conductivities $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n$ respectively. The wall is bounded on the indoor side by air at temperature $T_i$ and on the outdoor side by air at temperature $T_o$.

The surface heat transfer on the indoor side follows Newton's law of cooling:

$$Q = h_i \cdot A \cdot (T_i - T_{s1})$$

where $T_{s1}$ is the inner surface temperature of the wall. This can be rewritten as:

$$T_i - T_{s1} = \dfrac{Q}{h_i \cdot A} = Q \cdot R_{si}$$

For each layer $i$ in series, Fourier's law gives:

$$T_{s,i} - T_{s,i+1} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L_i}{k_i \cdot A} = Q \cdot R_i$$

For the outer surface:

$$T_{sn} - T_o = \dfrac{Q}{h_o \cdot A} = Q \cdot R_{so}$$

Summing all the temperature drops:

$$T_i - T_o = Q \cdot \left( R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + \ldots + R_n + R_{so} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$Q = \dfrac{A \cdot (T_i - T_o)}{R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i + R_{so}}$$

The U-value per unit area is:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{so}}$$

**BAS Application Discussion [3 marks]:**

Building Automation Systems utilize heat loss data for several key control functions:

1. **Weather-Compensated Setpoint Control:** BAS continuously receives outdoor temperature from a weather sensor and pre-calculates the heating demand using the U-value of the envelope. The supply water temperature of the boiler is modulated to match the actual heat loss, avoiding wasteful overheating.

2. **Optimal Start/Stop Algorithms:** BAS uses the heat loss rate and thermal mass of the building to determine the latest possible time to start HVAC in the morning, ensuring occupant comfort at scheduled occupancy time with minimum energy use.

3. **Night Setback Control:** During unoccupied hours, BAS intelligently lowers the heating setpoint. The allowable setback is calculated using the building's heat loss rate and desired morning recovery time.

4. **Fault Detection and Diagnostics (FDD):** Continuous monitoring of actual heat loss (via BTU meters) versus predicted heat loss (from U Г— A Г— О”T) enables the BAS to detect anomalies such as stuck valves, broken insulation, or open windows, and raise maintenance alarms.

5. **Zone-Level Demand Control:** In multi-zone buildings, BAS applies different setpoints and modulates zone dampers based on localized heat loss characteristics (e.g., north-facing zone with high wall-area-to-volume ratio gets a different control profile than a south-facing zone).

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> 
> **(a)** Explain in detail the three modes of heat transfer in buildings with neat diagrams. For a double-glazed window consisting of two 4 mm glass panes with a 12 mm air gap, estimate the U-value given: $k_{glass} = 0.81$ W/(mВ·K), $R_{air\ gap} = 0.16$ (mВІВ·K)/W, $h_i = 8.3$ W/(mВІВ·K), $h_o = 23$ W/(mВІВ·K). Find the heat loss per mВІ for a temperature difference of 15В°C. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Discuss the **degree-day method** for estimating seasonal heat loss in buildings. For a residential building with total envelope heat loss coefficient $UA = 250$ W/K and annual heating degree days (base 18В°C) = 1200, calculate the annual heating energy consumption in kWh and the approximate annual heating cost in вӮ№ (assuming boiler efficiency = 0.85 and LPG cost = вӮ№1100 per 14.2 kg cylinder with calorific value 50 MJ/kg). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a): Heat Transfer Modes and Window U-Value [7 marks]**

**Three modes of heat transfer [3 marks]:**

1. **Conduction** is the transfer of heat through a stationary medium by direct molecular contact. In buildings, conduction occurs through walls, roofs, floors, windows, and doors. Fourier's law governs the rate: $Q = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{L}$.

2. **Convection** is the transfer of heat between a solid surface and a moving fluid (air). In buildings, the indoor air movement (forced by fans or naturally by buoyancy) carries heat from walls to occupants and vice versa. Newton's law applies: $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$. The interior surface coefficient $h_i$ accounts for natural convection; the exterior $h_o$ accounts for combined wind and natural convection.

3. **Radiation** is the transfer of heat by electromagnetic waves, primarily in the infrared spectrum. All surfaces above absolute zero emit radiation proportional to the fourth power of their absolute temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann law). Buildings exchange radiation with the sky, ground, and surrounding structures.

**Schematic representation:**

```
WARM INTERIOR                  COLD EXTERIOR
   |                                ^
   |  CONVECTION  |  CONDUCTION  |  CONVECTION  |  RADIATION
   |  (hi)        |  (Layers)    |  (ho)        |  (to sky)
   v              |              |              |
   Ti ---Rsi--- [Layer1]---[Layer2]---...---Rso--- To
   |                                ^
   |<-- Total Thermal Resistance Rtotal -->|
   |              Q = A delta T / Rtotal  |
```

**Window U-value calculation [4 marks]:**

**Step 1: List the resistances in series for the double-glazed unit.**

$$R_{si} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} = \dfrac{1}{8.3} = 0.1205 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_{glass1} = \dfrac{0.004}{0.81} = 0.0049 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_{air\ gap} = 0.16 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W} \text{ (given)}$$

$$R_{glass2} = \dfrac{0.004}{0.81} = 0.0049 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

$$R_{so} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} = \dfrac{1}{23} = 0.0435 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

**Step 2: Sum the resistances.**

$$R_{total} = 0.1205 + 0.0049 + 0.16 + 0.0049 + 0.0435 = 0.3338 \text{ (m}^2\cdot\text{K)/W}$$

**Step 3: Calculate U-value.**

$$U = \dfrac{1}{0.3338} = 2.996 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

> **Answer:** $U \approx 3.0$ W/(mВІВ·K)

**Step 4: Calculate heat loss per mВІ for О”T = 15В°C.**

$$q = U \cdot \Delta T = 2.996 \times 15 = 44.94 \text{ W/m}^2$$

> **Answer:** Heat loss per mВІ вүҲ 45 W

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Insight:** Compare this with the insulated wall (0.51 W/(mВІВ·K)) from Question A. The double-glazed window loses **6Г— more heat per unit area** than the insulated wall вҖ” which is why window area and glazing specification are critical in energy-efficient building design.

---

**Part (b): Degree-Day Method and Annual Energy Cost [7 marks]**

**Degree-Day Method Theory [3 marks]:**

The **degree-day** is a heating/cooling demand index that represents the cumulative severity of outdoor weather over a period. A **heating degree day (HDD)** is defined as the difference between a reference base temperature (typically 18В°C) and the mean daily outdoor temperature, summed over the year:

$$HDD_{annual} = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \max(0, T_{base} - T_{mean,outdoor,d})$$

The annual heat loss through the building envelope is:

$$Q_{annual} = UA \cdot HDD_{annual} \cdot 24 \text{ hours/day} \text{ (in Wh)}$$

This is converted to energy input to the heating system by dividing by the boiler efficiency:

$$E_{input} = \dfrac{Q_{annual}}{\eta_{boiler}}$$

**Calculation [4 marks]:**

**Step 1: Calculate annual heat loss in Wh.**

$$Q_{annual} = 250 \text{ W/K} \times 1200 \text{ KВ·days} \times 24 \text{ h/day}$$

$$Q_{annual} = 7{,}200{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 7200 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2: Convert to MJ.**

$$Q_{annual} = 7200 \times 3.6 = 25{,}920 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3: Account for boiler efficiency.**

$$E_{input} = \dfrac{25{,}920}{0.85} = 30{,}494.12 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 4: Calculate LPG mass required.**

$$\text{LPG mass} = \dfrac{30{,}494.12}{50} = 609.88 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5: Calculate number of cylinders.**

$$\text{Cylinders} = \dfrac{609.88}{14.2} = 42.95 \text{ cylinders}$$

**Step 6: Calculate annual cost.**

$$\text{Cost} = 42.95 \times 1100 = вӮ№47{,}245$$

> **Answer:** Annual energy consumption = **7200 kWh** of heat delivered; annual heating cost вүҲ **вӮ№47,245** (at the given LPG price).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World BAS Impact:** A 30% reduction in envelope heat loss (e.g., by adding insulation or upgrading windows) would save approximately вӮ№14,000 per year in this building вҖ” a powerful argument for BAS-driven retrofits.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting surface resistances ($R_{si}$ and $R_{so}$):** Many students omit the surface film resistances and use only the bulk layer resistances. This gives an **incorrectly high U-value** and overestimates heat loss. The KTU key always awards full marks only when both surface terms are included.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up R-value and U-value:** R-value is in (mВІВ·K)/W (additive in series). U-value is in W/(mВІВ·K) (reciprocal of total R). A common error is to sum U-values directly. They must be summed as **1/U** (analogous to parallel electrical resistors).
> 
> 3. **Unit mismatch in О”T:** Temperature differences in Kelvin and Celsius are numerically equal, but ensure you don't write "О”T in K equals О”T in В°C" without proper notation. The proper expression is $\Delta T(K) = \Delta T(В°C)$.
> 
> 4. **Conduction vs. Convection confusion:** Many students apply Fourier's law (conduction) at the air interface. The correct law is Newton's law of cooling ($Q = hA\Delta T$) for air-surface heat transfer.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring infiltration/ventilation losses:** In real buildings, 20вҖ“40% of total heat loss is from air leakage, not just conduction. Always mention or include infiltration losses for a complete answer.
> 
> 6. **Degree-day formula units:** The standard formula $Q = UA \times DD \times 24$ yields Wh only when DD is in KВ·days, U in W/(mВІВ·K), A in mВІ, and the factor 24 is in hours/day. Many students forget the "24" multiplier, getting a wrong answer by a factor of 24.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist вҖ” Heat Loss (Module 3)**
> 
> вң… **Core Definition:** Heat loss is the rate of thermal energy escape from the building interior to the exterior, governed by conduction, convection, radiation, and infiltration.
> 
> вң… **Fourier's Law (Conduction):** $Q = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{L}$ вҖ” the foundation of all wall/roof/floor heat loss calculations.
> 
> вң… **Newton's Law of Cooling (Convection):** $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ вҖ” applies at all air-surface interfaces.
> 
> вң… **Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation):** $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ вҖ” critical for roof sky-radiation losses.
> 
> вң… **U-value Formula:** $U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$ вҖ” the most-tested formula in KTU Module 3.
> 
> вң… **Standard Surface Coefficients:** $h_i \approx 8.3$ W/(mВІВ·K) (interior, still air); $h_o \approx 23$ W/(mВІВ·K) (exterior, with wind).
> 
> вң… **Heat Loss Formula:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ вҖ” for steady-state, 1D heat loss through any envelope element.
> 
> вң… **Degree-Day Method:** $Q_{annual} = UA \times DD \times 24$ вҖ” for seasonal/annual estimates.
> 
> вң… **Composite Walls:** Resistances add in series; conductances (U-values) add in parallel вҖ” analogous to electrical resistor networks.
> 
> вң… **Infiltration Heat Loss:** $Q_{inf} = \rho c_p V \Delta T$; Ventilation loss: $Q_{vent} = 0.33 \times ACH \times V_{room} \times \Delta T$.
> 
> вң… **Stack Effect:** In high-rise buildings, $\Delta P = 0.0342 H (1/T_o - 1/T_i)$ drives infiltration; BAS mitigates with vestibules, shaft dampers, and pressurization fans.
> 
> вң… **R-value vs. U-value:** R is additive (layers in series), U is the inverse. A high R-value = low U-value = better insulation.
> 
> вң… **Typical U-values:** Insulated wall ~0.4; Double-glazed window ~3.0; Single-glazed window ~5.5 W/(mВІВ·K).
> 
> вң… **BAS Applications:** Weather-compensated control, optimal start/stop, night setback, FDD (fault detection), zone-level demand control вҖ” all driven by heat loss calculations.
> 
> вң… **Always remember:** Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(mВІВ·KвҒҙ) вҖ” the 4th-power temperature dependence makes radiation dominant at high temperatures and significant for clear-night roof losses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use of Active Technologies for Improving the Performance of Air Conditioning and Ventilation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Use of Active Technologies for Improving the Performance of Air Conditioning and Ventilation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Building Automation Systems (BAS) and the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Building Automation*, **Active Technologies** for HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) systems are defined as engineered subsystems that consume **external electrical, mechanical, or thermal energy** to dynamically modulate their operating parameters in real time, in order to **optimize thermal comfort, indoor air quality (IAQ), and energy efficiency** beyond the baseline performance of conventional constant-volume systems.

These technologies form the **"smart muscle"** of a modern green building, working in tandem with sensors, actuators, and the central **Building Management System (BMS)** controller to implement closed-loop feedback control. They are classified as "active" precisely because they **require auxiliary power** to perform their energy-saving function, in stark contrast to **passive technologies** (e.g., building orientation, thermal mass, natural ventilation) which rely solely on environmental forces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:** *An active technology in HVAC is any device or system that uses external energy input (electrical, mechanical, or solar) and is governed by an automatic control signal from the BAS to vary its output (capacity, flow, or pressure) in proportion to the instantaneous building load.*

The primary **performance objectives** of these active technologies are:
1. **Reduction of Specific Energy Consumption** (measured in **kWh/mВІ/year** or **kW/ton**).
2. **Improvement of Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of chillers and heat pumps.
3. **Compliance with ASHRAE Standard 90.1** and the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** of India.
4. **Demand Response** capability to interact with smart grids.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” "The Adaptive Body"

Imagine your body as a building:
- When you walk into a hot room, your body does **not** keep generating the same amount of sweat regardless of the temperature. Instead, it **actively** opens pores, increases blood flow to the skin, and starts sweating вҖ” using energy to cool down **only when needed**.
- The **sweating glands** are like an **active chilled beam** or **evaporative cooler** вҖ” they turn on and use energy *only* when necessary.
- The **muscles shivering** when cold are like a **variable speed heat pump** вҖ” they ramp up to generate heat precisely when and where it is required.

In a building without active technologies, the HVAC system behaves like a person who, whether mildly warm or freezing, maintains the **same constant sweat rate or shivering rate** вҖ” wasting huge amounts of energy. **Active technologies make the building "sweat smarter."**

### 1.3 Classification of Active Technologies

| Category | Example Technologies | Control Input |
|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Flow Control** | VAV (Variable Air Volume), VFDs on fans/pumps | 0вҖ“10 V DC or 4вҖ“20 mA analog signal |
| **Ventilation Control** | DCV (Demand Controlled Ventilation), Economizers | COвӮӮ sensor, enthalpy sensor |
| **Heat/Mass Exchange** | Energy Recovery Ventilators (ERV), Heat Pipes | Differential pressure, temperature |
| **Thermal Storage** | Ice-on-Coil TES, Chilled Water TES | Time-of-day scheduling |
| **Renewable Integration** | Geothermal Heat Pumps, Solar Cooling | Weather data, irradiance sensor |
| **Smart Distribution** | Active Chilled Beams, Radiant Ceiling Panels | Zone thermostat, occupancy sensor |
| **Refrigerant Cycle Enhancement** | Inverter Compressors, Magnetic Bearing Chillers | Load-based variable speed drive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 вҖ” Module 3):** The course outcome *CO3* of PEEIT526 mandates that the student must be able to *"illustrate the application of active control technologies to enhance the operational efficiency of HVAC subsystems."* Therefore, mastering this topic is essential for the **Part A (3-mark) short questions** and the **Part B (14-mark) descriptive questions**.

### 1.4 Performance Metrics вҖ” The "Killer" Numbers

| Metric | Symbol | Formula | Target Value (Green Building) |
|--------|--------|---------|------------------------------|
| Coefficient of Performance | COP | $COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | $\geq 4.0$ |
| Energy Efficiency Ratio | EER | $EER = \frac{Q_{BTU/hr}}{W_{Watts}}$ | $\geq 11.0$ |
| Seasonal EER | SEER | $SEER = \frac{\sum Q_{BTU}}{\sum W_{Wh}}$ | $\geq 14.0$ |
| Integrated Part Load Value | IPLV | $IPLV = 0.01A + 0.42B + 0.45C + 0.12D$ | $\geq 10.0$ |
| Specific Power Consumption | SPC | $SPC = \frac{kW}{TR}$ | $\leq 0.8$ |
| Heating Seasonal Performance Factor | HSPF | $HSPF = \frac{Q_{heat\_output}}{W_{input}}$ | $\geq 8.0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR) = 12,000 BTU/hr = 3.517 kW of cooling capacity.** This is a constant the examiner can assume you know, but stating it explicitly earns you a **"good impression" mark** in board valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Each Major Active Technology

#### 2.1.1 Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on Fans and Pumps

**Operational Concept:**
A VFD is a power-electronic device that varies the frequency and voltage supplied to an AC induction motor. The fundamental physics is governed by the **Affinity Laws** for centrifugal equipment.

**Why it saves energy:**
In a conventional constant-volume system, the fan runs at full speed even when the zone is only partially loaded. Throttling a damper to reduce flow wastes energy as **pressure drop across the damper**. A VFD reduces motor speed directly, which is far more efficient.

**How it works (Step-by-Step):**
1. The BMS receives zone temperature from a **temperature sensor (Pt100 / 10k NTC thermistor)**.
2. The PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller in the BMS calculates the error between setpoint and measured value.
3. The controller outputs a **0вҖ“10 V analog signal** (or **4вҖ“20 mA current loop**) to the VFD.
4. The VFD rectifies the AC mains to DC, then inverts it back to variable-frequency AC using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** via IGBTs (Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistors).
5. The motor speed changes, modulating the airflow or water flow.

#### 2.1.2 Variable Air Volume (VAV) Systems

**Operational Concept:**
Unlike CAV (Constant Air Volume) systems that vary temperature at constant flow, **VAV systems vary the airflow rate** at a constant supply air temperature. This is the single most common active technology in commercial buildings.

**Key Components:**
- **VAV Box (Terminal Unit):** Contains a damper actuator, flow sensor (differential pressure across a Venturi or Pitot tube), and a controller.
- **DDC Controller:** Executes the **Pressure-Reset (Static Pressure Reset)** logic.
- **Duct Static Pressure Sensor:** Located at 2/3rd length of the longest duct run.

**The "Critical Zone Reset" Logic:**
The VAV controller monitors the **most-open damper** position. If all dampers are <90% open, the BMS reduces the static pressure setpoint, which slows down the fan via the VFD. This is the **fan energy optimization (FEO)** algorithm вҖ” a guaranteed KTU favorite.

#### 2.1.3 Economizers (Air-Side)

**Operational Concept:**
