An economizer is an **automatic damper assembly** that introduces **outdoor air (OA)** into the building when the outdoor enthalpy or temperature is favorable, allowing the chiller to be turned off or unloaded.

**Two Control Strategies:**
1. **Dry-Bulb Temperature Economizer:** Compares outdoor dry-bulb temperature to a **changeover setpoint** (e.g., 13В°C). If OA temp < return air temp, the OA damper opens.
2. **Enthalpy Economizer:** Compares outdoor enthalpy (h_out) to return air enthalpy (h_ret). If h_out < h_ret, the damper opens. **Enthalpy is preferred** because it accounts for humidity, which dry-bulb control ignores.

**The Enthalpy Equation (Psychrometrics):**
$$h = 1.006 \cdot T + W \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T)$$

where $h$ is in **kJ/kg of dry air**, $T$ is in **В°C**, and $W$ is the **humidity ratio** in kg water vapor per kg dry air.

#### 2.1.4 Demand Controlled Ventilation (DCV) using COвӮӮ Sensors

**Operational Concept:**
ASHRAE Standard 62.1 specifies minimum outdoor air requirements based on **occupant density** (cfm/person) and **floor area** (cfm/ftВІ). In a conference room, occupancy varies from 5 to 200 people. DCV modulates the OA damper to deliver **only the necessary ventilation** based on real-time COвӮӮ concentration.

**The 700 ppm Rule:**
- **COвӮӮ setpoint:** Typically **700 ppm above outdoor ambient** (which is ~400 ppm), so the indoor setpoint is **~1,100 ppm** at maximum occupancy.
- **Sensor:** Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) COвӮӮ sensor with Вұ50 ppm accuracy.
- **Signal:** 0вҖ“10 VDC proportional output to the BMS.

#### 2.1.5 Energy Recovery Ventilators (ERV) and Heat Recovery Ventilators (HRV)

**Operational Concept:**
When buildings are tight (low infiltration), they need 100% OA for IAQ, but conditioning that OA is energy-intensive. ERVs/HRVs transfer heat (and sometimes moisture) between the **outgoing exhaust air** and the **incoming OA**, preconditioning the OA before it reaches the cooling/heating coil.

**Key Parameter: Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{in} - T_{OA}}{T_{exhaust} - T_{OA}}$$

A typical **cross-flow plate heat exchanger ERV** has $\varepsilon \approx 0.5$ to $0.75$. A **run-around coil loop** or **heat pipe** has $\varepsilon \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$.

#### 2.1.6 Active Chilled Beams (ACB)

**Operational Concept:**
A linear (or radial) diffuser integrated with a **chilled water coil**. The BMS modulates the **2-way or 3-way control valve** to vary chilled water flow into the beam, providing **sensible cooling only** (dehumidification must occur centrally).

**Why it is "active":** It uses a pump and a control valve (energy input) to deliver cooling. The "passive" version uses natural convection only.

#### 2.1.7 Inverter-Driven Variable Speed Chillers

**Operational Concept:**
The compressor speed is varied (instead of on/off cycling) to match the **building load profile**. Since chillers spend ~80% of their operating life at part-load, and since compressor power is roughly proportional to the cube of speed, even small speed reductions yield massive savings.

**IPLV (Integrated Part Load Value) вҖ” The Critical KTU Formula:**
$$IPLV = 0.01 \cdot EER_{100\%} + 0.42 \cdot EER_{75\%} + 0.45 \cdot EER_{50\%} + 0.12 \cdot EER_{25\%}$$

The coefficients (0.01, 0.42, 0.45, 0.12) are the **standard bin weights** defined by **AHRI Standard 550/590** representing the probability of operation at each load point.

#### 2.1.8 Geothermal (Ground Source) Heat Pumps (GSHP)

**Operational Concept:**
Uses the earth as a **heat source (in heating mode)** or **heat sink (in cooling mode)** via a closed-loop heat exchanger buried 1.5вҖ“3 m deep. The ground temperature is nearly constant year-round (15вҖ“25В°C), giving a **COP of 4.0вҖ“5.5** vs. 2.8вҖ“3.5 for air-source units.

#### 2.1.9 Thermal Energy Storage (TES)

**Operational Concept:**
Chillers generate "ice" (or chilled water) at **night** when electricity is cheap (off-peak) and use the stored cooling **during the day** to offset chiller operation, shifting the load off-peak. This is **active** because pumps, chillers, and controls are involved.

**Storage Capacity Formula:**
$$Q_{stored} = m_{water} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

For **Ice Storage**: $Q_{stored} = m_{ice} \cdot L_f$ (latent heat of fusion, $L_f = 334 \text{ kJ/kg}$).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | Dimensionless | Chiller, Heat Pump efficiency |
| 2 | $EER = 3.412 \cdot COP$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | BTU/Wh | Chiller rating |
| 3 | $SEER = \frac{\sum BTU_{cool}}{\sum Wh_{input}}$ | Seasonal EER | BTU/Wh | AC unit seasonal rating |
| 4 | $IPLV = 0.01A + 0.42B + 0.45C + 0.12D$ | Integrated Part Load Value | BTU/Wh | Chiller part-load evaluation |
| 5 | $kW/ton = \frac{P_{input,kW}}{Capacity_{TR}}$ | Specific Power | kW/TR | Chiller efficiency |
| 6 | $P_{fan} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta_{fan} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | Fan Power | kW | Fan energy calculation |
| 7 | $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity Law (Flow) | вҖ” | VFD speed-flow relation |
| 8 | $\frac{\Delta P_1}{\Delta P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity Law (Pressure) | вҖ” | VFD pressure relation |
| 9 | $\frac{HP_1}{HP_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity Law (Power) | вҖ” | VFD energy savings |
| 10 | $h = 1.006T + W(2501 + 1.805T)$ | Moist Air Enthalpy | kJ/kg_da | Economizer control |
| 11 | $\varepsilon = \frac{T_{supply,out} - T_{OA}}{T_{exhaust} - T_{OA}}$ | Heat Exchanger Effectiveness | dimensionless | ERV/HRV rating |
| 12 | $Q_{sensible} = 1.08 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible Heat Gain | BTU/hr | Room load calculation |
| 13 | $Q_{latent} = 0.68 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent Heat Gain | BTU/hr | Humidification load |
| 14 | $OA_{cfm} = N_{people} \cdot R_p + A_{floor} \cdot R_a$ | Ventilation Rate (62.1) | CFM | DCV setpoint |
| 15 | $Savings = P_{full} - P_{reduced} = P_{full}\left[1 - \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3\right]$ | VFD Power Savings | kW | Energy audit calculation |
| 16 | $COP_{carnot} = \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}$ | Carnot (Ideal) COP | dimensionless | Theoretical maximum |
| 17 | $\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{carnot}}$ | Second-Law Efficiency | % | Thermodynamic perfection |
| 18 | $N_{TU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless | Heat exchanger design |
| 19 | $Q_{tes} = V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible TES Capacity | kJ | Water storage tank |
| 20 | $Q_{tes,latent} = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent TES Capacity | kJ | Ice storage tank |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** The constants **$1.08$** and **$0.68$** in the sensible and latent heat formulas come from the product of air density ($\rho = 0.075 \text{ lb/ft}^3$) and specific heat ($c_p = 0.24 \text{ BTU/lbВ·В°F}$), multiplied by the time conversion factor (60 min/hr) to give **1.08** when CFM Г— О”T is in BTU/hr. Similarly, **0.68 = 0.075 Г— 1061 Г— 60 / 7000** (latent heat of vaporization in BTU/lb). **The examiner will give you full credit only if you show the constant derivation.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Active technologies are the cornerstone of:
- **LEED v4.1** certification (Energy & Atmosphere credits).
- **IGBC (Indian Green Building Council) Green Buildings** rating.
- **Net-Zero Energy Buildings (NZEB)** where annual energy generation equals consumption.
- **Smart Cities Mission** of India, where 100+ cities mandate ECBC compliance.
- **Data Centers** where PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) targets demand continuous VFD and economizer operation.

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to **calculate** energy savings, **select** appropriate technologies, and **integrate** them with the BAS control loops.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: VFD Energy Savings Using the Affinity Law (Cubic Power Relationship)

**Problem Statement:**
A building's supply fan operates at a constant volume of **40,000 CFM** against a static pressure of **2.5 inches w.g.** The fan motor is rated at **25 HP** with a combined fan+motor efficiency of **70%**. The VAV system reduces the airflow to **60% of design** during part-load. Calculate the power consumption at the reduced speed and the **annual energy savings** in kWh, assuming **3,000 hours/year** of part-load operation.

**Step 1: State the Affinity Laws explicitly.**
For a centrifugal fan, with impeller diameter held constant:
$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$
$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$
$$\frac{Power_2}{Power_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

**Step 2: Define the speed ratio from the airflow ratio.**
Since airflow is reduced to 60% of design:
$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 0.60$$

**Step 3: Calculate the new (reduced) power as a fraction of full power.**
$$\frac{Power_2}{Power_1} = (0.60)^3$$

Let us compute $(0.60)^3$ explicitly:
$$(0.60)^2 = 0.36$$
$$(0.60)^3 = 0.36 \times 0.60 = 0.216$$

So:
$$Power_2 = 0.216 \times Power_1$$

**Step 4: Compute the full-load power in kW.**
The motor is rated at 25 HP. Converting HP to kW using the standard conversion **1 HP = 0.7457 kW**:
$$P_{full} = 25 \times 0.7457 = 18.6425 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Compute the actual electrical input power considering efficiency.**
Electrical input power = Shaft power / Efficiency:
$$P_{electrical,1} = \frac{18.6425}{0.70} = 26.632 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Compute the reduced electrical input power at 60% airflow.**
$$P_{electrical,2} = 0.216 \times 26.632 = 5.753 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7: Compute the power savings in kW.**
$$\Delta P = P_{electrical,1} - P_{electrical,2} = 26.632 - 5.753 = 20.879 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 8: Compute the annual energy savings.**
$$E_{savings} = \Delta P \times t = 20.879 \text{ kW} \times 3000 \text{ hr/year}$$
$$E_{savings} = 62,637 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 9: Compute the percentage savings.**
$$\% Savings = \frac{\Delta P}{P_{electrical,1}} \times 100 = \frac{20.879}{26.632} \times 100 = 78.4\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the "Cubic Law Surprise":** Reducing airflow by 40% (from 100% to 60%) saves **78.4% of the power**. This is the single most important insight in HVAC energy engineering, and the KTU examiner will test it.

**Step 10: Compute the static pressure at the new speed (for completeness).**
$$\Delta P_2 = (0.60)^2 \times 2.5 = 0.36 \times 2.5 = 0.9 \text{ inches w.g.}$$

**Final Answer Summary Box:**
- Reduced power: **5.753 kW**
- Power savings: **20.879 kW**
- Annual energy savings: **62,637 kWh**
- Percentage savings: **78.4%**

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Enthalpy Economizer Energy Savings

**Problem Statement:**
A 5,000 mВІ office building has a CAV system with **20,000 CFM of supply air** and **8,000 CFM of minimum OA** (40% OA ratio). The chilled water coil currently cools the mixed air from 24В°C to 13В°C. With an **enthalpy economizer**, the BMS allows 100% OA when outdoor enthalpy is favorable. The chiller has an EER of 10.0. Calculate the **chiller energy savings** if the economizer operates for **1,800 hours/year**.

**Step 1: Compute the air-mass flow rate.**
The standard air density is $\rho = 1.204 \text{ kg/m}^3$. To convert CFM to mВі/s:
$$Q = 20{,}000 \text{ CFM} \times \frac{0.0004719 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}{\text{CFM}} = 9.439 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Wait, the conversion factor is: 1 CFM = 0.0004719 mВі/s. Let me verify:
1 CFM = 1 ftВі/min = (0.3048)Ві mВі / 60 s = 0.02832 / 60 = 0.000472 mВі/s. вң“

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 1.204 \times 9.439 = 11.365 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the coil cooling load (without economizer).**
The supply air is cooled from 24В°C to 13В°C:
$$Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$
$$c_p \text{ of air} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kgВ·В°C}$$
$$Q_{cool} = 11.365 \times 1.005 \times (24 - 13) = 11.365 \times 1.005 \times 11$$
$$Q_{cool} = 125.64 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Tons of Refrigeration.**
$$Q_{cool,TR} = \frac{125.64}{3.517} = 35.72 \text{ TR}$$

**Step 4: Compute the chiller electrical input power.**
With EER = 10.0 BTU/Wh, and 1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr:
$$P_{chiller} = \frac{35.72 \text{ TR} \times 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr/TR}}{10.0 \text{ BTU/Wh}} = \frac{428{,}640}{10.0} \text{ W} = 42{,}864 \text{ W} = 42.864 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Compute the energy savings during economizer operation.**
When the economizer is fully open, the chiller is **turned off** (or unloaded to minimum), so the savings equal the **full chiller power**:
$$E_{savings} = P_{chiller} \times t = 42.864 \text{ kW} \times 1800 \text{ hr}$$
$$E_{savings} = 77{,}155 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 6: Compute the money saved at вӮ№8/kWh (typical commercial tariff in Kerala).**
$$\text{Cost Savings} = 77{,}155 \times 8 = \text{вӮ№}6,17,240 \text{ per year}$$

**Final Answer Summary Box:**
- Chiller load (no economizer): **125.64 kW** (= 35.72 TR)
- Chiller electrical input: **42.864 kW**
- Annual energy savings: **77,155 kWh**
- Annual monetary savings: **вӮ№6,17,240**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** This is why ECBC mandates economizers for all AHUs > 7.5 TR. The payback period is typically **< 1.5 years** for the additional damper, actuator, and control logic.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: COвӮӮ-based DCV Ventilation Savings

**Problem Statement:**
A 200-person conference room has an ASHRAE 62.1 OA requirement of **20 CFM/person** = **4,000 CFM** at design occupancy. A COвӮӮ sensor-based DCV system reduces average OA to **2,400 CFM** (60% of design) because occupancy averages 60%. The fan power is **6.0 kW** at design flow. Calculate the **annual fan energy savings** assuming **2,500 hours/year** of operation and вӮ№9/kWh.

**Step 1: Compute the new flow ratio.**
$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{2400}{4000} = 0.60$$

**Step 2: Apply the cubic affinity law for fan power.**
$$P_2 = P_1 \times (0.60)^3 = 6.0 \times 0.216 = 1.296 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Compute the power savings.**
$$\Delta P = 6.0 - 1.296 = 4.704 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Compute the annual energy savings.**
$$E_{savings} = 4.704 \times 2500 = 11{,}760 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5: Compute the annual cost savings.**
$$Cost = 11{,}760 \times 9 = \text{вӮ№}1,05{,}840$$

**Step 6: Compute the IAQ compliance check.**
Setpoint for COвӮӮ: Outdoor (~400 ppm) + 700 ppm offset = **1,100 ppm**. The sensor reading at 60% OA is ~750 ppm, well within ASHRAE's 1,000 ppm + outdoor limit.

**Final Answer Summary Box:**
- Fan power (reduced): **1.296 kW**
- Annual energy savings: **11,760 kWh**
- Annual monetary savings: **вӮ№1,05,840**
- IAQ: **Compliant** (COвӮӮ ~750 ppm vs. 1,100 ppm limit)

### 4.2 Control Logic Flow: Economizer Decision Algorithm

The following **flowchart** captures the **decision tree** implemented in the BMS controller for the air-side economizer logic, which is a guaranteed exam topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Start of Each Control Cycle Every 30 sec] --> N1[Read Outdoor Air Temperature T_OA]
    N1 --> N2[Read Outdoor Humidity RH_OA]
    N2 --> N3[Calculate Outdoor Enthalpy h_OA]
    N3 --> N4[Read Return Air Temperature T_RA]
    N4 --> N5[Read Return Air Humidity RH_RA]
    N5 --> N6[Calculate Return Enthalpy h_RA]
    N6 --> N7{Is h_OA less than h_RA?}
    N7 -- Yes --> N8{Is h_OA less than 28 kiloJoule per kg?}
    N8 -- Yes --> N9[Set OA Damper to 100 percent Open Modulating]
    N8 -- No --> N10[Set OA Damper to Minimum Position 40 percent]
    N7 -- No --> N10
    N9 --> N11[Enable Free Cooling Mode Disable Chiller]
    N10 --> N12[Disable Free Cooling Enable Mechanical Cooling]
    N11 --> N13[End of Cycle Wait 30 sec]
    N12 --> N13
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A вҖ” 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024]** *Define "active technology" in the context of HVAC systems. Differentiate it from passive technology with one suitable example of each.*

**Model Answer:**
An **active technology** in HVAC is an engineered device that consumes **external energy (electrical, mechanical, or thermal)** and is controlled by the BAS to dynamically vary its output in response to real-time building load. **Example:** Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) on a supply fan, which reduces motor speed based on duct static pressure feedback.

A **passive technology** does not consume external operational energy; it works solely on natural environmental forces (wind, solar, buoyancy). **Example:** A Trombe wall, which uses solar radiation and natural convection to heat a building.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]** *State the Fan Affinity Laws. How are they used to justify the energy-saving potential of VFDs?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Fan Affinity Laws** (for a constant impeller diameter) are:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \quad \frac{Power_2}{Power_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

Since the **power varies with the cube of the speed ratio**, reducing motor speed to 50% of full speed reduces power consumption to **12.5%** of full-load power вҖ” yielding an energy saving of **87.5%**. This cubic relationship is the **theoretical foundation** for justifying VFD installation in AHUs, chilled water pumps, and cooling tower fans. **(3 marks вҖ” full credit requires stating all three laws and one numerical example.)**

---

### 5.2 PART B вҖ” 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> ### **Question A (Choice 1) вҖ” [KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the working principle of a **Variable Air Volume (VAV) system** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages over a Constant Air Volume (CAV) system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A VAV system serves a 1,000 mВІ office zone. The design supply airflow is 12,000 CFM at a static pressure of 1.8 inches w.g. Using the Affinity Laws, compute the fan power and the energy savings (in kWh/year) if the VAV reduces airflow to 50% of design for 2,000 hours/year. Assume a combined fan and motor efficiency of 65%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

1. **Definition of VAV:** A VAV system varies the **airflow rate** delivered to each zone in response to the zone thermostat, while keeping the **supply air temperature constant**. In contrast, a CAV system delivers a constant airflow and varies the supply air temperature.

2. **Components:**
   - **VAV Terminal Box** (with damper actuator and flow sensor).
   - **DDC Controller** for each zone.
   - **Duct Static Pressure Sensor (SPS)** at 2/3 length of the longest duct.
   - **VFD** on the supply fan.

3. **Working Principle (Step-by-Step):**
   - Step 1: The **zone thermostat** measures the room temperature and sends a setpoint deviation to the VAV DDC controller.
   - Step 2: The DDC controller **modulates the damper** in the VAV box via the actuator, increasing or decreasing the airflow to that zone.
   - Step 3: The **SPS** measures the duct static pressure downstream.
   - Step 4: The BMS implements the **"Trim & Respond"** or **"Pressure Reset"** algorithm: it continuously searches for the **most-open damper**. If all dampers are < 90% open, the BMS **reduces the static pressure setpoint**, which commands the VFD to **slow down the fan**.
   - Step 5: This dynamic reset ensures the fan only supplies the minimum pressure needed to satisfy the most-loaded zone, eliminating waste.

4. **Advantages over CAV:**
   - **Energy savings of 30вҖ“70%** in fan power (cubic law advantage).
   - **Better zone-level comfort control** (individual zone temperature).
   - **Lower noise** at part-load (reduced air velocity).
   - **Reduced chiller load** in many cases.
   - **Compatible with DCV** for further savings.

5. **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 above.)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:**
- Design airflow: $Q_1 = 12{,}000 \text{ CFM}$
- Design static pressure: $\Delta P_1 = 1.8 \text{ inches w.g.}$
- Reduced airflow: $Q_2 = 0.50 \times 12{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ CFM}$
- Part-load hours: $t = 2{,}000 \text{ hr/year}$
- Combined efficiency: $\eta = 0.65$

**Step 1: Compute the new static pressure using the Affinity Law.**
$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^2 = (0.50)^2 = 0.25$$
$$\Delta P_2 = 0.25 \times 1.8 = 0.45 \text{ inches w.g.}$$

**Step 2: Convert static pressure to fan power using the standard formula.**
$$P_{fan,1} = \frac{\Delta P_1 \times Q_1}{\eta \times 6356}$$

The constant **6356** is a standard conversion factor that combines unit conversions from inches w.g. to ft-lb/s and CFM to ftВі/s, with **1 in w.g. = 5.2 lb/ftВІ**.

$$P_{fan,1} = \frac{1.8 \times 12{,}000}{0.65 \times 6356} = \frac{21{,}600}{4131.4} = 5.228 \text{ HP}$$

Converting HP to kW: $5.228 \times 0.7457 = 3.899 \text{ kW}$

**Step 3: Compute the fan power at 50% airflow using the cubic law.**
$$P_{fan,2} = P_{fan,1} \times (0.50)^3 = 5.228 \times 0.125 = 0.6535 \text{ HP}$$
$$= 0.6535 \times 0.7457 = 0.4874 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Compute the power savings.**
$$\Delta P = 3.899 - 0.4874 = 3.412 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Compute the annual energy savings.**
$$E_{savings} = 3.412 \text{ kW} \times 2{,}000 \text{ hr} = 6{,}824 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Final Answer for Q.A(b):**
- Fan power at full load: **3.899 kW**
- Fan power at 50% load: **0.4874 kW**
- Annual energy savings: **6,824 kWh/year**
- % Savings: **87.5%** (cubic law)

---

> ### **Question B (Choice 2) вҖ” [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]**
> **(a)** With the help of a flowchart, explain the **working of an enthalpy-based air-side economizer** in a central AHU. State the psychrometric equation used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the operating principle, types, and energy-saving potential of **Energy Recovery Ventilators (ERV)**. A building requires 10,000 CFM of outdoor air. The outdoor air is at 35В°C dry-bulb and 28В°C wet-bulb; the exhaust air is at 24В°C dry-bulb and 18В°C wet-bulb. If the ERV has an effectiveness of 65%, calculate the **temperature of the outdoor air after the ERV** and the **annual cooling energy saved** if the ERV operates 4,000 hours/year. Take chiller EER = 11.0. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

1. **Definition:** An enthalpy economizer modulates the outdoor air (OA) and return air (RA) dampers to use **100% outdoor air** for "free cooling" whenever the **enthalpy of outdoor air is less than the enthalpy of return air**. Enthalpy control is preferred over dry-bulb control because it accounts for **latent heat (humidity)**.

2. **Psychrometric Equation:**
$$h = 1.006 \cdot T + W \cdot (2501 + 1.805 \cdot T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg dry air]}$$

where $T$ is in В°C and $W$ is the humidity ratio (kg water / kg dry air).

3. **Flowchart (Refer to Section 4.2 above for the complete flow diagram):**
   - Read $T_{OA}$, $RH_{OA}$ вҶ’ Compute $h_{OA}$.
   - Read $T_{RA}$, $RH_{RA}$ вҶ’ Compute $h_{RA}$.
   - If $h_{OA} < h_{RA}$ AND $h_{OA} < 28$ kJ/kg вҶ’ Open OA damper to 100%, disable chiller.
   - Otherwise вҶ’ Set OA to minimum (typically 20вҖ“40%), enable mechanical cooling.

4. **Hardware:** Differential enthalpy sensor (two probe types in one unit), motorized modulating dampers (0вҖ“10 V control), and the BMS PID loop.

5. **Energy Savings:** Typically 15вҖ“40% of annual chiller energy in mixed-humid climates like Kerala.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Given:**
- OA flow: $Q_{OA} = 10{,}000 \text{ CFM} = 4.719 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $T_{OA} = 35В°\text{C}$, $T_{exhaust} = 24В°\text{C}$
- ERV effectiveness: $\varepsilon = 0.65$
- Operating hours: $t = 4{,}000 \text{ hr/year}$
- Chiller EER = 11.0

**Step 1: Compute the supply air temperature after the ERV using the effectiveness formula.**
$$T_{supply,out} = T_{OA} + \varepsilon \cdot (T_{exhaust} - T_{OA})$$
$$T_{supply,out} = 35 + 0.65 \times (24 - 35) = 35 + 0.65 \times (-11) = 35 - 7.15$$
$$T_{supply,out} = 27.85В°\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Compute the temperature reduction achieved by the ERV.**
$$\Delta T_{saved} = T_{OA} - T_{supply,out} = 35 - 27.85 = 7.15В°\text{C}$$

(Without the ERV, the chiller would have to cool OA from 35В°C to 13В°C = 22В°C of cooling. With the ERV, only $13 - 27.85 = -14.85В°\text{C}$? Wait, the supply is still hotter than 13В°C, so the chiller still must cool it further. But the **work done by the chiller is reduced** by the equivalent of 7.15В°C.)

**Step 3: Compute the cooling load "saved" by the ERV (in kW).**
Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1.204 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 4.719 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.682 \text{ kg/s}$

$$Q_{saved} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{saved} = 5.682 \times 1.005 \times 7.15 = 40.84 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Tons of Refrigeration.**
$$TR_{saved} = \frac{40.84}{3.517} = 11.61 \text{ TR}$$

**Step 5: Compute the chiller electrical input equivalent.**
With EER = 11.0 BTU/Wh:
$$P_{chiller} = \frac{11.61 \times 12{,}000}{11.0} = \frac{139{,}320}{11.0} = 12{,}665 \text{ W} = 12.665 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Compute the annual energy savings.**
$$E_{savings} = 12.665 \text{ kW} \times 4{,}000 \text{ hr} = 50{,}660 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Final Answer for Q.B(b):**
- Temperature of OA after ERV: **27.85В°C**
- Temperature reduction: **7.15В°C**
- Annual energy savings: **50,660 kWh/year**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in PEEIT526 Module 3:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert HP to kW.** Use **1 HP = 0.7457 kW** explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if you write "P = 5.228 HP" as the final answer without conversion.
>
> 2. **Using the WRONG Affinity Law.** Some students apply the **square law** for power instead of the **cube law**. The power is **NВі**, not NВІ. NВІ is for **pressure**, not power.
>
> 3. **Confusing SEER and EER.** SEER is integrated over a season; EER is a single point. The ratio SEER/EER is typically 1.1 to 1.3.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units in enthalpy calculation.** Always state **"h in kJ/kg of dry air"**. The number "28" alone is meaningless; write "h < 28 kJ/kg_da".
>
> 5. **Not stating the conversion constant 6356** in fan power formulas. Examiners expect you to either derive it or state it.
>
> 6. **Omitting the block diagram in VAV/Economizer questions.** A "neat block diagram" carries **2 of the 7 marks** in part (a) of 14-mark questions. Always draw one.
>
> 7. **Confusing sensible and latent EAT (Effective Air Temperature).** For active chilled beams, the cooling is **sensible only** (no dehumidification at the beam). State this clearly to earn full credit.
>
> 8. **Ignoring part-load performance.** Never use the chiller's **full-load EER** for annual calculations; use **IPLV**. A chiller with full-load EER = 10 and IPLV = 14.5 is normal; ignoring IPLV underestimates the chiller's true efficiency.
>
> 9. **Wrong direction of effectiveness formula.** A student once wrote $\varepsilon = \frac{T_{exhaust} - T_{supply,out}}{T_{OA} - T_{supply,out}}$ вҖ” completely wrong. The numerator must always be the **temperature change of the airstream being heated/cooled**, and the denominator is the **maximum possible change** (which is the temperature difference between the two inlet streams).
>
> 10. **Forgetting to account for humidity in dry-bulb economizer logic.** In humid climates (Kerala!), a dry-bulb economizer can introduce **hot, humid air** and overload the chiller with latent load. Always use **enthalpy economizers** in coastal Indian climates.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets вҖ” Master These Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Active vs. Passive:** Active consumes external energy and is BAS-controlled; passive uses natural forces.
- [ ] **COP** = Useful cooling / Energy input (dimensionless). **EER = 3.412 Г— COP**.
- [ ] **1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr = 3.517 kW** of cooling.
- [ ] **Affinity Laws:** Flow вҲқ N, Pressure вҲқ NВІ, Power вҲқ NВі.
- [ ] **VFD Savings:** Reducing speed to 50% saves **87.5% energy** (cubic law).
- [ ] **Fan Power Formula:** $P = \frac{\Delta P \times Q}{6356 \times \eta}$ (HP, with $\Delta P$ in inches w.g., $Q$ in CFM).
- [ ] **VAV System:** Constant supply temp, variable airflow. Uses **static pressure reset** algorithm.
- [ ] **Economizer:** Modulates OA damper based on **enthalpy comparison** (preferred over dry-bulb in humid climates).
- [ ] **Enthalpy Formula:** $h = 1.006T + W(2501 + 1.805T)$ kJ/kg_da.
- [ ] **DCV:** Uses **COвӮӮ sensor (NDIR)**, setpoint typically 700 ppm above outdoor (~1,100 ppm).
- [ ] **ASHRAE 62.1 Ventilation:** $OA_{cfm} = N_p \cdot R_p + A_{floor} \cdot R_a$.
- [ ] **ERV Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = \frac{T_{supply} - T_{OA}}{T_{exhaust} - T_{OA}}$, typical $\varepsilon$ = 0.5 to 0.75.
- [ ] **Sensible Heat:** $Q_s = 1.08 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta T$ BTU/hr.
- [ ] **Latent Heat:** $Q_L = 0.68 \cdot CFM \cdot \Delta W$ BTU/hr.
- [ ] **IPLV:** $0.01A + 0.42B + 0.45C + 0.12D$ (A=100%, B=75%, C=50%, D=25% load EERs).
- [ ] **Active Chilled Beam:** Sensible cooling only; dehumidification must be central.
- [ ] **Inverter Chiller:** Variable-speed compressor; **20вҖ“35% savings** vs. fixed-speed.
- [ ] **Geothermal Heat Pump:** Uses ground at 15вҖ“25В°C; COP = 4.0вҖ“5.5.
- [ ] **TES:** Shifts chiller load to off-peak; uses **ice (L_f = 334 kJ/kg)** or chilled water.
- [ ] **VAV Box Components:** Damper actuator + flow sensor (Pitot/Venturi) + DDC controller.
- [ ] **Pressure Sensor Location:** 2/3 length of the longest duct run.
- [ ] **Kerala Climate Note:** Always prefer **enthalpy economizers** due to high humidity.
- [ ] **ECBC:** Mandates economizers for AHUs > 7.5 TR and VFDs for motors > 7.5 kW.
- [ ] **IPLV vs. Full-Load EER:** IPLV is always higher; it represents **part-load** efficiency.
- [ ] **Free Cooling:** Achieved when $T_{OA} < T_{return}$ and $h_{OA} < h_{return}$ and $h_{OA} < 28$ kJ/kg.
- [ ] **NDIR Sensor:** Non-Dispersive Infrared вҖ” the industry standard for COвӮӮ sensing.
- [ ] **Communication Protocols:** BACnet (ASHRAE 135), Modbus, LonWorks вҖ” used for BAS integration.
- [ ] **0вҖ“10 V vs. 4вҖ“20 mA:** Both are analog signaling standards; 4вҖ“20 mA is more noise-immune for long runs.
- [ ] **PWM (VFD):** Uses IGBTs at switching frequencies of 4вҖ“16 kHz.
- [ ] **Carbon Trust Standard:** 30% fan energy reduction = typical VAV retrofit outcome.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Cooling Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pre-Cooling Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pre-Cooling Strategies** in Building Automation refer to a set of intelligent HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) control methodologies wherein a building's thermal mass and indoor air temperature are deliberately cooled **prior to the onset of peak cooling demand**вҖ”typically during off-peak hours, early morning hours, or periods of lower outdoor temperatures and lower electricity tariffs.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, pre-cooling is formally defined as a **load-shifting and demand-side energy management technique** wherein the Building Management System (BMS) or Building Automation System (BAS) pre-conditions the building envelope to a temperature setpoint lower than the conventional occupied comfort range, thereby storing "coolth" in the building's thermal mass (concrete slabs, masonry walls, furniture, etc.) for gradual release during the subsequent peak-load period.

The underlying engineering principle is governed by the **Thermal Time Constant (П„)** of the building, which determines how quickly the structure loses its stored cooling capacity.

$$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{pre}$$

Where:
- $Q_{stored}$ = Thermal energy stored in the building mass (in Joules, **J**)
- $m$ = Effective thermal mass of the building structure (in **kg**)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat capacity of the building material (in **J/kgВ·K**)
- $\Delta T_{pre}$ = Pre-cooling temperature depression (in **K** or **В°C**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pre-cooling is classified under **"Energy Conservation Strategies in HVAC"** and is directly mapped to Course Outcomes (CO) emphasizing the design of energy-efficient automated building systems. It is a high-weightage topic for Part B (14-mark) questions.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Cold Water Bottle in a Thermos" Effect

Imagine you are going on a hot summer picnic. What do you do the night before?

You place a **cold water bottle inside a thermos flask**. During the day, even though the outside temperature rises, the thermos keeps the water cold for hours because the coldness was **stored in advance** inside an insulated container.

Now, replace the thermos with a **building's walls, floors, and ceilings** (which act as natural thermal storage), and the water bottle with **chilled air supplied by the HVAC system**. That is exactly what pre-cooling does!

The BMS acts as your "smart alarm clock" that:
1. Wakes the HVAC system up **before the occupants arrive**
2. Cools the building structure to a **lower-than-comfort temperature**
3. "Locks in" the coolness using the building's inherent insulation
4. Allows the structure to **gradually warm up** during the day, maintaining comfort without the HVAC working at full blast during expensive peak hours

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Takeaway:** Pre-cooling is like **paying for ice cream at yesterday's discounted price** to eat it today. You do the work when it's cheap and easy, then enjoy the benefit later when it would have been expensive.

---

## 1.3 Why Pre-Cooling? The Core Drivers

| Driver | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Peak Demand Reduction** | Shifts load from peak (high tariff) to off-peak (low tariff) hours |
| **Energy Cost Savings** | Electricity tariffs in India typically differ by **3Г— to 5Г—** between peak and off-peak hours under Time-of-Use (ToU) metering |
| **Grid Stability** | Reduces strain on the utility grid during afternoon peaks (typically **2 PM вҖ“ 6 PM**) |
| **Equipment Sizing** | Allows downsizing of chiller capacity due to lower instantaneous peak load |
| **Thermal Comfort** | Maintains stable indoor temperature without oscillations during occupied hours |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Reduces overall kWh consumption and associated COвӮӮ emissions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) Standard 90.1** and the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2017** (India) both recognize pre-cooling as a recommended energy efficiency measure under the "Load Management" category.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Pre-cooling is fundamentally an application of the **principle of thermal energy storage (TES)** combined with **predictive scheduling control**. The process can be broken down into four sequential phases:

### Phase 1: Off-Peak Charging Phase (Pre-Cooling Window)
- Time: Typically **midnight to 6:00 AM**
- The BAS activates the chiller/AHU system to cool the building
- Setpoint is lowered to **22В°C to 24В°C** (below the normal 25В°C comfort setpoint)
- Cooling energy is **stored in the thermal mass** of the structure
- Electricity cost is at its **lowest tariff rate**

### Phase 2: Drift / Free-Float Phase
- Time: Typically **6:00 AM to 9:00 AM** (pre-occupancy)
- HVAC system may be switched OFF or operate at minimum capacity
- Indoor temperature slowly rises due to heat gains from envelope, lighting, and equipment
- The rise rate is governed by the **thermal time constant (П„)** of the building

### Phase 3: Occupied Steady-State Phase
- Time: **9:00 AM to 6:00 PM**
- The HVAC system maintains the comfort setpoint with **reduced capacity** because the structure starts cool
- Peak demand from the chiller is **shifted earlier** or reduced in magnitude

### Phase 4: Recovery / Reset Phase
- Time: **6:00 PM to midnight**
- HVAC may operate in **economizer mode** using cool outdoor air
- System prepares for next day's pre-cooling cycle

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variable Definitions | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Energy Stored** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temp drop | **J** (or kWh) | Calculating pre-cooling capacity required |
| **Thermal Time Constant** | $\tau = R_{th} \cdot C_{th}$ | $R_{th}$ = thermal resistance, $C_{th}$ = thermal capacitance | **hours (h)** | Determining how long coolth is retained |
| **Heat Gain Rate** | $Q_{gain} = UA \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + Q_{internal}$ | $UA$ = overall heat transfer coefficient Г— area, $Q_{internal}$ = internal loads | **W** (Joules/sec) | Estimating drift rate during pre-cooling |
| **Cooling Load Reduction** | $\Delta Q_{peak} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{comfort} - T_{pre-cool}) / t_{drift}$ | $t_{drift}$ = duration of free-float | **W** or **kW** | Quantifying peak demand reduction |
| **Coefficient of Performance Benefit** | $COP_{effective} = COP_{rated} \cdot (1 + f_{benefit})$ | $f_{benefit}$ = improvement factor (typically **0.10 вҖ“ 0.25**) | dimensionless | Energy savings calculation |
| **Pre-Cooling Setpoint** | $T_{pre} = T_{comfort} - \Delta T_{pre}$ | $T_{comfort}$ = occupied setpoint (typically **25В°C**), $\Delta T_{pre}$ = depression (typically **2вҖ“4В°C**) | **В°C** | BAS control logic |
| **Energy Cost Savings** | $Savings = E_{shifted} \cdot (C_{peak} - C_{off-peak})$ | $E_{shifted}$ = energy shifted, $C$ = cost per kWh | **INR (вӮ№)** or **USD ($)** | Economic justification |
| **Power Reduction Ratio** | $PRR = P_{peak,baseline} - P_{peak,precool} \over P_{peak,baseline}$ | Comparison of peak power with and without pre-cooling | dimensionless | Performance metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute value notations and conditionals in the formulas above use standard mathematical symbols. The variable $UA$ refers to the product of the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** and the **envelope area (A)**, and is written without vertical pipe delimiters to preserve KTU markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pre-cooling is widely deployed in:

1. **Commercial Office Buildings** вҖ” Pre-cooling at night for daytime comfort (e.g., Infosys, TCS campuses in India)
2. **Shopping Malls** вҖ” Heavy internal loads from lighting and crowds require pre-conditioning
3. **Data Centers** вҖ” Server rooms benefit massively from pre-cooling to maintain stable 22В°C setpoints
4. **Hospitals & Laboratories** вҖ” Critical environments with strict temperature/humidity requirements
5. **Educational Institutions** вҖ” KTU-affiliated engineering colleges with seminar halls and computer centers
6. **Smart Homes with IoT** вҖ” Smart thermostats (Nest, Ecobee) use predictive pre-cooling based on occupancy learning algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Statistic:** Studies by the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India** indicate that well-implemented pre-cooling strategies can reduce peak HVAC electricity consumption by **15% to 30%** in commercial buildings, translating to annual savings of **вӮ№5 to вӮ№15 per square foot** depending on the climate zone and tariff structure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantifying the Pre-Cooling Energy Storage Capacity

### Given Scenario (Typical KTU Numerical Problem):

A reinforced concrete office floor slab has the following specifications:
- Mass: $m = 50{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- Specific heat capacity of concrete: $c_p = 880 \text{ J/kgВ·K}$
- Occupied comfort setpoint: $T_{comfort} = 25В°C$
- Pre-cooling setpoint: $T_{pre} = 22В°C$
- Pre-cooling duration: $t_{pre} = 4 \text{ hours}$
- Chiller COP: $COP = 3.5$

**Find:** (a) Thermal energy stored, (b) Electrical energy consumed by chiller, (c) Peak demand reduction

---

### Step 1: Calculate the Temperature Depression ($\Delta T_{pre}$)

$$\Delta T_{pre} = T_{comfort} - T_{pre}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta T_{pre} = 25В°C - 22В°C = 3 \text{ K}$$

> **Logic:** A temperature difference of **3 Kelvin** is identical to a difference of **3В°C** because the temperature *interval* is the same on both scales. The Kelvin and Celsius scales only differ in their zero-point reference.

---

### Step 2: Calculate the Thermal Energy Stored ($Q_{stored}$)

$$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{pre}$$

Substituting:

$$Q_{stored} = 50{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 880 \text{ J/kgВ·K} \times 3 \text{ K}$$

Performing the multiplication step-by-step:

$$Q_{stored} = 50{,}000 \times 880 \times 3$$

$$Q_{stored} = 50{,}000 \times 2{,}640$$

$$Q_{stored} = 132{,}000{,}000 \text{ J}$$

Converting to **kilowatt-hours** (since $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$):

$$Q_{stored} = \frac{132{,}000{,}000}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 36.67 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **useful cooling energy** that has been "banked" in the building's thermal mass. The occupants will benefit from this stored coolth over the next several hours without requiring the chiller to run at full capacity.

---

### Step 3: Calculate the Electrical Energy Consumed by the Chiller

The chiller has a Coefficient of Performance (COP) of 3.5, meaning for every **1 kWh of electricity**, it produces **3.5 kWh of cooling**.

$$E_{electrical} = \frac{Q_{stored}}{COP}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{electrical} = \frac{36.67 \text{ kWh}}{3.5} = 10.48 \text{ kWh}$$

> **Logic:** To store 36.67 kWh of coolth, the chiller needs to consume only 10.48 kWh of electricity because the heat pump is thermodynamically efficient. This is the energy "investment" during off-peak hours.

---

### Step 4: Calculate the Peak Demand Reduction

Assume the building's heat gain rate during peak hours is:

$$Q_{gain} = UA \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + Q_{internal} = 30 \text{ kW}$$

If the pre-cooling stored **36.67 kWh** of coolth, and the drift phase lasts for:

$$t_{drift} = 4 \text{ hours}$$

Then the **effective cooling contribution** from pre-cooling is:

$$P_{pre-cool} = \frac{Q_{stored}}{t_{drift}}$$

$$P_{pre-cool} = \frac{36.67 \text{ kWh}}{4 \text{ h}} = 9.17 \text{ kW}$$

The peak demand reduction ratio:

$$PRR = \frac{P_{pre-cool}}{Q_{gain}} = \frac{9.17}{30} = 0.306 = 30.6\%$$

> **Logic:** The chiller only needs to provide **30 - 9.17 = 20.83 kW** during peak hours instead of the full 30 kW, representing a **30.6% reduction in peak electrical demand** from the HVAC system.

---

### Step 5: Economic Analysis

Assume peak tariff: $C_{peak} = вӮ№12/\text{kWh}$
Assume off-peak tariff: $C_{off-peak} = вӮ№5/\text{kWh}$

**Cost WITH pre-cooling (off-peak):**
$$Cost_{pre} = 10.48 \text{ kWh} \times вӮ№5 = вӮ№52.40$$

**Cost WITHOUT pre-cooling (running during peak to deliver 36.67 kWh):**
$$E_{no-pre} = \frac{36.67}{3.5} = 10.48 \text{ kWh (same energy, but at peak rate)}$$
$$Cost_{no-pre} = 10.48 \text{ kWh} \times вӮ№12 = вӮ№125.76$$

**Daily savings:**
$$Savings = вӮ№125.76 - вӮ№52.40 = вӮ№73.36$$

> **Logic:** Pre-cooling is economically attractive *primarily* when there is a significant **tariff differential** between peak and off-peak hours. The bigger the differential, the larger the savings.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pre-Cooling vs. Conventional Cooling

| Parameter | Conventional Cooling | Pre-Cooling Strategy | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chiller Operating Time** | 9 AM вҖ“ 6 PM (peak) | 12 AM вҖ“ 6 AM (off-peak) | Shifts from peak |
| **Peak Electrical Demand** | **Full load** (~30 kW) | **Partial load** (~20.8 kW) | **30.6% reduction** |
| **Electricity Cost per kWh** | **вӮ№12/kWh** (peak) | **вӮ№5/kWh** (off-peak) | **58% cheaper** |
| **Indoor Temperature Stability** | Fluctuates Вұ2В°C | Stable Вұ0.5В°C | **4Г— more stable** |
| **Compressor Cycling** | Frequent start/stop | Smooth ramp-up/ramp-down | Longer equipment life |
| **Carbon Footprint** | Higher (peak = coal-heavy grid) | Lower (off-peak = efficient baseload) | **10вҖ“20% reduction** |
| **BAS Complexity** | Simple thermostat | Predictive algorithm + sensors | Higher initial complexity |
| **Initial Investment** | Lower | Higher (controls, sensors) | **ROI in 2вҖ“4 years** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When answering exam questions, always include the **quantitative justification** (with units) and the **qualitative benefits** in your answer. KTU examiners award marks for both technical accuracy and engineering judgment.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pre-Cooling Operational Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete control logic** of a BAS-implemented pre-cooling strategy, including decision nodes, sensor inputs, and actuator outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Day 12:00 AM] --> B{BAS Scheduler Active?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Read Outdoor Temp Sensor]
    B -->|No| Z[System Idle]
    C --> D{Outdoor Temp less than 28C?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Activate Economizer Mode Fresh Air Cooling]
    D -->|No| F[Activate Chiller in Pre-Cool Mode]
    E --> G[Cool Building to Tpre 22C]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Monitor Indoor Temp via BMS}
    H -->|Tin greater than 22C| F
    H -->|Tin less than or equal to 22C| I[Cut Off Chiller]
    I --> J[Enter Drift Phase 6 AM to 9 AM]
    J --> K[Indoor Temp Slowly Rises]
    K --> L{Occupancy Detected by PIR Sensor?}
    L -->|No| K
    L -->|Yes| M[Enter Occupied Phase 9 AM Onwards]
    M --> N[Maintain Tcomfort 25C]
    N --> O{Time greater than 6 PM?}
    O -->|No| N
    O -->|Yes| P[System Reset for Next Cycle]
    P --> A
```

**Diagram Interpretation Notes:**
- Node **A** represents the cycle start (typically midnight)
- Decision nodes (diamonds) implement the **conditional control logic** of the BAS
- The loop between nodes **F**, **G**, and **H** represents the **closed-loop feedback control** during pre-cooling
- Node **K** represents the natural **thermal drift** governed by the building's time constant

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pre-Cooling System

The following diagram shows the **functional interaction** between the major hardware and software components of a pre-cooling system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSORS[Sensor Layer]
        S1[Outdoor Air Temp Sensor]
        S2[Indoor Air Temp Sensor]
        S3[Humidity Sensor]
        S4[Occupancy PIR Sensor]
        S5[Electricity Tariff Meter]
    end

    subgraph BAS[Building Automation System Layer]
        CPU[BAS Controller CPU]
        SCHED[Scheduling Algorithm]
        PRED[Predictive Load Model]
        DB[Historical Data Storage]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATORS[Actuator Layer]
        A1[Chiller ON OFF Control]
        A2[AHU Damper Modulation]
        A3[Smart Thermostat Setpoint]
        A4[Economizer Damper]
    end

    subgraph LOAD[Building Thermal Mass]
        TM[Concrete Slabs Walls Furniture]
    end

    S1 --> CPU
    S2 --> CPU
    S3 --> CPU
    S4 --> CPU
    S5 --> CPU
    CPU --> SCHED
    CPU --> PRED
    SCHED --> DB
    PRED --> DB
    CPU --> A1
    CPU --> A2
    CPU --> A3
    CPU --> A4
    A1 --> TM
    A2 --> TM
    A3 --> TM
    A4 --> TM
    TM -.Feedback.-> S2
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Sensor Layer** | Collects real-time data on temperature, humidity, occupancy, and tariff |
| **BAS Layer** | The "brain" that processes data using scheduling and predictive algorithms |
| **Actuator Layer** | Physical devices that execute the cooling commands |
| **Building Thermal Mass** | The "thermal battery" that stores the pre-cooling energy |
| **Dashed Feedback Line** | The closed-loop feedback that enables continuous regulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **dashed feedback line from the building thermal mass back to the indoor temperature sensor** is the most critical aspect of pre-cooling control. Without this feedback, the system would be operating in **open-loop mode**, which is prone to over-cooling (wasting energy) or under-cooling (causing discomfort). The **closed-loop PID controller** inside the BAS maintains the setpoint within Вұ0.5В°C accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define pre-cooling in the context of building automation. List any two benefits of implementing a pre-cooling strategy in a commercial building.**

**Model Answer:**

Pre-cooling is an **energy management technique** in Building Automation Systems (BAS) where the HVAC system is operated during off-peak hours (typically late night or early morning) to cool the building structure and indoor air to a temperature lower than the normal occupied comfort setpoint. This stored "coolth" in the building's thermal mass is then released gradually during the subsequent peak-load period, reducing the chiller's instantaneous power demand.

**Two benefits:**
1. **Peak Demand Reduction** вҖ” Reduces the electrical load during expensive peak tariff hours (typically 2 PM вҖ“ 6 PM), lowering electricity bills by **15% to 30%**.
2. **Improved Thermal Comfort** вҖ” The indoor temperature remains more stable throughout the day because the thermal mass acts as a buffer, reducing temperature swings.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of the building's thermal mass in a pre-cooling strategy? Why is the pre-cooling setpoint typically 2В°C to 4В°C lower than the occupied comfort setpoint?**

**Model Answer:**

The building's **thermal mass** (concrete slabs, brick walls, tile floors, and even furniture) acts as a **thermal energy storage medium**. When cooled during the pre-cooling phase, it stores cooling energy in the form of reduced internal temperature. This stored energy is then released slowly during the day as the building absorbs heat from solar radiation, occupants, lighting, and equipment, thereby reducing the HVAC's active cooling requirement.

The pre-cooling setpoint is maintained **2В°C to 4В°C below the occupied comfort setpoint** for the following reasons:
1. **Condensation Prevention** вҖ” A depression greater than 4В°C may cause surface condensation on walls and windows, leading to mold growth and material degradation.
2. **Occupant Comfort at Transition** вҖ” By the time occupants arrive, the temperature has naturally drifted up to near-comfort levels, avoiding the "too cold" sensation.
3. **Energy Optimization** вҖ” A 2В°C to 4В°C depression provides a good balance between stored coolth and chiller energy expenditure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) вҖ” Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question A:**
**(a)** Explain the fundamental principle of pre-cooling strategy in building automation with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the BAS controller, sensors, and actuators in implementing the pre-cooling logic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial office building has a concrete floor slab of mass **80,000 kg** with specific heat capacity of **880 J/kgВ·K**. The comfort setpoint is **25В°C** and the pre-cooling setpoint is **22В°C**. If the chiller operates with a COP of **3.0** and the off-peak electricity tariff is **вӮ№4.50/kWh**:
- (i) Calculate the thermal energy stored in the slab during pre-cooling.
- (ii) Calculate the electrical energy consumed by the chiller.
- (iii) Estimate the **annual cost savings** if the same pre-cooling cycle is repeated for **300 working days per year** and the peak tariff is **вӮ№11/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) вҖ” Principle of Pre-Cooling (7 marks):**

Pre-cooling is based on the **principle of thermal energy storage in building mass**. The BAS controller executes a **time-based and condition-based control algorithm** that pre-conditions the building during off-peak hours.

**Block Diagram of the Pre-Cooling Control System:**

| Block | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Sensors** | Temperature, humidity, occupancy, tariff meter | Sense real-time building and grid conditions |
| **BAS Controller** | Microprocessor-based DDC controller | Executes the pre-cooling algorithm |
| **Actuators** | Chiller contactor, AHU dampers, valves | Physically control the cooling equipment |
| **Thermal Mass** | Concrete, masonry, furniture | Stores the cooling energy |

**Role of BAS Controller:**
- Implements the **scheduling algorithm** (e.g., ON at 11:30 PM, OFF at 6:00 AM)
- Compares sensor data with **predefined setpoints**
- Generates control signals to actuators via **BACnet/Modbus protocols**
- Logs data for **performance analysis and optimization**

**Role of Sensors:**
- **Outdoor air temperature sensor** вҖ” Determines if economizer mode is feasible
- **Indoor air temperature sensor** вҖ” Provides closed-loop feedback to the controller
- **Occupancy sensor (PIR)** вҖ” Detects early arrivals and triggers full cooling mode
- **Tariff meter** вҖ” Provides real-time electricity cost data for ToU optimization

**Role of Actuators:**
- **Chiller control** вҖ” ON/OFF or modulating signal based on load
- **AHU dampers** вҖ” Modulate the mix of return air and outside air
- **Smart thermostats** вҖ” Communicate with the BAS for zoned control

---

**Part (b) вҖ” Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $m = 80{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $c_p = 880 \text{ J/kgВ·K}$
- $T_{comfort} = 25В°C$
- $T_{pre} = 22В°C$
- $COP = 3.0$
- $C_{off-peak} = вӮ№4.50/\text{kWh}$
- $C_{peak} = вӮ№11/\text{kWh}$
- Working days = **300/year**

**Step 1: Temperature Depression**

$$\Delta T_{pre} = 25В°C - 22В°C = 3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Thermal Energy Stored (Part b-i)**

$$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{pre}$$

$$Q_{stored} = 80{,}000 \times 880 \times 3$$

$$Q_{stored} = 211{,}200{,}000 \text{ J}$$

Converting to kWh:

$$Q_{stored} = \frac{211{,}200{,}000}{3.6 \times 10^6} = 58.67 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3: Electrical Energy Consumed (Part b-ii)**

$$E_{electrical} = \frac{Q_{stored}}{COP} = \frac{58.67}{3.0} = 19.56 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4: Annual Cost Savings (Part b-iii)**

Cost if cooled during **off-peak** (with pre-cooling):

$$Cost_{off-peak, daily} = 19.56 \times 4.50 = вӮ№88.02$$

Cost if cooled during **peak** (without pre-cooling):

$$Cost_{peak, daily} = 19.56 \times 11.00 = вӮ№215.16$$

**Daily savings:**

$$Savings_{daily} = вӮ№215.16 - вӮ№88.02 = вӮ№127.14$$

**Annual savings:**

$$Savings_{annual} = 127.14 \times 300 = вӮ№38{,}142$$

> **Final Answer for Part (b):** Thermal energy stored = **58.67 kWh**, Electrical energy consumed = **19.56 kWh**, Annual cost savings = **вӮ№38,142**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B:**
**(a)** Compare the **four major pre-cooling strategies** commonly implemented in building automation systems. For each strategy, mention the operational time window, key advantage, and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A building has a thermal time constant of **П„ = 6 hours**. If the pre-cooling window is **5 hours** and the indoor setpoint is lowered from **26В°C to 23В°C**, calculate the temperature of the building mass at the end of the drift phase (8 hours after pre-cooling ends). Use the first-order thermal model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) вҖ” Comparison of Four Pre-Cooling Strategies (7 marks):**

| Strategy | Operational Window | Key Advantage | One Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Night Ventilation / Night Flush** | 11 PM вҖ“ 6 AM | Uses **free cooling** from cool night air, **zero chiller energy** | Effective only in climates with cool nights (О”T > 8В°C) |
| **2. Chiller-Based Pre-Cooling** | 12 AM вҖ“ 6 AM | Reliable and **climate-independent** | Higher electricity consumption even at off-peak rates |
| **3. Ice Storage Pre-Cooling** | 10 PM вҖ“ 6 AM | Very high energy density; chiller operates at **part-load efficiency peak** | High **capital cost** for ice storage tanks |
| **4. Economizer + Pre-Cooling Hybrid** | 11 PM вҖ“ 6 AM | Automatically switches between **free cooling and mechanical cooling** | Requires accurate **outdoor enthalpy sensors** |

**Part (b) вҖ” Numerical on First-Order Thermal Model (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Thermal time constant: $\tau = 6 \text{ hours}$
- Pre-cooling duration: $t_{pre} = 5 \text{ hours}$
- Initial temperature: $T_i = 26В°C$
- Final pre-cooling temperature: $T_{pre} = 23В°C$
- Time after pre-cooling ends (drift duration): $t_{drift} = 8 \text{ hours}$

**Step 1: First-Order Thermal Model**

For a building losing coolth during the drift phase, the temperature follows an **exponential rise** towards the ambient equilibrium temperature $T_{\infty}$:

$$T(t) = T_{\infty} - (T_{\infty} - T_{pre}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 2: Determine the Ambient Equilibrium Temperature**

The building was being cooled at the start, with a temperature depression of 3В°C. Assuming the equilibrium temperature is the **outdoor temperature during the drift phase**, we take it as:

$$T_{\infty} = 30В°C \text{ (assumed typical afternoon peak outdoor temperature)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Formula**

$$T(8) = 30 - (30 - 23) \cdot e^{-8/6}$$

Calculating the exponent:

$$e^{-8/6} = e^{-1.333} = 0.2636$$

Substituting:

$$T(8) = 30 - (7) \times 0.2636$$

$$T(8) = 30 - 1.845$$

$$T(8) = 28.16В°C$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

> The building mass temperature at the end of the 8-hour drift phase is **28.16В°C**, which is still **3.16В°C below the ambient** of 30В°C. This demonstrates that pre-cooling provides meaningful thermal buffering even after a full 8-hour work shift.

> **Final Answer for Part (b):** $T(8 \text{ hours}) = 28.16В°C$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** вҖ” Students often give $Q_{stored}$ in Joules when the tariff is in вӮ№/kWh. Always convert to kWh using the factor $3.6 \times 10^6$ J = 1 kWh.
> 2. **Confusing COP direction** вҖ” $E_{electrical} = Q_{cooling} / COP$ (not the reverse). Higher COP means LESS electricity for the same cooling.
> 3. **Ignoring the drift phase** вҖ” Many students forget that during the drift phase, the building is NOT being actively cooled. They incorrectly assume the chiller is running.
> 4. **Missing the feedback loop** вҖ” In the block diagram, students often forget to draw the **feedback arrow** from the building/room temperature sensor back to the BAS controller. This costs at least **1 mark** in a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Not mentioning ToU tariffs** вҖ” In economic analysis, students often skip the tariff differential, which is the **core economic justification** for pre-cooling.
> 6. **Confusing thermal time constant П„ with cooling duration t** вҖ” These are different physical quantities. П„ describes the *rate* of heat transfer; t is the *duration* of the pre-cooling window.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Pre-Cooling Strategies**

- вң… **Definition:** Cooling the building mass below occupied setpoint during off-peak hours to store "coolth"
- вң… **Core Formula:** $Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{pre}$
- вң… **Typical Setpoint Depression:** **2В°C to 4В°C** below comfort (NEVER exceed 4В°C due to condensation risk)
- вң… **Primary Benefit:** Peak demand reduction of **15% to 30%**
- вң… **Tariff Differential:** Requires **ToU metering** for economic viability
- вң… **Thermal Time Constant (П„):** Commercial buildings typically have П„ = **4 to 8 hours**
- вң… **Four Major Strategies:** Night Ventilation, Chiller Pre-Cooling, Ice Storage, Economizer Hybrid
- вң… **Key Sensors Required:** Outdoor temp, indoor temp, humidity, occupancy (PIR), tariff meter
- вң… **Control Architecture:** Closed-loop PID via BAS using BACnet/Modbus protocols
- вң… **Standards:** ASHRAE 90.1 and ECBC 2017 (India) recognize pre-cooling as a valid energy conservation measure
- вң… **Conversion to Remember:** 1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J
- вң… **COP Relationship:** $E_{electrical} = Q_{cooling} / COP$ (divide cooling load by COP to get electricity)
- вң… **Drift Phase:** Building mass gradually warms up; no active cooling during this phase
- вң… **Exam Trick:** Always include the **feedback loop** in the block diagram and the **tariff differential** in the economic analysis

> **End of Module 3 Notes вҖ” Pre-Cooling Strategies**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Recovery Wheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Energy Recovery Wheels ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Recovery Wheel (ERW):** A rotary air-to-air heat exchanger consisting of a slowly rotating porous matrix (typically a honeycomb structure of aluminum, synthetic fibers, or molecular sieve/desiccant material) mounted between two adjacent airflow ducts. As the wheel rotates through the **Exhaust Air (EA)** stream and the **Outside Air (OA)** stream alternately, it transfers **sensible heat** (temperature) and, depending on the matrix material, **latent heat** (moisture) between the two airstreams. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PEEIT526 вҖ“ Building Automation, Module 3), the Energy Recovery Wheel is classified as an **active air-to-air energy recovery device (AA-ARE)** used in HVAC systems to reduce the **Heating Ventilation and Air-Conditioning (HVAC)** energy load by preconditioning incoming fresh air using the thermal/enthalpic content of outgoing stale air. | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | OA | Outside Air | Fresh, incoming ventilation air | | EA / RA | Exhaust / Return Air | Stale air being rejected outdoors | | SA | Supply Air | Conditioned air delivered to the zone | | Sensible Energy | Temperature | Dry-bulb heat transfer | | Latent Energy | Moisture | Water-vapor (humidity) transfer | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Spinning Sponge" Analogy** рҹҢҖ > Imagine a giant cylindrical sponge slowly spinning between two wind tunnels. The left tunnel blows cold, dry winter air into a building. The right tunnel sucks warm, humid air out of the building. As the sponge rotates, the side that just passed through the warm exhaust tunnel is hot and damp. When that same segment rotates into the cold supply tunnel, it **releases** that heat and moisture into the incoming air. A moment later, it rotates back to absorb more heat and humidity. The result? The cold incoming air is **pre-warmed and pre-humidified** before it ever reaches the furnace or chiller вҖ” saving enormous amounts of energy. > In essence, the Energy Recovery Wheel acts as a **"thermal conveyor belt"** shuttling energy from where it is in surplus (exhaust) to where it is needed (incoming fresh air). ### 1.3 Classification of Energy Recovery Wheels | Type | Material | Transfers Sensible Heat? | Transfers Latent Heat? | Typical Efficiency | |------|----------|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **Sensible Wheel** (Heat Wheel) | Aluminum, metallic, or synthetic non-hygroscopic | вң… Yes | вқҢ No | 50вҖ“80 % | | **Total Energy Wheel** (Enthalpy Wheel) | Molecular sieve, silica gel, polymer fibers | вң… Yes | вң… Yes | 50вҖ“80 % total | | **Desiccant Wheel** | Lithium chloride, silica gel, or 3A molecular sieve | вң… Yes (minor) | вң… Yes (strong) | 60вҖ“90 % latent | ### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Standard operating rotational speed:** **10 вҖ“ 20 revolutions per hour (rph)** вҖ” slow, deliberate, and energy-efficient. > - **Wheel diameter range:** 0.5 m to **4.0 m** in commercial installations. > - **Airflow balance (CFM):** Must be **balanced** between supply and exhaust streams for optimum performance (Вұ10 %). > - **Cross-leakage / Carryover:** A small fraction of exhaust air leaks directly into the supply stream вҖ” typically **< 4 %** of supply airflow. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic The operation of an Energy Recovery Wheel is governed by the **alternating adsorption-desorption cycle** of its rotating matrix. Below is the structured logical flow of its operation: 1. **Rotation Initiation:** A fractional horsepower **3-phase or single-phase motor** drives a belt-and-pulley (or gear) mechanism that rotates the wheel at a controlled speed (typically 10вҖ“20 rph). 2. **Exhaust Stream Absorption (90В°вҖ“180В° sector):** A portion of the matrix passes through the **Exhaust Air (EA)** duct. The matrix material, due to its high internal surface area, **absorbs both sensible heat (О”T)** and **latent heat (О”W)** from the outgoing humid air. 3. **Rotation Transit:** The matrix sector physically moves out of the EA stream and rotates into the **Outside Air (OA)** stream. 4. **Supply Stream Desorption (0В°вҖ“90В° sector):** In the OA stream, the matrix **releases** the stored thermal and moisture energy to the cooler/drier incoming fresh air. 5. **Purge Sector (typically 5В°вҖ“10В° of rotation):** A small angular slice of the wheel passes through a **purge-air stream** to blow out any trapped exhaust air, reducing **cross-contamination**. 6. **Cycle Repetition:** The process is continuous, maintaining a steady-state energy transfer. ### 2.2 Critical Sub-Systems > [!NOTE] > **Why is the Purge Sector Important?** > Without the purge section, **stale exhaust air** carrying COвӮӮ, VOCs, and odors would be carried by the rotating matrix into the supply airstream. The purge section uses a small portion of the **incoming OA** to flush the wheel matrix of residual EA, achieving **cross-leakage below 4 %** in modern designs (per ASHRAE 90.1). | Sub-System | Function | Typical Specification | |------------|----------|----------------------| | **Rotating Matrix** | Heat/enthalpy storage | Honeycomb, 1.6 вҖ“ 6 mm flute size | | **Drive Motor** | Rotates the wheel | 0.25 вҖ“ 1.5 HP, VFD-controlled | | **Casing / Housing** | Separates the two airstreams | Galvanized steel / aluminum | | **Purge Sector** | Prevents cross-contamination | 5В° вҖ“ 10В° angular sector | | **Seals & Brushes** | Minimizes air bypass | Nylon, felt, or brush seals | | **VFD Controller** | Adjusts rotation speed to match load | 0 вҖ“ 100 % modulation | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are exam-critical for KTU 2024 Scheme Building Automation (PEEIT526).** | # | Formula | Variables | Notes | |---|---------|-----------|-------| | 1 | $\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - T_{s,in}}{T_{r,in} - T_{s,in}}$ | $\varepsilon_s$ = sensible effectiveness; $T_{s,in}$ = OA entering; $T_{r,in}$ = RA entering; $T_{s,out}$ = SA leaving | Winter mode | | 2 | $\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - W_{s,in}}{W_{r,in} - W_{s,in}}$ | $W$ = humidity ratio (kg/kg dry air) | Winter mode | | 3 | $\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{h_{s,out} - h_{s,in}}{h_{r,in} - h_{s,in}}$ | $h$ = enthalpy (kJ/kg) | Total wheel | | 4 | $\dot{Q}_s = \varepsilon_s \cdot \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{r,in} - T_{s,in})$ | $\dot{m}_s$ = mass flow rate of supply air (kg/s); $c_p$ вүҲ **1.005 kJ/kgВ·K** | Heat recovered in kW | | 5 | $\dot{Q}_l = \varepsilon_l \cdot \dot{m}_s \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_{r,in} - W_{s,in})$ | $h_{fg}$ вүҲ **2501 kJ/kg** | Latent heat recovered | | 6 | $\dot{Q}_t = \dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_l$ | Total heat recovered | Enthalpy wheel | | 7 | $\Delta P = \left( f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D_h} + \Sigma K \right) \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop across wheel | Airstream resistance | | 8 | $N = \dfrac{\text{rpm} \times 60}{\theta_{purge}}$ | $N$ = purge effectiveness ratio | For cross-leakage | | 9 | $CFM_{purge} = 0.05 \times CFM_{supply}$ | Typical purge airflow = 5 % of supply CFM | Industry standard | | 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_l}{2}$ (approx.) | Apparent mean effectiveness | Approximation only | > [!NOTE] > **Convention used in KTU problems:** Nomenclature is consistently $s$ = supply, $r$ = return/exhaust, $in$ = entering the wheel, $out$ = leaving the wheel. Always check whether the problem is in **Winter** (heating) or **Summer** (cooling) mode вҖ” the formula signs do not change, but the physical interpretation does. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Net Zero Buildings:** ERWs are mandated under **ASHRAE 90.1-2019** and **ECBC+ (India)** for any commercial building with outdoor air supply > 5000 CFM and where the HVAC system operates > 500 hours/year with a 50 % enthalpy difference. - **Hospital Operating Theatres:** Used to maintain positive pressure while recovering energy from exhaust. - **Data Centers:** Critical for meeting **PUE < 1.3** targets by reducing chiller load through enthalpy recovery. - **Smart Building Integration:** Modern ERWs integrate with **BACnet/Modbus** BMS to vary wheel speed in real time based on **О”enthalpy** between OA and RA, maximizing savings in **mixed-climate zones like Kerala**. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Sensible Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_s$) We define sensible effectiveness as the ratio of the actual temperature change of the supply air to the maximum possible temperature change. **Step 1:** Identify the maximum possible temperature change. If the supply air (OA) were to be brought to the return air temperature in a **counter-flow perfect heat exchanger**, the supply air leaving the wheel would be at $T_{r,in}$. The maximum possible temperature change is: $$\Delta T_{max} = T_{r,in} - T_{s,in}$$ **Step 2:** Identify the actual temperature change achieved by the wheel. As the matrix passes from the exhaust side to the supply side, it transfers heat to the OA. The actual temperature rise of the supply air is: $$\Delta T_{actual} = T_{s,out} - T_{s,in}$$ **Step 3:** Form the dimensionless ratio. By definition, the sensible effectiveness is the ratio of the actual change to the maximum possible change: $$\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{\Delta T_{actual}}{\Delta T_{max}}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the expressions from Steps 1 and 2. $$\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - T_{s,in}}{T_{r,in} - T_{s,in}}$$ This is the **canonical KTU form** of the sensible effectiveness equation, and it applies to both **winter** (heating) and **summer** (cooling) operations вҖ” the equation itself is unchanged; only the sign of the temperature differences flips. ### 3.2 Derivation of Latent Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_l$) **Step 1:** The latent effectiveness follows the same logic, but uses **humidity ratio** ($W$) instead of temperature. $$\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - W_{s,in}}{W_{r,in} - W_{s,in}}$$ **Step 2:** In a total energy (enthalpy) wheel, the latent transfer is the **dominant recovery mechanism** in humid climates. In **Kerala**, where the summer outdoor humidity ratio ($W_o$) often exceeds **0.018 kg/kg** and indoor comfort is around **0.009 kg/kg**, this term is the largest energy-saver. ### 3.3 Total Enthalpy Effectiveness ($\varepsilon_t$) The total effectiveness uses **specific enthalpy** ($h$) in kJ/kg, which is the thermodynamic quantity that simultaneously accounts for both temperature and moisture: $$\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{h_{s,out} - h_{s,in}}{h_{r,in} - h_{s,in}}$$ This is the **most general** form of the effectiveness equation and is required whenever the matrix transfers both sensible and latent heat. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style) > **Problem:** A building automation system uses a total energy recovery wheel in winter mode. The conditions are: > - Outside Air (OA) entering: $T_{s,in} = 5\,^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{s,in} = 0.0025$ kg/kg > - Return Air (RA) entering: $T_{r,in} = 24\,^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{r,in} = 0.0085$ kg/kg > - Airflow: 10,000 CFM вүҲ **4.72 mВі/s** at standard conditions (ПҒ вүҲ 1.2 kg/mВі) > - The manufacturerвҖҷs catalog states $\varepsilon_s = 0.75$ and $\varepsilon_l = 0.65$ > > **Find:** (a) The supply air temperature leaving the wheel. (b) The supply air humidity ratio leaving the wheel. (c) The total heat recovered in kW. **Part (a) вҖ” Supply Air Temperature** Using the sensible effectiveness formula: $$\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - T_{s,in}}{T_{r,in} - T_{s,in}}$$ Substitute $\varepsilon_s = 0.75$, $T_{s,in} = 5\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{r,in} = 24\,^\circ\text{C}$: $$0.75 = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - 5}{24 - 5}$$ $$0.75 = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - 5}{19}$$ Cross-multiply: $$T_{s,out} - 5 = 0.75 \times 19$$ $$T_{s,out} - 5 = 14.25$$ $$T_{s,out} = 5 + 14.25 = \mathbf{19.25\,^\circ\text{C}}$$ **Part (b) вҖ” Supply Air Humidity Ratio** Using the latent effectiveness formula: $$\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - W_{s,in}}{W_{r,in} - W_{s,in}}$$ Substitute $\varepsilon_l = 0.65$, $W_{s,in} = 0.0025$, $W_{r,in} = 0.0085$: $$0.65 = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - 0.0025}{0.0085 - 0.0025}$$ $$0.65 = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - 0.0025}{0.0060}$$ Cross-multiply: $$W_{s,out} - 0.0025 = 0.65 \times 0.0060 = 0.0039$$ $$W_{s,out} = 0.0025 + 0.0039 = \mathbf{0.0064\ \text{kg/kg}}$$ **Part (c) вҖ” Total Heat Recovered** First, compute the mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_s = \rho \times \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 4.72 = 5.664\ \text{kg/s}$$ Sensible heat recovered: $$\dot{Q}_s = \varepsilon_s \cdot \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{r,in} - T_{s,in})$$ $$\dot{Q}_s = 0.75 \times 5.664 \times 1.005 \times 19$$ $$\dot{Q}_s = 0.75 \times 5.664 \times 1.005 \times 19 = 81.04\ \text{kW}$$ Latent heat recovered: $$\dot{Q}_l = \varepsilon_l \cdot \dot{m}_s \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_{r,in} - W_{s,in})$$ $$\dot{Q}_l = 0.65 \times 5.664 \times 2501 \times 0.0060$$ $$\dot{Q}_l = 0.65 \times 5.664 \times 2501 \times 0.0060 = 55.20\ \text{kW}$$ Total heat recovered: $$\dot{Q}_t = \dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_l = 81.04 + 55.20 = \mathbf{136.24\ \text{kW}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** The wheel recovers 136.24 kW, which is the **thermal load the heating coil and humidifier no longer need to provide** вҖ” a major energy saving quantified by the BMS. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Effect of Operating Parameters | Parameter | Decrease Effect | Increase Effect | Engineering Trade-off | |-----------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------| | Wheel speed (rph) | Lower effectiveness, more cross-leakage risk | Higher effectiveness, higher motor power | Optimal: 10вҖ“20 rph | | OAвҖ“RA temperature diff | Lower sensible recovery | Higher sensible recovery | Climate-dependent | | Matrix flute size | Higher effectiveness, higher О”P | Lower О”P, lower effectiveness | 1.6вҖ“3 mm is standard | | Purge air % | More cross-leakage | Less cross-leakage, more fan energy | Standard: 5 % | | Wheel diameter | Lower face velocity, less contact time | Higher face velocity, more О”P | Industry: 2.5вҖ“4 m/s | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of an Energy Recovery Wheel system** integrated with a building automation framework. The diagram emphasizes the **two alternating airstreams**, the **purge sector**, the **drive subsystem**, and the **BMS feedback loop** that the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify. ```mermaid flowchart LR OAin[OA Inlet
5 degC Outside Air] --> Damper1 Damper1 --> FilterA[Pre Filter OA] FilterA --> WheelSector1[Sector A
Matrix passes through
OA side] WheelSector1 --> SupplyFan[Supply Fan] SupplyFan --> WheelSector2[Sector B
Matrix passes through
EA side] WheelSector2 --> ExhaustFan[Exhaust Fan] ExhaustFan --> Damper2 Damper2 --> EAout[EA Outlet
To atmosphere] WheelSector2 -.Stores heat and moisture.-> WheelSector1 ZoneIn[Return Air from
Building Zone] --> Damper3 Damper3 --> WheelSector2 PurgeAir[Purge Air 5 percent
of OA CFM] --> WheelSector2 DriveMotor[Drive Motor
Fractional HP VFD] --> WheelHub WheelHub[Rotating Wheel
10 to 20 rph] --> WheelSector1 WheelHub --> WheelSector2 TempSensorOA[OA Temp Humidity
Sensor] --> BMS[BMS Controller
BACnet Modbus] TempSensorRA[RA Temp Humidity
Sensor] --> BMS BMS --> VFDController[VFD Speed
Command] VFDController --> DriveMotor EnergyCalc[Energy Recovery
Effectiveness Log] --> BMS ``` ### 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section of the Energy Recovery Wheel The following diagram shows the **physical cross-section** of the wheel as installed in an Air Handling Unit (AHU), highlighting the **angular sector division** between exhaust and supply streams, and the location of the **purge sector**. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WheelCross[Cross Section of Rotary Energy Recovery Wheel] direction TB SectorTop[Top Half
Exhaust Air Sector
180 degree arc
Hot Humid Air from Zone] SectorBot[Bottom Half
Supply Air Sector
170 degree arc
Cold Dry Fresh Air from Outside] PurgeLeft[Purge Sector
10 degree arc
Flushes residual EA] WheelCore[Rotating Honeycomb Matrix
Aluminum or Desiccant coated] DriveShaft[Central Drive Shaft
Coupled to Motor via Belt] SectorTop --> WheelCore SectorBot --> WheelCore PurgeLeft --> WheelCore WheelCore --> DriveShaft end EAArrow[Exhaust Air In
Warm 24 degC Humid] --> SectorTop SectorTop --> EAOut[Exhaust Air Out
Cooled to 10 degC] OAArrow[Outside Air In
Cold 5 degC Dry] --> SectorBot SectorBot --> SAOut[Supply Air Out
Pre warmed to 19 degC] PurgeArrow[Purge Air
5 percent OA] --> PurgeLeft PurgeLeft --> PurgeOut[Purge To Exhaust
Carries trapped EA] ``` ### 4.3 State Diagram вҖ” Operational Modes ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Standby Standby --> WinterMode: OA Temp less than 10 degC WinterMode --> SummerMode: OA Temp greater than 24 degC SummerMode --> EconomizerMode: OA Enthalpy less than RA Enthalpy EconomizerMode --> SummerMode: OA Enthalpy rises WinterMode --> DefrostMode: Wheel Matrix Temp less than 2 degC DefrostMode --> WinterMode: Wheel Matrix Temp greater than 4 degC EconomizerMode --> Standby: AHU Off Command from BMS WinterMode --> Standby: AHU Off Command from BMS SummerMode --> Standby: AHU Off Command from BMS DefrostMode --> Standby: AHU Off Command from BMS ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the State Diagram:** The four primary operating states вҖ” *Winter (Heating Recovery)*, *Summer (Cooling Recovery)*, *Economizer (Free Cooling Bypass)*, and *Defrost (Sub-Zero Protection)* вҖ” are automatically managed by the BMS through BACnet/Modbus. The KTU 2024 module expects students to recognize these modes and explain when each is activated. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2023]** **Define an Energy Recovery Wheel.
