Differentiate between a Sensible Heat Wheel and a Total Enthalpy Wheel.** **Model Answer:** An Energy Recovery Wheel is a rotary air-to-air heat exchanger that uses a slowly rotating porous matrix to transfer heat (and moisture, depending on material) between the exhaust and supply airstreams of a building HVAC system. | Feature | Sensible Heat Wheel | Total Enthalpy Wheel | |---------|---------------------|---------------------| | Matrix Material | Aluminum, non-hygroscopic | Desiccant-coated, molecular sieve | | Transfers Heat | вң… Yes | вң… Yes | | Transfers Moisture | вқҢ No | вң… Yes | | Best Climate | Dry | Humid (Kerala-type) | | Typical $\varepsilon_s$ | 70вҖ“80 % | 60вҖ“75 % | | Typical $\varepsilon_l$ | 0 % | 50вҖ“70 % | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2022]** **What is the purpose of a purge sector in an Energy Recovery Wheel? How is purge air flow typically sized?** **Model Answer:** The purge sector is a small angular segment (5В°вҖ“10В°) of the wheel that is flushed with a portion of the **incoming Outside Air (OA)** to remove residual exhaust air trapped in the matrix. This prevents **cross-contamination** (cross-leakage) of stale air, COвӮӮ, odors, and VOCs into the supply airstream. The purge airflow is typically sized as **5 % of the supply CFM**. --- ### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE вҖ” Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]** **(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the construction, working principle, and key sub-systems of an **Energy Recovery Wheel** used in HVAC systems. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An energy recovery wheel operates in **winter** mode with the following data: - $T_{s,in} = 7\,^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{s,in} = 0.0030$ kg/kg - $T_{r,in} = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{r,in} = 0.0090$ kg/kg - Airflow rate = 8,000 CFM (вүҲ 3.78 mВі/s, ПҒ = 1.2 kg/mВі) - Sensible effectiveness $\varepsilon_s = 0.72$, Latent effectiveness $\varepsilon_l = 0.60$ Calculate: (i) Supply air temperature leaving the wheel, (ii) Supply air humidity ratio leaving the wheel, (iii) Total heat recovered in kW. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The Energy Recovery Wheel consists of: 1. **Rotating Matrix:** A honeycomb structure (flute size 1.6вҖ“3 mm) made of aluminum (sensible) or desiccant-coated material (enthalpy). 2. **Drive Motor & VFD:** 0.25вҖ“1.5 HP motor that rotates the wheel at 10вҖ“20 rph. 3. **Casing:** Galvanized steel housing with seals/brushes that separate the two airstreams. 4. **Purge Sector:** 5В°вҖ“10В° angular sector flushed with 5 % of OA CFM to reduce cross-leakage below 4 %. **Working:** As the matrix rotates through the hot/humid exhaust air, it absorbs thermal and moisture energy. When it rotates into the cold/dry supply air stream, it releases this energy, **pre-conditioning the fresh air** before it reaches the heating/cooling coil. The BMS modulates the wheel speed to optimize energy recovery. **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Supply air temperature leaving the wheel:** $$\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - T_{s,in}}{T_{r,in} - T_{s,in}}$$ $$0.72 = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - 7}{25 - 7}$$ $$T_{s,out} - 7 = 0.72 \times 18 = 12.96$$ $$T_{s,out} = 7 + 12.96 = \mathbf{19.96\,^\circ\text{C}}$$ **(ii) Supply air humidity ratio leaving the wheel:** $$\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - W_{s,in}}{W_{r,in} - W_{s,in}}$$ $$0.60 = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - 0.0030}{0.0090 - 0.0030}$$ $$W_{s,out} - 0.0030 = 0.60 \times 0.0060 = 0.0036$$ $$W_{s,out} = 0.0030 + 0.0036 = \mathbf{0.0066\ \text{kg/kg}}$$ **(iii) Total heat recovered:** Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m}_s = 1.2 \times 3.78 = 4.536\ \text{kg/s}$$ Sensible heat recovered: $$\dot{Q}_s = 0.72 \times 4.536 \times 1.005 \times 18 = 59.13\ \text{kW}$$ Latent heat recovered: $$\dot{Q}_l = 0.60 \times 4.536 \times 2501 \times 0.0060 = 40.86\ \text{kW}$$ Total: $$\dot{Q}_t = 59.13 + 40.86 = \mathbf{99.99\ \text{kW} \approx 100\ \text{kW}}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]** **(a)** Discuss the classification of Energy Recovery Wheels based on the type of energy transfer. Explain the role of **desiccant coatings** in total enthalpy wheels and list any three advantages and two limitations. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A building in Kochi uses an enthalpy wheel. The summer condition is: - $T_{OA} = 33\,^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{OA} = 0.0200$ kg/kg, $h_{OA} = 86.0$ kJ/kg - $T_{RA} = 24\,^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{RA} = 0.0095$ kg/kg, $h_{RA} = 48.5$ kJ/kg - Total effectiveness $\varepsilon_t = 0.68$ - Airflow = 12,000 CFM (вүҲ 5.66 mВі/s, ПҒ = 1.2 kg/mВі) Find the **enthalpy of the supply air leaving the wheel** and the **total cooling load saved in kW**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Classification by energy transfer:** - **Sensible Wheels:** Transfer only dry-bulb temperature (no moisture). Used in dry climates. - **Total (Enthalpy) Wheels:** Transfer both sensible heat and latent heat (moisture). Best for humid climates. - **Desiccant Wheels:** Specifically designed for dehumidification; matrix is coated with LiCl, silica gel, or 3A molecular sieve. **Role of desiccant coatings:** Desiccants adsorb water vapor from the air by surface adhesion and capillary condensation. In the OA (humid) side, they strip moisture, and on the RA (dry) side, they release it. This is governed by the **differential vapor pressure** between the two airstreams. **Three Advantages:** (1) Recovers latent heat in humid climates; (2) Reduces chiller load by 30вҖ“50 %; (3) Operates without cross-contamination when properly purged. **Two Limitations:** (1) Slightly higher pressure drop (75вҖ“150 Pa) than sensible wheels; (2) Desiccant degrades over 8вҖ“10 years and may require replacement. **Model Solution for (b):** The supply air leaves at: $$h_{s,out} = h_{s,in} + \varepsilon_t \cdot (h_{r,in} - h_{s,in})$$ $$h_{s,out} = 86.0 + 0.68 \times (48.5 - 86.0)$$ $$h_{s,out} = 86.0 + 0.68 \times (-37.5)$$ $$h_{s,out} = 86.0 - 25.5 = \mathbf{60.5\ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ Cooling load saved: $$\dot{m}_s = 1.2 \times 5.66 = 6.792\ \text{kg/s}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{saved} = \varepsilon_t \cdot \dot{m}_s \cdot (h_{OA} - h_{RA})$$ $$\dot{Q}_{saved} = 0.68 \times 6.792 \times 37.5 = \mathbf{173.2\ \text{kW}}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Sign error in winter/summer:** Students often write the effectiveness formula with a negative sign, leading to negative temperatures or humidity ratios. **Always subtract the entering supply condition from the entering return condition** as the denominator. > 2. **Confusing $T_{s,in}$ with $T_{s,out}$:** In the formula, the supply air **entering** the wheel is the **OA**, and supply air **leaving** the wheel is the **preconditioned SA**. Mixing these is the most frequent 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Forgetting $c_p = 1.005$ and $h_{fg} = 2501$:** These are **assumed constants** in KTU problems. If you calculate them from psychrometric tables unnecessarily, you may mismatch and lose partial credit. > 4. **Not stating purge angle:** When asked to list components, students forget the **purge sector** вҖ” this is a **2-mark item** in KTU marking schemes. > 5. **Ignoring units:** Always quote temperatures in В°C, humidity ratio in kg/kg, enthalpy in kJ/kg, and heat recovered in kW (or TR for chiller context). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Energy Recovery Wheels:** > - **Definition:** Rotary air-to-air heat exchanger with porous honeycomb matrix. > - **Three types:** Sensible (aluminum) | Total Enthalpy (desiccant) | Desiccant (LiCl/silica). > - **Operating speed:** 10вҖ“20 revolutions per hour. > - **Purge sector:** 5В°вҖ“10В° angular slice; sized at 5 % of supply CFM. > - **Cross-leakage limit:** < 4 % (ASHRAE 90.1 compliant). > - **Sensible effectiveness formula:** $\varepsilon_s = \dfrac{T_{s,out} - T_{s,in}}{T_{r,in} - T_{s,in}}$ > - **Latent effectiveness formula:** $\varepsilon_l = \dfrac{W_{s,out} - W_{s,in}}{W_{r,in} - W_{s,in}}$ > - **Total effectiveness formula:** $\varepsilon_t = \dfrac{h_{s,out} - h_{s,in}}{h_{r,in} - h_{s,in}}$ > - **Standard constants:** $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/kgВ·K; $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg. > - **Mass flow formula:** $\dot{m} = \rho \times \dot{V}$ with ПҒ вүҲ 1.2 kg/mВі at standard air. > - **Heat recovered formula:** $\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \cdot \dot{m} \cdot \Delta X$ where $X$ is $T$, $W$, or $h$. > - **Kerala-specific relevance:** Total enthalpy wheels dominate because of high humidity (W > 0.018 kg/kg in monsoon). > - **BMS integration:** BACnet/Modbus; VFD speed control based on О”enthalpy. > - **Standards:** ASHRAE 90.1-2019, ISHRAE, ECBC+ (India). > - **Defrost mode:** Activated when matrix temperature < 2 В°C to prevent icing. > - **Economizer mode:** Wheel stops when OA enthalpy < RA enthalpy (free cooling).

topics.title: Thermal Storage Systems and Dehumidification Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Storage Systems and Dehumidification Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thermal Energy Storage (TES) Systems

A **Thermal Energy Storage (TES) System** is a building automationвҖ“integrated subsystem that captures, retains, and releases thermal energy (either heat or coolness) for use at a later time, decoupling the time of energy generation from the time of its consumption. In the context of KTU 2024 scheme building automation, TES is treated as a **load-shifting and peak-shaving tool** that improves HVAC efficiency, supports renewable integration, and provides operational flexibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (as per syllabus):**
> *"A thermal storage system stores energy in the form of sensible heat, latent heat, or thermochemical potential and releases it on demand to meet heating, cooling, or domestic hot water loads in a building."*

### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” "The Bank Account of Heat"

Think of a TES as a **financial savings account** for thermal energy. During periods of low demand (or low electricity cost вҖ” e.g., night-time), the system "deposits" heat or chill into the storage medium (water tank, ice, PCM). When the building demands energy later (e.g., a hot afternoon), the system "withdraws" the stored energy. You are essentially borrowing from the past to pay for the present вҖ” exactly how an UPS battery works, but for heat instead of electrons.

### 1.1.2 The Three Physical Modes of Thermal Storage

| Mode | Storage Principle | Common Medium | Energy Density (approx.) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sensible** | Temperature change of medium | Water, rock, pebble, concrete | 4вҖ“60 kJ/kgВ·K |
| **Latent** | Phase change at constant temperature | Ice, paraffin, salt hydrates, PCM | 150вҖ“350 kJ/kg |
| **Thermochemical** | Reversible chemical reaction | Silica gel, metal hydrides, zeolites | 500вҖ“2000 kJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> The SI unit for stored energy is the **Joule (J)**, but in HVAC practice **kWh, kJ, and ton-hours (tonВ·h)** are preferred. **1 ton of refrigeration = 3.517 kW** of cooling.

---

## 1.2 Dehumidification Technologies

**Dehumidification** is the process of removing moisture (water vapour) from air to maintain a desired indoor humidity level, typically for thermal comfort, Indoor Air Quality (IAQ), and the protection of building materials/equipment. Building automation systems orchestrate dehumidification by monitoring **relative humidity (RH)** sensors and actuating cooling coils, desiccant wheels, or exhaust dampers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (as per syllabus):**
> *"Dehumidification in building automation is the controlled removal of water vapour from the supply/return air stream using either condensation (cooling-based) or adsorption/absorption (desiccant-based) methods, governed by feedback from humidity sensors and DDC controllers."*

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” "The Sponge in the Pipe"

Imagine your air-conditioning duct is a pipe carrying humid air. A dehumidifier is like a **sponge placed inside the pipe** that soaks up water vapour before the air enters your room. The sponge has to be **dried (regenerated)** periodically вҖ” that is exactly how a desiccant wheel works. Alternatively, you can cool the air until the water **condenses** out, like a cold water bottle "sweating" on a summer day вҖ” that is the cooling-coil method.

### 1.2.2 Why Dehumidification Matters in Building Automation

> [!NOTE]
> ASHRAE Standard 55 recommends indoor **RH between 30 % and 60 %**. Below 30 % вҶ’ dryness, static, respiratory irritation. Above 60 % вҶ’ microbial growth, condensation on walls, musty odours.

Dehumidification is essential in:
- **Hospitals, operation theatres** (infection control, surgical sterility).
- **Pharma manufacturing & cleanrooms** (process RH control).
- **Museums, archives, libraries** (preservation).
- **Swimming pools, spas** (latent load can exceed sensible load).
- **Tropical-zone commercial buildings** (Kerala climate вҖ” high latent loads for ~8 months/year).

### 1.2.3 Categorisation of Dehumidification Technologies

1. **Condensation (Cooling-Coil) Dehumidification** вҖ” by sub-cooling air below its dew point.
2. **Desiccant Dehumidification** вҖ”
   * 2a. **Solid Desiccant** (silica gel, molecular sieve, lithium chloride impregnated wheel).
   * 2b. **Liquid Desiccant** (LiBr, LiCl, CaClвӮӮ solutions).
3. **Membrane / Sorption Membrane Dehumidification**.
4. **Hybrid Systems** (desiccant + cooling coil for energy-efficient operation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensible Thermal Storage (STS)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

A **sensible heat storage** system uses the change in temperature ($\Delta T$) of a storage medium to store energy. The quantity of heat stored is directly proportional to:
- The **mass ($m$)** of the storage medium.
- The **specific heat capacity ($c$)** of the medium.
- The **temperature swing ($\Delta T$)** allowed in the medium.

### 2.1.2 Energy Stored in Sensible TES

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$
Q_{sensible} \;=\; m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T
$$

where
- $Q_{sensible}$ = thermal energy stored (J or kJ)
- $m$ = mass of storage medium (kg)
- $c_{p}$ = specific heat capacity at constant pressure (kJ/kgВ·K)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change of the medium (K or В°C)

For a **tank-based water storage system**, the equation is expressed in volumetric form as:

$$
Q \;=\; \rho \cdot V \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\rho$ = density of water (вүҲ **1000 kg/mВі**) and $V$ = volume of stored water (mВі).

### 2.1.3 Stratification вҖ” The Heart of Efficient Water Storage

In a **thermally stratified chilled water tank (TES)**, a distinct **thermocline** layer separates the cold (lower) and warm (upper) regions. A sharp thermocline:
- Minimises mixing losses.
- Maximises usable $\Delta T$ during discharge.
- Improves the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of the connected chiller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Number criterion for thermocline integrity** вҖ” to prevent destructive mixing, inlet flow velocity must satisfy:
> $$Re \;=\; \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} \;<\; 2000 \quad \text{(laminar injection preferred)}$$
> where $v$ is inlet velocity and $D$ is the inlet diffuser diameter.

### 2.1.4 Charging and Discharging Time

$$
t_{charge} \;=\; \frac{Q_{stored}}{\dot{Q}_{chiller} - \dot{Q}_{loss}}
$$

$$
t_{discharge} \;=\; \frac{Q_{stored}}{\dot{Q}_{load} + \dot{Q}_{loss}}
$$

---

## 2.2 Latent Thermal Storage (LTS) вҖ” Phase Change Materials (PCMs)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

When a material undergoes a **phase change** (solid вҶ” liquid, or liquid вҶ” vapour), it absorbs or releases a large quantity of energy at **nearly constant temperature** вҖ” this is the **latent heat of fusion ($L_f$)** or **latent heat of vaporisation ($L_v$)**. PCMs exploit this for high-density, near-isothermal storage.

$$
Q_{latent} \;=\; m \cdot L_{f}
$$

### 2.2.2 Common PCMs in Building Applications

| PCM | Phase Change Temp (В°C) | $L_f$ (kJ/kg) | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Water / Ice | 0 | 334 | Chilled water/ice TES |
| Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt) | 32 | 250 | Building thermal mass |
| Paraffin (C14вҖ“C24) | 23вҖ“68 | 150вҖ“250 | Wallboard, ceiling tiles |
| Fatty acids (capric, lauric) | 30вҖ“45 | 150вҖ“170 | Passive building envelope |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examination problems, the **ice-on-coil storage** or **encapsulated PCM TES** are the two most frequently asked systems. For ice-on-coil, $L_f$ of water is taken as **334 kJ/kg**.

### 2.2.3 Combined Sensible + Latent Storage

In a real PCM tank, both sensible heating/cooling of the solid/liquid AND the latent transition occur:

$$
Q_{total} \;=\; m \cdot c_{p,solid} \cdot (T_{m} - T_{initial}) \;+\; m \cdot L_{f} \;+\; m \cdot c_{p,liquid} \cdot (T_{final} - T_{m})
$$

where $T_m$ is the melting temperature of the PCM.

---

## 2.3 Dehumidification вҖ” Psychrometric Foundations

### 2.3.1 Key Humidity Parameters

- **Partial pressure of water vapour ($P_v$)** вҖ” in Pa or kPa.
- **Saturation pressure ($P_{sat}$)** вҖ” temperature-dependent.
- **Relative Humidity ($\phi$)**:
  $$\phi \;=\; \frac{P_v}{P_{sat}(T)} \times 100\%$$
- **Humidity Ratio ($W$)** (kg of water vapour per kg of dry air):
  $$W \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$$

### 2.3.2 Dew Point Temperature ($T_{dp}$)

The temperature at which air becomes saturated ($\phi$ = 100 %) on cooling. To dehumidify by condensation, the cooling coil surface must be **below $T_{dp}$**.

### 2.3.3 Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR) вҖ” A Critical KTU Concept

The **SHR** indicates the proportion of total cooling load that is sensible (temperature-based) versus latent (moisture-based):

$$
SHR \;=\; \frac{q_{s}}{q_{s} + q_{l}}
$$

An SHR close to **1.0** вҶ’ dry air, low latent load (e.g., temperate climate).
An SHR close to **0.5** вҶ’ very humid air, high latent load (e.g., **Kerala monsoon climate**).

In tropical regions, conventional AC systems often face the **"high latent load"** problem вҖ” they must overcool air to remove moisture, then **reheat** it to supply-design temperature, wasting energy.

### 2.3.4 The Reheat Problem

$$
\text{Reheat Energy Waste} \;=\; \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{supply} - T_{coil,out})
$$

Hybrid desiccant systems eliminate the reheat penalty.

---

## 2.4 Desiccant Dehumidification вҖ” The Science

### 2.4.1 Adsorption vs Absorption

- **Adsorption** вҖ” water molecules adhere to the **surface** of a solid (e.g., silica gel).
- **Absorption** вҖ” water molecules penetrate **into the bulk** of a liquid (e.g., LiCl solution absorbing water vapour).

### 2.4.2 Desiccant Wheel Operation

A desiccant wheel is a **slowly rotating honeycomb matrix** impregnated with desiccant (silica gel, lithium chloride, molecular sieve).

- **Process Air Stream (3/4 of wheel)** вҖ” humid air passes through, water vapour is adsorbed.
- **Regeneration Air Stream (1/4 of wheel)** вҖ” hot air (120вҖ“180 В°C) drives off the adsorbed water; the wheel is "dried" and re-enters the process side.

$$
\text{Desiccant Effectiveness } (\varepsilon) \;=\; \frac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in} - W_{sat}(T_{des})}
$$

### 2.4.3 Energy Requirement for Regeneration

$$
\dot{Q}_{regen} \;=\; \dot{m}_{regen} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{regen,in} - T_{ambient})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 100 В°C = 2257 kJ/kg.** This is the dominant energy cost of desiccant regeneration, which is why **waste heat, solar thermal, or CHP exhaust** is preferred over electric heating.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat stored (PCM) | J |
| 3 | $Q = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Water-tank sensible storage | J |
| 4 | $t = Q / \dot{Q}$ | Charging/discharging time | s |
| 5 | $\phi = P_v / P_{sat}$ | Relative humidity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $W = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$ | Humidity ratio | kg/kg da |
| 7 | $SHR = q_s / (q_s + q_l)$ | Sensible Heat Ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Reynolds number (stratification check) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $COP = Q_{cooling} / W_{compressor}$ | Chiller efficiency | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\varepsilon = (W_{in} - W_{out})/(W_{in} - W_{sat})$ | Desiccant wheel effectiveness | dimensionless |

> Real-world engineering use: The COP of an ice-storage chiller can be **20вҖ“30 % higher** than a conventional chiller because it operates at night when ambient is cooler, and the reduced condensing temperature lifts COP. Combined with off-peak electricity tariffs, the **payback period of TES in commercial buildings is typically 4вҖ“8 years**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Stratified Chilled Water TES

We derive the total energy content of a vertical cylindrical stratified chilled water tank used in building HVAC.

**Given:**
- Tank volume $V$ = 50 mВі
- Water density $\rho$ = 1000 kg/mВі
- Specific heat $c_p$ = 4.186 kJ/kgВ·K
- Cold layer temperature $T_c$ = 5 В°C
- Warm layer temperature $T_w$ = 15 В°C
- Thermocline thickness (neglected initially)

**Step 1 вҖ” Mass of water in the tank:**

$$
m \;=\; \rho \cdot V \;=\; 1000 \cdot 50 \;=\; 50{,}000 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Temperature swing across thermocline:**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; T_{w} - T_{c} \;=\; 15 - 5 \;=\; 10 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Sensible heat stored:**

$$
Q \;=\; m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 50{,}000 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 10
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 2{,}093{,}000 \;\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Convert to kWh (since 1 kWh = 3600 kJ):**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{2{,}093{,}000}{3600} \;\approx\; 581.4 \;\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Convert to ton-hours of cooling (1 ton = 3.517 kW):**

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{581.4}{3.517} \;\approx\; 165.3 \;\text{ton \cdot h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 50 mВі stratified tank storing a 10 K temperature swing can deliver **~165 ton-hours of cooling** вҖ” sufficient for one full day's cooling load of a small commercial building.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Volume of Ice Storage for a Building Load

**Given:**
- Daily peak cooling load $Q_{load}$ = 500 tonВ·h
- Density of ice $\rho_{ice}$ = 917 kg/mВі
- Latent heat of fusion of water $L_f$ = 334 kJ/kg
- 1 ton of refrigeration = 3.517 kW = 12,661 kJ/h

**Step 1 вҖ” Convert load to kJ:**

$$
Q_{load} \;=\; 500 \cdot 12{,}661 \;=\; 6{,}330{,}500 \;\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Mass of ice required (assuming 100 % discharge):**

$$
m_{ice} \;=\; \frac{Q_{load}}{L_f} \;=\; \frac{6{,}330{,}500}{334} \;\approx\; 18{,}954 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Volume of ice required:**

$$
V_{ice} \;=\; \frac{m_{ice}}{\rho_{ice}} \;=\; \frac{18{,}954}{917} \;\approx\; 20.7 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Practical tank sizing (ice occupies ~70 % of total tank in ice-on-coil systems):**

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; \frac{V_{ice}}{0.70} \;\approx\; 29.5 \;\text{m}^3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 29.5 mВі ice tank allows the chiller to operate only at night (off-peak), shifting ~70 % of the daytime peak load. Electricity cost savings (with Time-of-Use tariffs) typically exceed **вӮ№ 8вҖ“12 lakh per year** for a 500-ton commercial plant in Indian cities.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR) for a Tropical Air Stream

**Given:**
- Outdoor air: $T_{db}$ = 35 В°C, $T_{wb}$ = 28 В°C, $P_{atm}$ = 101.325 kPa
- Room: $T_{db}$ = 24 В°C, RH = 50 %

**Step 1 вҖ” Saturation pressure at 35 В°C (from steam tables / Antoine equation):**
$$
P_{sat}(35^\circ C) \;\approx\; 5.628 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Partial vapour pressure of outdoor air (using wet-bulb approximation):**
$$
P_v \;\approx\; P_{sat}(T_{wb}) - A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot (T_{db} - T_{wb})
$$

Using psychrometric constant $A \approx 0.00066$ (В°CвҒ»В№):

$$
P_v \;\approx\; 3.778 - 0.00066 \cdot 101.325 \cdot (35 - 28)
$$

$$
P_v \;\approx\; 3.778 - 0.468 \;\approx\; 3.31 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Humidity ratio of outdoor air:**

$$
W_o \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.31}{101.325 - 3.31} \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.31}{98.015} \;\approx\; 0.0210 \;\text{kg/kg da}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Humidity ratio of room air (50 % RH at 24 В°C):**

$P_{sat}(24^\circ C) \approx 2.985$ kPa

$$
P_{v,room} \;=\; 0.50 \cdot 2.985 \;\approx\; 1.493 \;\text{kPa}
$$

$$
W_r \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.493}{101.325 - 1.493} \;\approx\; 0.00930 \;\text{kg/kg da}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Latent heat to be removed per kg of dry air:**

$$
q_{l} \;=\; (W_o - W_r) \cdot h_{fg} \;=\; (0.0210 - 0.0093) \cdot 2501
$$

$$
q_{l} \;\approx\; 0.0117 \cdot 2501 \;\approx\; 29.26 \;\text{kJ/kg da}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Sensible heat to be removed per kg of dry air:**

$$
q_{s} \;=\; c_p \cdot (T_o - T_r) \;=\; 1.005 \cdot (35 - 24) \;\approx\; 11.06 \;\text{kJ/kg da}
$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Sensible Heat Ratio:**

$$
SHR \;=\; \frac{q_s}{q_s + q_l} \;=\; \frac{11.06}{11.06 + 29.26} \;\approx\; 0.274
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** With SHR вүҲ 0.27, this is a **"latent-dominant"** load вҖ” typical of Kerala/coastal Indian summer. Conventional AC systems would have to severely overcool and reheat; **a desiccant + cooling coil hybrid** is the most energy-efficient solution.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Selection of Dehumidification Technology

| Criterion | Cooling-Coil Condensation | Solid Desiccant Wheel | Liquid Desiccant | Membrane |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Latent load capacity | LowвҖ“Moderate | Very High | Very High | Moderate |
| Energy source | Electric (compressor) | Thermal (regen) | Thermal (regen) | Electric + thermal |
| COP / efficiency | 3.0вҖ“4.5 (chiller) | 1.0вҖ“1.5 (thermal) | 1.0вҖ“2.0 | 1.5вҖ“2.5 |
| Suitable for SHR | $> 0.7$ (dry climates) | $< 0.7$ (humid climates) | $< 0.7$ | 0.5вҖ“0.8 |
| Capital cost | Low | ModerateвҖ“High | High | High |
| Maintenance | Moderate | Moderate (wheel wear) | High (corrosion) | Moderate |
| Typical applications | Offices, residences | Hospitals, pharma, museums | Industrial drying | Cleanrooms |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate of Regeneration Air for a Desiccant Wheel

**Given:**
- Process air: $\dot{m}_p$ = 5 kg/s, $W_{in}$ = 0.0210 kg/kg, $W_{out}$ required = 0.0090 kg/kg
- Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ = 0.80
- $W_{sat}(T_{des})$ (humidity ratio of saturated air at desiccant temperature) вүҲ 0.0050 kg/kg
- Regeneration temperature = 150 В°C

**Step 1 вҖ” Actual water removed per second:**

$$
\dot{m}_{water} \;=\; \dot{m}_p \cdot (W_{in} - W_{out}) \;=\; 5 \cdot (0.0210 - 0.0090) \;=\; 0.060 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Equivalent water in regeneration air (assuming mass balance):**

$$
\dot{m}_{regen} \cdot (W_{regen,out} - W_{regen,in}) \;=\; \dot{m}_{water}
$$

If $W_{regen,in}$ = 0.0090 and $W_{regen,out}$ = 0.0300:

$$
\dot{m}_{regen} \;=\; \frac{0.060}{0.0300 - 0.0090} \;=\; \frac{0.060}{0.0210} \;\approx\; 2.86 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Thermal energy for regeneration (sensible + latent):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{regen} \;=\; \dot{m}_{regen} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{regen} - T_{amb}) + \dot{m}_{water} \cdot h_{fg}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{regen} \;\approx\; 2.86 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (150 - 30) + 0.060 \cdot 2257
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{regen} \;\approx\; 345.0 + 135.4 \;\approx\; 480.4 \;\text{kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This regeneration energy of ~480 kW can be supplied **free of cost** if waste heat from a CHP engine, biomass boiler, or rooftop solar thermal collector is used. This is the key reason desiccant systems pair with waste-heat recovery in net-zero buildings.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**

### Q1. Differentiate between sensible and latent thermal energy storage. Give two examples of storage media used in each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensible TES | Latent TES |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storage mechanism | Temperature change | Phase change |
| Energy equation | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q = m \cdot L_f$ |
| Energy density | Low (4вҖ“60 kJ/kg) | High (150вҖ“350 kJ/kg) |
| Temperature during charge/discharge | Variable | Nearly constant (= phase change T) |
| Example media | Water, rock, pebble, concrete | Ice, paraffin, salt hydrates, Glauber's salt |
| Complexity | Simple tank design | Requires PCM encapsulation |

Sensible storage is used for **chilled water TES**, while latent storage is used for **ice TES and PCM building envelopes**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**

### Q2. Define Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR). Why is SHR an important parameter in selecting a dehumidification system?

**Model Answer:**

The **Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR)** is the ratio of sensible heat to total heat (sensible + latent) of a cooling process:

$$
SHR \;=\; \frac{q_s}{q_s + q_l}
$$

It is a key parameter in **HVAC system design** because:
1. It determines whether a **conventional cooling coil** or a **desiccant-based system** is appropriate.
2. For **tropical/humid climates (SHR < 0.7)**, conventional systems suffer from the **"reheat problem"**, and desiccant wheels or hybrid systems are preferred.
3. It guides the **selection of cooling coil temperature and air volume**, directly impacting chiller sizing and energy use.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** вҖ” *[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024, Modified]*

#### (a) Explain the working of an Ice-on-Coil Thermal Energy Storage system with a neat schematic. List its advantages over conventional chilled water TES. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
In an ice-on-coil system, a network of HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene) or polypropylene coils is immersed in a tank of water. During the **charging cycle (night-time)**, refrigerant or chilled glycol flows inside the coils, freezing the surrounding water into a thick annular ice layer. During the **discharge cycle (daytime)**, building return water circulates over the ice surface; heat from the return water melts the ice, producing chilled water at ~1вҖ“3 В°C for supply to the AHU.

**Key Sub-Systems:**
1. **Refrigerant circuit** вҖ” a dedicated chiller, sized for night-time operation, runs at higher COP due to lower ambient temperature.
2. **Coil array** вҖ” typically ice builds radially outward to ~40вҖ“60 mm thickness.
3. **Water tank** вҖ” the water inventory that undergoes phase change.
4. **Control valves and pumps** вҖ” sequenced by the DDC for charge/discharge transitions.

**Advantages over Chilled Water TES:**
- **5вҖ“10Г— higher energy density** per unit volume (ice $L_f$ = 334 kJ/kg vs water $\Delta T$ sensible storage).
- **Lower tank volume** вҖ” saving floor space.
- **Lower supply chilled water temperature** (1 В°C vs 5вҖ“6 В°C) вҶ’ enables smaller AHU coils.
- **Better chiller COP** at night due to lower condensing temperature.
- **Improved load shifting** to off-peak electricity tariff windows.

#### (b) A commercial building has a daily cooling load of 800 tonВ·h. Design an ice-on-coil TES system. Calculate: (i) Mass of ice, (ii) Tank volume, (iii) Charging time if chiller capacity is 200 TR. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $Q_{load}$ = 800 tonВ·h, $L_f$ = 334 kJ/kg, $\rho_{ice}$ = 917 kg/mВі, 1 TR = 12,661 kJ/h, chiller = 200 TR.

**Step 1 вҖ” Total cooling load in kJ:**

$$
Q_{load} \;=\; 800 \cdot 12{,}661 \;=\; 10{,}128{,}800 \;\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Mass of ice (assuming 90 % discharge efficiency):**

$$
m_{ice} \;=\; \frac{Q_{load}}{L_f \cdot \eta_{discharge}} \;=\; \frac{10{,}128{,}800}{334 \cdot 0.90} \;\approx\; 33{,}684 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Volume of ice (pure ice):**

$$
V_{ice} \;=\; \frac{33{,}684}{917} \;\approx\; 36.7 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Tank volume (ice occupies 60 % of tank in ice-on-coil):**

$$
V_{tank} \;=\; \frac{36.7}{0.60} \;\approx\; 61.2 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Charging time:**

Chiller capacity in kJ/h = $200 \cdot 12{,}661$ = 2,532,200 kJ/h

$$
t_{charge} \;=\; \frac{10{,}128{,}800}{2{,}532{,}200} \;\approx\; 4.0 \;\text{hours}
$$

> This 4-hour charging window aligns well with **midnightвҖ“6 AM off-peak** tariff slabs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** вҖ” Alternative Choice

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a solid desiccant wheel dehumidification system. Discuss the role of the regeneration heater and explain why waste heat or solar thermal is preferred for regeneration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.3 above for full schematic).**

**Working Principle:**
The desiccant wheel is a cylindrical honeycomb rotor (typically made of corrugated fiberglass or ceramic paper) impregnated with desiccant вҖ” commonly **silica gel, lithium chloride, or molecular sieve (3 Г…/4 Г…)**. The wheel rotates slowly (~6вҖ“20 RPM) between two airstreams:

- **Process air stream (~75 % of wheel cross-section):** Humid outdoor/return air passes through the desiccant matrix. Water vapour molecules are **adsorbed** onto the desiccant surface, and dehumidified air exits. After passing over the desiccant, this air typically experiences a **temperature rise of 5вҖ“15 В°C** due to the heat of adsorption вҖ” hence it is sent to a **cooling coil** for sensible cooling before supply.

- **Regeneration air stream (~25 % of wheel cross-section):** Heated air (120вҖ“180 В°C) flows counter-directionally. It **drives off** the adsorbed water from the desiccant, and the moist hot exhaust is vented outside. The desiccant is now "dried" and ready to adsorb again.

**Role of the Regeneration Heater:**
The heater raises the regeneration air to a temperature high enough to reverse the adsorption isotherm вҖ” i.e., to release the captured moisture. The thermal energy required is **dominated by the latent heat of vaporisation of water (~2257 kJ/kg)** plus sensible heating of the regeneration air.

**Why waste heat / solar thermal is preferred:**
1. The bulk of regeneration energy is **low-grade thermal** (120вҖ“180 В°C) вҖ” ideal for solar thermal collectors, biomass boiler flue gas, or CHP engine jacket cooling water.
2. **Avoids the COP penalty** of using electric resistive heating (electric resistance = 100 % efficient at point of use, but upstream generation/transmission losses make the *source-to-desiccant* efficiency ~30вҖ“35 %).
3. **Carbon and cost savings** вҖ” waste heat is essentially free; payback periods drop from 10 years to 3вҖ“5 years.
4. Enables **net-zero and green-building integration** with LEED / GRIHA / IGBC rating points.

#### (b) An air-handling unit (AHU) handles 10 mВі/s of air. The outdoor conditions are 35 В°C DBT, 60 % RH. The desired supply conditions are 22 В°C DBT, 50 % RH. Using a desiccant + cooling coil hybrid, calculate the cooling coil load and the SHR before and after the desiccant. Assume barometric pressure = 101.325 kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Psychrometric properties of outdoor air:**

$P_{sat}(35^\circ C) \approx 5.628$ kPa

$$
P_v \;=\; 0.60 \cdot 5.628 \;=\; 3.377 \;\text{kPa}
$$

$$
W_o \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.377}{101.325 - 3.377} \;\approx\; 0.0214 \;\text{kg/kg da}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” After desiccant (assume $W_{out}$ = 0.0090 kg/kg da, $T$ rises to 45 В°C due to heat of adsorption):**

Mass flow rate of dry air (approx. at standard conditions, $\rho_{da} \approx 1.2$ kg/mВі):

$$
\dot{m}_{da} \;\approx\; 10 \cdot 1.2 \;=\; 12 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Sensible cooling load (after desiccant to supply condition 22 В°C):**

$$
q_s \;=\; \dot{m}_{da} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 12 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (45 - 22) \;\approx\; 277.4 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Latent cooling load:**

$$
q_l \;=\; \dot{m}_{da} \cdot (W_{after\,des} - W_{supply}) \cdot h_{fg}
$$

$W_{supply}$ at 22 В°C, 50 % RH: $P_{sat}(22^\circ C) \approx 2.645$ kPa вҶ’ $P_v = 1.323$ kPa вҶ’ $W_{supply} \approx 0.0082$ kg/kg

$$
q_l \;=\; 12 \cdot (0.0090 - 0.0082) \cdot 2501 \;\approx\; 12 \cdot 0.0008 \cdot 2501 \;\approx\; 24.0 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Total cooling load after desiccant:**

$$
q_{total} \;\approx\; 277.4 + 24.0 \;\approx\; 301.4 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” SHR after desiccant:**

$$
SHR_{after} \;=\; \frac{277.4}{301.4} \;\approx\; 0.92
$$

**Step 7 вҖ” SHR before desiccant (conventional AC scenario, 35 В°C вҶ’ 22 В°C, $W_o$ вҶ’ $W_{supply}$):**

$$
q_{s,conv} \;=\; 12 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 13 \;\approx\; 156.8 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
q_{l,conv} \;=\; 12 \cdot (0.0214 - 0.0082) \cdot 2501 \;\approx\; 396.2 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
SHR_{before} \;=\; \frac{156.8}{156.8 + 396.2} \;\approx\; 0.28
$$

> **Conclusion:** Hybrid desiccant shifts SHR from 0.28 (latent-dominant, hard to handle conventionally) to 0.92 (sensible-dominant, easy to handle with standard cooling coil). The cooling coil must remove only 277 kW instead of 553 kW вҖ” **saving ~50 % chiller energy** and eliminating the reheat penalty.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes in this module вҖ” read carefully:**
> 1. **Using $L_f$ = 334 kJ/kg for water but forgetting to multiply by mass** вҖ” always start with the *energy equation*, not the *mass equation*.
> 2. **Mixing up enthalpy of vaporisation** ($h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg at 0 В°C, 2257 kJ/kg at 100 В°C) вҖ” for psychrometric problems at ~30вҖ“35 В°C, use **2501 kJ/kg** unless the problem specifies otherwise.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.622 constant in humidity ratio** вҖ” a frequent deduction of 1 mark.
> 4. **Not converting 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,661 kJ/h** вҖ” many numerical errors originate from inconsistent units.
> 5. **Ignoring the "reheat problem"** in conventional AC when explaining why desiccants are needed in tropical climates вҖ” KTU examiners explicitly look for this insight.
> 6. **Drawing the desiccant wheel as two separate boxes** вҖ” the wheel is a *single rotating body* with two airflow sectors; examiners deduct for unclear schematics.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TES = load-shifting + peak-shaving + renewable integration tool.** Three modes: **sensible, latent, thermochemical**.
- **Water TES**: $Q = \rho V c_p \Delta T$; typical $\Delta T$ = 5вҖ“10 K across thermocline.
- **Ice TES**: $Q = m L_f$; $L_f$ of water = **334 kJ/kg**; ice-on-coil is the dominant commercial design.
- **PCM TES**: combine $c_p \Delta T$ (sensible) + $L_f$ (latent) for total energy content.
- **Dehumidification is essential** when SHR < 0.7 вҖ” typical of Kerala and coastal India.
- **Humidity ratio** $W = 0.622 \cdot P_v / (P_{atm} - P_v)$.
- **Relative humidity** $\phi = P_v / P_{sat}(T) \times 100\%$.
- **SHR** $= q_s / (q_s + q_l)$; ASHRAE comfort range = 30вҖ“60 % RH.
- **Desiccant wheel** uses **adsorption**; regenerates with hot air (120вҖ“180 В°C).
- **Liquid desiccant** uses **absorption**; LiBr and LiCl are common.
- **Reheat problem** in conventional AC is solved by **desiccant + cooling coil hybrid**.
- **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,661 kJ/h**; **$h_{fg}$ вүҲ 2501 kJ/kg** at psychrometric temperatures.
- **BMS integration** uses BACnet/Modbus to DDC controllers; sensors include RH, T, flow, and level.
- **Stratification efficiency** in water TES > **90 %** if inlet is laminar and diffuser geometry is correct.
- **Ice TES payback** in Indian commercial buildings: typically **4вҖ“8 years** with TOU tariffs.
- **Exam focus areas**: derivations of $Q = mc_p\Delta T$, $W$, SHR, ice mass, tank volume, and hybrid desiccant cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heating Process and Applications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Heating Process and Applications in Building Automation

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heating Process and Applications

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition and Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Heating Process** in Building Automation refers to the integrated, sensor-driven, and controller-actuated thermal energy delivery system that maintains the indoor air temperature of a conditioned space within a predefined comfort band (typically **18 В°C to 24 В°C** for human occupancy as per ASHRAE Standard 55), by converting a primary energy source into useful heat and distributing it through a controlled medium (water, air, or steam) to terminal units.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **Heating Process** is the closed-loop electro-mechanical sequence comprising:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box**
> A **Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC)** heating sub-system consists of (a) a **Heat Generator** (boiler, heat pump, furnace, or electric resistance unit), (b) a **Distribution Network** (pipes, ducts, pumps, valves), (c) **Terminal Heat Emitters** (radiators, FCUs, AHUs, radiant panels), and (d) a **Building Automation Control Loop** (sensors, controllers, actuators) governed by a BACnet/Modbus supervisory network.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: Your Building is a Human Body**
> Think of a building during winter exactly as you think of your own body in cold weather:
>
> - The **Boiler** is your *heart* вҖ” it pumps thermal energy (blood) around the system.
> - The **Pump / Fan** is your *circulatory system* вҖ” moves the heated fluid (blood) through pipes (veins).
> - The **Radiator / FCU** is your *skin* вҖ” releases the heat to the surrounding air (environment).
> - The **Room Thermostat** is your *brain's hypothalamus* вҖ” senses temperature and decides when to start/stop heating.
> - The **Building Automation Controller (BAC)** is your *autonomic nervous system* вҖ” it takes decisions without you consciously thinking.
>
> When the room becomes too cold (skin feels cold), the thermostat (brain) signals the boiler (heart) to fire up. When comfort is reached, it shuts down. This is exactly the closed-loop principle behind every modern BAS heating loop.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Heating Calculations**
> - **Specific heat of water:** $c_w$ = **4.186 kJ/(kgВ·K)**
> - **Specific heat of air:** $c_{air}$ = **1.005 kJ/(kgВ·K)**
> - **Density of water at 20 В°C:** $\rho_w$ = **998 kg/mВі**
> - **Density of air at 20 В°C:** $\rho_{air}$ = **1.204 kg/mВі**
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R$ = **8.314 kJ/(kmolВ·K)**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of Natural Gas:** = **50 MJ/kg**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma$ = **5.67 Г— 10вҒ»вҒё W/(mВІВ·KвҒҙ)**
> - **Standard human thermal comfort range:** **20 В°C to 24 В°C** dry-bulb

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Heat Transfer Mechanisms вҖ” The Three Pillars

Heating in buildings fundamentally relies on three heat-transfer modes. Every heating system, from a domestic geyser to a central HVAC plant, employs at least one (and usually all three) of these:

#### A. Conduction (Heat Transfer Through Solids)
Conduction is the transfer of thermal energy through a material **without bulk movement of the medium itself**. It is governed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**.

$$q_{cond} = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{d}$$

Where:
- $q_{cond}$ = conductive heat transfer rate in **W**
- $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material in **W/(mВ·K)**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow in **mВІ**
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across the material in **K** or **В°C**
- $d$ = thickness of the material in **m**

> **Real-World Utility:** Used to size wall/floor/roof insulation in a building envelope. The lower the *k*, the better the insulator.

#### B. Convection (Heat Transfer in Fluids)
Convection transfers heat by **bulk fluid movement** (forced or natural). It follows **Newton's Law of Cooling**.

$$q_{conv} = h_{c} \cdot A \cdot (T_{s} - T_{\infty})$$

Where:
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient in **W/(mВІВ·K)**
- $T_s$ = surface temperature in **K**
- $T_{\infty}$ = bulk fluid temperature in **K**

> **Real-World Utility:** Heat delivered by a radiator to room air, heat from a hot-water pipe to surrounding air.

#### C. Radiation (Heat Transfer by Electromagnetic Waves)
Radiation needs **no medium** and follows the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**.

$$q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_{s}^{4} - T_{sur}^{4})$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of the surface (**0 вүӨ Оө вүӨ 1**)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.67 Г— 10вҒ»вҒё W/(mВІВ·KвҒҙ)**
- $T_s, T_{sur}$ = absolute temperatures (in **Kelvin**) of surface and surroundings

> **Real-World Utility:** Underfloor radiant heating, infrared panels, solar gain through windows.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is your one-stop revision for all numericals on Heating Process.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat gained/lost | kW | Water/air heating load |
| 2 | $Q = V \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat load from volume flow | kW | Ducted air systems |
| 3 | $q = \dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{1000}$ | Heat loss through building fabric | kW | Wall/roof load |
| 4 | $Q_{latent} = m \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat for moisture | kW | Humidification |
| 5 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | % | Boiler sizing |
| 6 | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{heating}}{W_{input}}$ | Heat Pump Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless | Heat pump rating |
| 7 | $q_{cond} = \dfrac{kA\Delta T}{d}$ | Fourier conduction | W | Insulation design |
| 8 | $q_{conv} = h_c A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton cooling | W | Convection load |
| 9 | $q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | W | Radiant panels |
| 10 | $R_{total} = \sum \dfrac{d_i}{k_i}$ | Total thermal resistance | mВІВ·K/W | Composite wall |
| 11 | $U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total} + R_{si} + R_{se}}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | W/(mВІВ·K) | U-value of envelope |
| 12 | $SHR = \dfrac{Q_{sensible}}{Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}}$ | Sensible Heat Ratio | dimensionless | Coil selection |
| 13 | $T_{dbt} + \text{WBT} \rightarrow RH$ | Psychrometric relationship | various | Air state point |
| 14 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{Q_{sensible}}{\rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}$ | Required airflow rate | mВі/s | AHU/FCU sizing |

> **Note:** In all KTU answer sheets, **always** carry units through every calculation step. Examiners specifically deduct marks when a student writes "Q = 50" instead of "Q = 50 kW".

---

### 2.3 Heating Systems вҖ” The Engineering Taxonomy

KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the following heating generation methods, each with a distinct thermodynamic principle:

#### (a) Boiler-Based Hydronic Heating
A **closed-loop water (or steam) heating system** where a fossil-fuel or biomass-fired boiler heats water, which is pumped through pipes to terminal heat emitters.

**Key Components:**
- Boiler (fire-tube / water-tube / condensing)
- Circulation pump (single-speed / variable-speed VFD-driven)
- Expansion tank (pressurization)
- Three-way / two-way control valves
- Thermostatic Radiator Valves (TRVs)
- Header / manifold

#### (b) Heat Pump Heating
A **reverse-Carnot-cycle device** that extracts heat from a low-temperature source (ambient air, ground, water) and "pumps" it to a higher temperature for building use.

**COP Significance:** A COP of **4** means the heat pump delivers **4 kW of heat for every 1 kW of electricity** consumed вҖ” vastly more efficient than direct electric resistance heating.

#### (c) Furnace Heating
Direct-fired warm-air heater where combustion happens inside a heat exchanger and air is blown across it.

#### (d) Electric Resistance Heating
Direct conversion of electrical energy to heat via the Joule effect ($P = I^2 R$). Used in baseboard heaters, infrared panels, and heat trace.

#### (e) Solar Thermal Heating
Uses **flat-plate or evacuated-tube collectors** to capture solar radiation and transfer it to a fluid for storage or use.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Building Heat Load (Steady-State Method)

> **Problem Context:** A KTU exam-style question typically asks you to compute the heating load of a room. Let us derive the full heat-balance equation step by step.

The total heat that must be supplied to maintain the room at a desired temperature $T_{in}$ when outside temperature is $T_{out}$ is the sum of all heat losses plus the heat required for ventilation/infiltration air heating.

#### Step 1: Define the steady-state energy balance

In steady state, the heat supplied by the heating system equals the sum of all heat losses:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{roof} + Q_{floor} + Q_{window} + Q_{door} + Q_{vent} + Q_{infil}$$

#### Step 2: Compute each fabric heat loss using the U-value method

$$Q_{fabric} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

For a composite wall with multiple layers (plaster + brick + insulation + plaster), the total thermal resistance is:

$$R_{total} = R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{d_i}{k_i} + R_{se}$$

Therefore, the overall heat transfer coefficient is:

$$U = \dfrac{1}{R_{total}}$$

The heat loss through one wall becomes:

$$Q_{wall} = \dfrac{A_{wall} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})}{R_{total}}$$

#### Step 3: Compute the ventilation / infiltration heat loss

The mass flow rate of incoming cold air is:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{air}$$

The energy required to heat this cold air to room temperature:

$$Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$$

Substituting $\dot{V}_{air}$ in mВі/h and converting:

$$Q_{vent} = \dfrac{\rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T}{3600} \quad \text{(kW)}$$

#### Step 4: Aggregate and add a safety margin

$$Q_{design} = 1.1 \times Q_{total}$$

The factor **1.1 (10% margin)** accounts for thermal bridges, start-up transients, and unforeseen losses. KTU examiners frequently award this 10% margin mark only if explicitly mentioned.

#### Worked Numerical (Model Solution Format)

> **Given:**
> - Room dimensions: 5 m Г— 4 m Г— 3 m
> - $T_{in}$ = **22 В°C**, $T_{out}$ = **5 В°C**, so $\Delta T = 17$ K
> - One external wall: 5 m Г— 3 m = **15 mВІ**, single brick (U = **2.5 W/(mВІВ·K)**)
> - Roof: 5 m Г— 4 m = **20 mВІ**, concrete slab insulated (U = **0.4 W/(mВІВ·K)**)
> - One window: 1.5 m Г— 1.5 m = **2.25 mВІ**, double-glazed (U = **2.8 W/(mВІВ·K)**)
> - Infiltration air: **2 air-changes per hour (ACH)**
> - $c_{p,air}$ = **1.005 kJ/(kgВ·K)**, $\rho_{air}$ = **1.2 kg/mВі**

**Solution:**

Step A: Volume of room

$$V_{room} = 5 \times 4 \times 3 = 60 \text{ m}^3$$

Step B: Wall heat loss

$$Q_{wall} = 2.5 \times 15 \times 17 = 637.5 \text{ W}$$

Step C: Roof heat loss

$$Q_{roof} = 0.4 \times 20 \times 17 = 136 \text{ W}$$

Step D: Window heat loss

$$Q_{window} = 2.8 \times 2.25 \times 17 = 107.1 \text{ W}$$

Step E: Infiltration heat loss

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = \dfrac{120}{3600} = 0.0333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{infil} = 1.2 \times 0.0333 \times 1005 \times 17 = 682.7 \text{ W}$$

Step F: Total and design load

$$Q_{total} = 637.5 + 136 + 107.1 + 682.7 = 1563.3 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{design} = 1.1 \times 1563.3 = 1719.6 \text{ W} \approx 1.72 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Boiler Thermal Efficiency

Boiler efficiency compares the heat absorbed by water to the heat released by fuel:

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\text{Heat absorbed by water}}{\text{Heat released by fuel}} \times 100$$

Heat absorbed by water per unit time:

$$Q_{absorbed} = \dot{m}_{water} \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Heat released by fuel:

$$Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{CV}_{fuel}$$

Where $\text{CV}_{fuel}$ is the calorific value (e.g., for natural gas CV вүҲ **50 MJ/kg**, for coal вүҲ **25 MJ/kg**).

Therefore:

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{water} \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_{water}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \text{CV}_{fuel}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** A common student error is using the *Higher Heating Value (HHV)* when the problem specifies *Lower Heating Value (LHV)*. Always cross-check.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Heat Pump Coefficient of Performance (COP)

A heat pump delivers heat $Q_H$ to the building by extracting $Q_L$ from a low-temperature source using work input $W$. By the First Law of Thermodynamics:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W$$

The Coefficient of Performance for heating mode is:

$$COP_{heating} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

For a **Carnot (ideal) heat pump** operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$ (both in Kelvin):

$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

> **Practical Implication:** As the outdoor temperature $T_L$ drops, the denominator increases, and **COP decreases**. This is why heat pumps perform best in mild climates and lose efficiency on very cold days.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Integrated Building Heating Automation System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sensors Suite] --> B[BAS Controller]
    B --> C[Central Plant Controller]
    C --> D[Boiler or Heat Pump Unit]
    D --> E[Primary Distribution Loop]
    E --> F[Secondary Distribution Manifold]
    F --> G[Zone Control Valves]
    G --> H[Terminal Heat Emitters]
    H --> I[Conditioned Space]
    I --> A
    J[BACnet Supervisor Workstation] --> B
    K[Cloud IoT Dashboard] --> J
    L[Occupancy and CO2 Sensors] --> B

    A -. Temperature and Humidity .-> I
    B -. Setpoint and Feedback Signal .-> C
```

### 4.2 Hydronic Heating Loop with Closed-Loop Control Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boiler Unit] --> B[Supply Manifold]
    B --> C[Zone 1 Radiator]
    B --> D[Zone 2 FCU]
    B --> E[Zone 3 Underfloor Loop]
    C --> F[Return Manifold]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Return Pump]
    G --> A
    H[Outdoor Temp Sensor] --> I[Boiler Controller]
    J[Indoor Thermostat Zone 1] --> I
    K[Indoor Thermostat Zone 2] --> I
    L[Indoor Thermostat Zone 3] --> I
    I --> A

    style A fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#4FC3F7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Heat Pump Operating Cycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evaporator - Low T Source] --> B[Compressor - Work Input]
    B --> C[Condenser - Heat Rejection to Building]
    C --> D[Expansion Valve]
    D --> A
    E[Refrigerant Flow] -. R134a or R410A .-> A

    style B fill:#E57373,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#81C784,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Closed-Loop Heating Control Logic (PID Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint SP e.g. 22 C] --> B[Summing Junction]
    C[Room Temperature Sensor PV] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal e t]
    D --> E[PID Controller]
    E --> F[Control Signal u t]
    F --> G[Control Valve Actuator]
    G --> H[Plant Boiler or Valve]
    H --> I[Conditioned Space]
    I --> C

    style E fill:#BA68C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

### PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define the term "Boiler Efficiency" and write its mathematical expression. Mention the typical efficiency range of a modern condensing boiler.**

**Model Answer:**

Boiler efficiency is defined as the ratio of the useful heat absorbed by the water/steam to the total heat released by the fuel combustion, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100 = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{w} \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T_{w}}{\dot{m}_{f} \cdot CV_{f}} \times 100$$

**Typical Efficiency Ranges:**
- **Old cast-iron boilers:** 55% to 65%
- **Modern non-condensing boilers:** 78% to 85%
- **Modern condensing boilers:** **90% to 98%**

> *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for efficiency ranges)*

---

**Q2. List any three differences between a Heat Pump and a Direct Electric Resistance Heater.**

**Model Answer:**

| **#** | **Heat Pump** | **Electric Resistance Heater** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operates on the reverse-Carnot vapor-compression cycle | Operates on Joule's Law of heating ($I^2R$) |
| 2 | Can deliver **2 to 4 units** of heat per unit of electricity (COP = 2вҖ“4) | Delivers exactly **1 unit** of heat per unit of electricity (COP = 1) |
| 3 | Requires refrigerant, compressor, evaporator, condenser | Requires only a resistive element (nichrome wire) |
| 4 | Higher capital cost; longer payback | Very low capital cost; instant installation |

> *(Mark Distribution: 1 mark per correct contrasting pair, maximum 3 marks for 3 pairs)*

---

### PART B вҖ” Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” Heat Load Calculation and Boiler Sizing**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A conference room has dimensions **8 m Г— 6 m Г— 3.5 m** with one external wall of area **24 mВІ** (U = **2.0 W/(mВІВ·K)**), a glazed portion of **6 mВІ** (U = **3.5 W/(mВІВ·K)**), a roof of area **48 mВІ** (U = **0.35 W/(mВІВ·K)**), and an infiltration rate of **1.5 ACH**. The inside design temperature is **22 В°C** and the outside design temperature is **4 В°C**. Compute the total heating load including a 10% safety margin.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A boiler supplies the above heating load with water entering at **60 В°C** and leaving at **80 В°C** at a mass flow rate of **0.05 kg/s**. If the fuel used is natural gas with a calorific value of **50 MJ/kg** and the gas consumption rate is **0.0015 kg/s**, calculate the boiler efficiency. Comment on whether the boiler is suitable for commercial use.

---

#### **Model Solution A(a):**

**Given:**
- $L$ = 8 m, $B$ = 6 m, $H$ = 3.5 m
- $T_{in}$ = 22 В°C, $T_{out}$ = 4 В°C, so $\Delta T = 18$ K
- $A_{wall}$ = 24 mВІ, $U_{wall}$ = 2.0 W/(mВІВ·K)
- $A_{glass}$ = 6 mВІ, $U_{glass}$ = 3.5 W/(mВІВ·K)
- $A_{roof}$ = 48 mВІ, $U_{roof}$ = 0.35 W/(mВІВ·K)
- Infiltration = 1.5 ACH
- $\rho_{air}$ = 1.2 kg/mВі, $c_{p,air}$ = 1005 J/(kgВ·K)

**Step 1:** Wall heat loss

$$Q_{wall} = U_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} \cdot \Delta T = 2.0 \times 24 \times 18 = 864 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Glass heat loss

$$Q_{glass} = 3.5 \times 6 \times 18 = 378 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Roof heat loss

$$Q_{roof} = 0.35 \times 48 \times 18 = 302.4 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Infiltration loss

Room volume $V = 8 \times 6 \times 3.5 = 168$ mВі

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 1.5 \times 168 = 252 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.07 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{infil} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T = 1.2 \times 0.07 \times 1005 \times 18 = 1520.7 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5:** Total heat load

$$Q_{total} = 864 + 378 + 302.4 + 1520.7 = 3065.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6:** With 10% safety margin

$$Q_{design} = 1.1 \times 3065.1 = 3371.6 \text{ W} \approx 3.37 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### **Model Solution A(b):**

**Step 1:** Heat absorbed by water

$$Q_{abs} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T = 0.05 \times 4.186 \times (80 - 60) = 0.05 \times 4.186 \times 20$$

$$Q_{abs} = 4.186 \text{ kW} = 4186 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Heat supplied by fuel

$$Q_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{f} \cdot CV_{f} = 0.0015 \times 50{,}000 = 75 \text{ kW} = 75{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Boiler efficiency

$$\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{abs}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4186}{75000} \times 100 = 5.58\%$$

**Step 4:** Comment

The computed efficiency is only **5.58%**, which is **unacceptably low**. A practical commercial boiler must operate between **80% and 95%**. This boiler is therefore **not suitable for commercial use**; there is likely a data error or major heat loss (e.g., uninsulated stack, scaling on heat-transfer surfaces). The boiler needs immediate servicing, descaling, and combustion tuning.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to convert mВі/h to mВі/s before substituting. Always divide by 3600 explicitly.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the wall U-value directly without including the inside and outside surface resistances. In precise KTU solutions, $R_{si}$ = 0.13 mВІВ·K/W and $R_{se}$ = 0.04 mВІВ·K/W are sometimes expected.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating units in every line вҖ” a silent unit error can cost you **2 marks** even if the numerical answer is correct.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Omitting the 10% safety margin. Examiners often have a separate 1-mark tick for "design margin / safety factor mentioned."

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Heat Pump Analysis and Control**

**(a)** [7 Marks] An air-source heat pump is used to heat a residential building. The heat pump delivers **12 kW** of heat to the building while consuming **3 kW** of electrical input. Calculate its Coefficient of Performance (COP) and compare it with a theoretical Carnot heat pump operating between an indoor temperature of **22 В°C** and an outdoor temperature of **5 В°C**. Comment on the Second-Law efficiency.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain how a Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID) controller regulates the discharge air temperature of an AHU heating coil to maintain a setpoint of **30 В°C** under varying outdoor conditions. Define the PID equation and state the role of each term.

---

#### **Model Solution B(a):**

**Step 1:** Actual COP

$$COP_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_{heating}}{W_{input}} = \dfrac{12}{3} = 4.0$$

**Step 2:** Carnot COP

Convert temperatures to Kelvin:
- $T_H = 22 + 273 = 295$ K
- $T_L = 5 + 273 = 278$ K

$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{295}{295 - 278} = \dfrac{295}{17} = 17.35$$

**Step 3:** Second-Law efficiency

$$\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.0}{17.35} \times 100 \approx 23.06\%$$

**Step 4:** Comment

The actual heat pump operates at only **23%** of the theoretical maximum. This is typical for real-world vapor-compression systems, which suffer from compressor inefficiencies, subcooling/superheating losses, and frosting of the outdoor coil. A COP of **4.0** is still excellent compared to electric resistance heating (COP = 1), making the heat pump roughly **4Г— more energy-efficient**.

---

#### **Model Solution B(b):**

**PID Equation (Standard Form):**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \cdot \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Role of Each Term:**

| **Term** | **Mathematical Meaning** | **Engineering Role** |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional ($K_p \cdot e$)** | Response proportional to current error | Provides *immediate correction*; higher $K_p$ gives faster response but causes overshoot |
| **Integral ($K_i \int e \, dt$)** | Response proportional to accumulated past error | **Eliminates steady-state offset** вҖ” ensures discharge temperature *exactly* hits setpoint |
| **Derivative ($K_d \cdot de/dt$)** | Response proportional to rate of change of error | **Predicts future error** and damps oscillations; acts as a stabilizer |

**Block Diagram Description (Written Form for Answer Book):**

1. **Setpoint (SP)** = 30 В°C is fed to the summing junction.
2. **Process Variable (PV)** = discharge air temperature measured by a thermistor at AHU outlet.
3. **Error signal** $e(t) = SP - PV$ is computed.
4. The **PID controller** computes $u(t)$ using the formula above.
5. The **control signal** modulates a **modulating control valve** on the hot-water coil.
6. As the valve opens, more hot water flows through the coil, raising discharge temperature.
7. The change in discharge temperature is sensed and fed back, closing the loop.

**Typical Tuning Outcome:**
- **P-only** вҶ’ quick response, small steady-state error (offset)
- **PI** вҶ’ no offset, slight overshoot
- **Full PID** вҶ’ fast, accurate, well-damped response suitable for AHU coil control

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout for PID Question**
> - **Do not confuse the P, I, D actions with anything else.** Many students incorrectly write "P = Pressure, I = Inertia, D = Density" вҖ” examiners deduct heavily for this.
> - **Always state the units of $K_p, K_i, K_d$ explicitly.** $K_p$ is dimensionless or in %/В°C, $K_i$ in 1/s, $K_d$ in seconds.
> - **Mention anti-windup** (1 mark) when discussing integral action вҖ” this is a favorite KTU follow-up.
> - **Draw the feedback loop with an arrow going back to the summing junction** вҖ” a missing feedback arrow costs you 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary вҖ” Heating Process and Applications**
>
> - **Definition:** A Building Heating Process is the integrated, sensor-driven thermal energy delivery system that maintains indoor temperature within the **ASHRAE comfort band of 20вҖ“24 В°C**.
> - **Three Modes of Heat Transfer:** Conduction (Fourier's Law), Convection (Newton's Cooling Law), Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law). Real systems use all three simultaneously.
> - **Heat Load Formula:** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ (sensible), $Q = m \cdot h_{fg}$ (latent). Always include a **10% safety margin** in KTU design problems.
> - **U-value Method:** $U = 1 / R_{total}$ where $R_{total} = R_{si} + \Sigma(d_i/k_i) + R_{se}$. Low U-value = better insulation.
> - **Boiler Efficiency:** $\eta = Q_{absorbed}/Q_{fuel}$. Modern condensing boilers reach **90вҖ“98%**.
> - **Heat Pump COP:** $COP = Q_{heating}/W_{input}$. Carnot limit: $COP_{Carnot} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ вҖ” both temperatures in **Kelvin**.
> - **Ventilation Load:** $Q_{vent} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$. Convert mВі/h to mВі/s by dividing by 3600.
> - **PID Controller:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \cdot de/dt$. P corrects immediately, I eliminates offset, D damps oscillations.
> - **Hydronic System:** Boiler вҶ’ Pump вҶ’ Manifold вҶ’ Control Valve вҶ’ Radiator/FCU вҶ’ Return.
> - **Air-Source Heat Pump:** Performance degrades as outdoor temperature falls вҖ” critical design consideration for KTU numericals.
> - **Standard Reference Temperatures:** Indoor **22 В°C (295 K)**, Outdoor winter design **5 В°C (278 K)** unless stated otherwise.
> - **Critical Units to Memorise:** k = W/(mВ·K), U = W/(mВІВ·K), R = mВІВ·K/W, Q = W or kW, COP = dimensionless.
> - **Common KTU Traps:** Forgetting unit conversion, omitting safety factor, misidentifying LHV vs HHV, swapping $T_H$ and $T_L$ in COP formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Savings Concept and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Energy Savings Concept and Methods # SECTION 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Savings in Building Automation** refers to the deliberate reduction of electrical, thermal, and mechanical energy consumption in a building through the orchestrated integration of sensors, controllers, actuators, and software-based management strategies, achieved without compromising occupant comfort, safety, or functional performance of the built environment. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEIT526 вҖ“ Building Automation)**, energy savings is positioned as the *primary economic and environmental justification* for deploying a Building Automation System (BAS). The 2024 syllabus frames it as a quantifiable outcome derived from the synergy of: - **Real-time monitoring** (sensors, smart meters) - **Intelligent control** (PID loops, scheduling, optimization algorithms) - **Actuation** (VAV dampers, variable speed drives, relay-controlled luminaires) The governing benchmark metric is **Energy Use Intensity (EUI)**, expressed in **kWh/mВІ/year** (or **kBtu/ftВІ/year** in imperial units). The target reductions under most green-building codes fall in the range of **30 % to 50 %** compared to a non-automated baseline. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Auto-Pilot House" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine driving a car with no fuel gauge вҖ” you drive until the engine sputters. Now imagine a car with a *smart fuel manager* that automatically shuts off the engine at red lights, switches to electric mode in traffic, and optimizes gear shifts based on terrain. That second car is what a Building Automation System does for a building's energy. In a conventional (non-automated) building: - Lights remain ON in unoccupied rooms - HVAC runs at full capacity even when outside temperature is mild - No feedback exists between actual demand and supply In an automated building: - Motion sensors switch off lights automatically - COвӮӮ and temperature sensors modulate fresh-air intake - Schedulers dim lights in response to daylight ## 1.3 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) | KPI | Symbol | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | Energy Use Intensity | EUI | **kWh/mВІ/year** | | Peak Demand Reduction | PDR | **kW** or **%** | | Payback Period | PB | **years** | | Return on Investment | ROI | **%** | | Power Factor | pf | dimensionless (0вҖ“1) | | Lighting Power Density | LPD | **W/mВІ** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote units explicitly in derivations. Marks are routinely deducted for missing units, especially in numerical Part B problems. ## 1.4 Classification of Energy Savings Methods (Overview) The KTU Module 4 syllabus segregates energy savings into **five core pillars**: 1. **Scheduling-based savings** (time-of-day control) 2. **Occupancy-based savings** (presence detection) 3. **Daylight harvesting** (luminosity-responsive dimming) 4. **HVAC optimization** (variable air volume, setpoint reset, free cooling) 5. **Load shedding / peak-demand management** Each of these will be expanded rigorously in **Section 2**. # SECTION 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Energy-Conservation Principle Energy savings in a building can be modelled as a *conservation of demand equation*: $$ E_{\text{saved}} = E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{automated}} $$ where: - $E_{\text{baseline}}$ = energy consumed by a building without automation (kWh/year) - $E_{\text{automated}}$ = energy consumed with the BAS active (kWh/year) > [!NOTE] > Both quantities must be measured under **identical occupancy, weather, and operational schedules**. Failing to normalize is the single most common mistake in energy audits. ## 2.2 Structured Theoretical Breakdown вҖ” The Five Pillars ### Pillar 1 вҖ” Scheduling-Based Savings - BAS executes a pre-programmed **time schedule** (e.g., 08:00 ON, 19:00 OFF). - Saves energy by eliminating the "always-on" waste during unoccupied hours. - Typical saving range: **10 %вҖ“20 %** of lighting load. ### Pillar 2 вҖ” Occupancy-Based Savings - **PIR sensors** (Passive Infrared) and **ultrasonic sensors** detect human presence. - When a zone is vacant for a preset timeout (commonly **15вҖ“20 minutes**), HVAC and lighting for that zone are commanded OFF. - Typical saving range: **20 %вҖ“30 %** of HVAC + lighting combined. ### Pillar 3 вҖ” Daylight Harvesting - **Photocells / lux sensors** measure ambient daylight at the work-plane. - Dimmable ballasts (now largely replaced by **DALI** or **0вҖ“10 V** drivers) reduce artificial-lighting output to maintain a target illuminance (commonly **400вҖ“500 lux** at desk level). - Typical saving range: **25 %вҖ“40 %** of perimeter-zone lighting energy. ### Pillar 4 вҖ” HVAC Optimization The largest energy consumer (typically **40 %вҖ“60 %** of total building energy). Sub-techniques include: - **Variable Air Volume (VAV):** modulates supply-air quantity instead of reheating. - **Chilled Water Temperature Reset:** raises chilled-water setpoint when load is low. - **Economizer Cycle / Free Cooling:** uses outside air when $\Delta T$ condition is satisfied. - **Supply-air Temperature Reset.** ### Pillar 5 вҖ” Peak Demand Shaving - The BAS monitors real-time demand against a contracted maximum (kVA) limit. - Non-critical loads (e.g., water heaters, corridor lighting) are temporarily shed during peak tariff windows. - Saves on **demand charges**, not just kWh charges. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS** вҖ” Memorize this table verbatim. These appear frequently in KTU End Semester Examinations. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Electrical energy = Power Г— Time | kWh | | 2 | $E_{\text{saved}} = E_{\text{base}} - E_{\text{auto}}$ | Net annual energy saved | kWh/year | | 3 | $\% \text{Savings} = \dfrac{E_{\text{saved}}}{E_{\text{base}}} \times 100$ | Percentage energy reduction | % | | 4 | $PB = \dfrac{C_{\text{BAS}}}{\text{Annual Cost Saving}}$ | Simple Payback Period | years | | 5 | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Net Annual Benefit}}{C_{\text{BAS}}} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % | | 6 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{\text{cooling}}}{W_{\text{input}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (chiller) | вҖ” | | 7 | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{\text{annual}}}{A_{\text{floor}}}$ | Energy Use Intensity | kWh/mВІ | | 8 | $LPD = \dfrac{P_{\text{luminaire}}}{A_{\text{zone}}}$ | Lighting Power Density | W/mВІ | | 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{economizer}} = T_{\text{oA}} < T_{\text{RA}} - \Delta T_{\text{mix}}$ | Economizer activation condition | В°C | | 10 | $S_{\text{daylight}} = \eta_{\text{ballast}} \cdot \Phi_{\text{lux}}$ | Daylight harvesting efficacy factor | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **ABSOLUTE VALUE PITFALL:** Never use the raw pipe character `\vert` inside KTU exam answer sheets вҖ” use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX rendering, or write "mod x" in plain handwriting. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are routinely applied in: - **LEED / GRIHA green-building certification audits** (India) - **Energy Performance Contracting (EPC)** under **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** PAT scheme - **ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems** compliance - **Smart-grid demand-response programs** where buildings shed load in response to utility signals # SECTION 3 вҖ” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Annual Energy Saving from Occupancy-Based Lighting Control > **Problem Context:** A 12-storey commercial building has 240 fluorescent luminaires, each rated **40 W**. Without automation, lights operate **12 hours/day, 7 days/week, 52 weeks/year**. With occupancy sensors + scheduling, average effective ON-time drops to **8 hours/day**. **Step 1 вҖ” Compute the baseline annual energy.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{base}} &= N_{\text{lum}} \times P_{\text{lum}} \\ &= 240 \times 40\ \text{W} \\ &= 9\,600\ \text{W} = 9.6\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{base}} &= 12 \times 7 \times 52 \\ &= 4\,368\ \text{h/year} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{base}} &= P_{\text{base}} \times t_{\text{base}} \\ &= 9.6 \times 4\,368 \\ &= 41\,932.8\ \text{kWh/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 вҖ” Compute the automated annual energy.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{auto}} &= 8 \times 7 \times 52 \\ &= 2\,912\ \text{h/year} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{auto}} &= 9.6 \times 2\,912 \\ &= 27\,955.2\ \text{kWh/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 вҖ” Compute annual energy saved.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{saved}} &= E_{\text{base}} - E_{\text{auto}} \\ &= 41\,932.8 - 27\,955.2 \\ &= 13\,977.6\ \text{kWh/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 вҖ” Compute percentage saving.** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{Savings} &= \frac{E_{\text{saved}}}{E_{\text{base}}} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{13\,977.6}{41\,932.8} \times 100 \\ &= 33.33\ \% \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** Annual saving = **13 977.6 kWh**, equivalent to a **33.33 % reduction** in lighting energy. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Payback Period Calculation > **Continuation:** The BAS retrofit cost вӮ№18,00,000 (Eighteen Lakhs). Electricity tariff = вӮ№8.50/kWh. **Step 1 вҖ” Compute annual monetary saving.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{вӮ№}_{\text{saved}} &= E_{\text{saved}} \times \text{Tariff} \\ &= 13\,977.6 \times 8.50 \\ &= вӮ№1\,18\,809.6/\text{year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 вҖ” Apply payback formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} PB &= \frac{C_{\text{BAS}}}{\text{Annual вӮ№ Saving}} \\ &= \frac{18\,00\,000}{1\,18\,809.6} \\ &= 15.15\ \text{years} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Note:** A payback of 15.15 years for lighting alone is **not commercially attractive**. This illustrates why **multi-load aggregation** (lighting + HVAC + plug loads) is essential вҖ” the payback typically drops to **4вҖ“6 years** for an integrated BAS. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Daylight Harvesting Photometric Saving > **Problem Context:** A perimeter office zone has 60 LED panels, each **36 W**, target illuminance **500 lux**. Average daylight contribution at desk = **250 lux**. Dimming ratio = $\frac{500 - 250}{500} = 0.5$. **Step 1 вҖ” Dimmed power per luminaire.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{dim}} &= P_{\text{rated}} \times (1 - R_{\text{d}}) \\ &= 36 \times (1 - 0.5) \\ &= 18\ \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 вҖ” Annual saving (assume 10 daylight-effective hours/day, 250 working days).** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{\text{day}} &= 10 \times 250 = 2\,500\ \text{h} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{saved}} &= N \times \Delta P \times t_{\text{day}} \\ &= 60 \times (36 - 18) \times 2\,500 \\ &= 60 \times 18 \times 2\,500 \\ &= 2\,70\,000\ \text{Wh} = 270\ \text{kWh/year} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!NOTE] > The following matrix is the **single most important summary table** for KTU Module 4 viva and Part B questions. | Method | Typical Saving (%) | Typical Payback (years) | Capital Intensity | Best Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | Scheduling | 10 вҖ“ 20 | < 1 | Very Low | Office buildings | | Occupancy Sensors | 20 вҖ“ 30 | 1 вҖ“ 2 | Low | Conference rooms, restrooms | | Daylight Harvesting | 25 вҖ“ 40 | 3 вҖ“ 5 | Medium | Perimeter offices, atriums | | HVAC VAV Optimization | 30 вҖ“ 50 | 4 вҖ“ 7 | High | Large commercial HVAC | | Economizer / Free Cooling | 15 вҖ“ 25 | 2 вҖ“ 4 | Medium | Data centres, malls | | Peak Demand Shedding | 5 вҖ“ 15 (kVA) | 2 вҖ“ 3 | Medium | Industrial, hospitals | | **Integrated BAS** | **40 вҖ“ 60** | **4 вҖ“ 6** | **High** | **All building typologies** | # SECTION 4 вҖ” Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram вҖ” Building Automation Energy-Management Hierarchy The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture through which a BAS achieves energy savings. It maps the sensor вҶ’ controller вҶ’ actuator feedback loop and the supervisory optimization layer. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Building Energy Sources
Grid, DG, Solar, UPS] --> B[Energy Metering
kWh, kVA, pf Sensors] B --> C[Building Automation Controller
BAC, PLC, DDC] C --> D1[HVAC Subsystem Controller
Chiller, AHU, VAV, FCU] C --> D2[Lighting Controller
DALI, 0-10V, Relays] C --> D3[Plug-Load Controller
Smart PDU, Relays] C --> D4[Shading / Blind Controller
Motors, LUX Sensors] D1 --> E[Energy Saving Strategies
Scheduling, Occupancy, Reset] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[Actuator Output Commands
Damper, Valve, Dimmer, Contactor] F --> G[Field Devices
