# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024]**
*Define Energy Use Intensity (EUI) and state its significance in benchmarking building efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Energy Use Intensity (EUI)** is defined as the total annual energy consumed by a building divided by its total floor area.

$$
EUI = \frac{\text{Total Annual Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Total Floor Area (m}^2\text{)}}
$$

**Units:** kWh/mВІ/year.
**Significance:** EUI is a *normalized* metric that allows comparison between buildings of different sizes, climatic zones, and occupancy types. It serves as the *primary KPI* in ASHRAE 90.1 benchmarking, LEED performance verification, and ECBC (India) compliance. A lower EUI indicates higher efficiency. Typical EUI for an Indian office: **100вҖ“180 kWh/mВІ/year**; BEE 5-star rated buildings target **< 90 kWh/mВІ/year**. *(1 mark for formula + 1 mark for unit + 1 mark for significance)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023]**
*List any three strategies for improving energy efficiency in HVAC systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** on AHU fans, chilled water pumps, and condenser water pumps to modulate speed based on part-load demand (saves up to 50% fan/pump energy via the cubic affinity law).
2. **Chilled Water Temperature Reset** вҖ” Raising the leaving chilled water temperature (e.g., from 7 В°C to 9 В°C) during low outdoor temperatures reduces compressor lift and saves 2вҖ“4% energy per В°C reset.
3. **Economizer / Free Cooling Cycle** вҖ” Utilizing cool outdoor air (enthalpy < 26 kJ/kg) to bypass mechanical refrigeration when conditions permit.
*(1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) вҖ” 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ May 2024 Model Question]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop architecture of a Building Energy Management System (BEMS)** for achieving continuous efficiency improvement. Describe the role of *sensors, controllers, actuators, and analytics engine* in this loop. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A centrifugal chiller has a COP of **5.8** at full load. The chiller serves a building cooling load of **800 TR (Tons of Refrigeration)**. Calculate:
>   (i) The electrical power input in **kW** at full load.
>   (ii) The new power input if a VFD on the condenser water pump reduces its speed to **75%** of full speed, applying the affinity law (assume pump power is **12%** of total chiller power consumption).
>   (iii) The annual cost saving in вӮ№ if the chiller operates **4500 hours/year** and electricity cost is **вӮ№9/kWh**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-A (a) вҖ” 7 Marks

The **Building Energy Management System (BEMS)** operates on a continuous **Sense вҶ’ Analyze вҶ’ Decide вҶ’ Actuate** cycle.

**1. Sensor Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Temperature sensors** (PT100, thermistor): Zone and duct air, chilled water, hot water.
- **Humidity sensors**: Return air and outdoor air.
- **COвӮӮ sensors**: For demand-controlled ventilation.
- **Lux sensors**: Daylight harvesting in lighting zones.
- **Occupancy sensors**: PIR and ultrasonic.
- **Energy meters**: Main incomer, sub-circuits, and individual equipment (chiller, AHU, lifts).

**2. Controller Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **DDC (Direct Digital Controllers)** at field level вҖ” execute local loops (e.g., room temperature control).
- **PLCs** for sequencing of chillers, pumps, and DG sets.
- **Edge Gateways** for protocol translation (BACnet вҶ” Modbus вҶ” LonWorks вҶ” MQTT).
- **Central SCADA/EMS server** for global coordination and analytics.

**3. Analytics & Optimization Engine (1.5 marks):**
- Receives historian data (time-series DB).
- Runs **rule-based logic** (e.g., "if zone unoccupied for 15 min, set setback temperature").
- Runs **ML-based predictive models** for occupancy, weather, and load forecasting.
- Implements **Model-Predictive Control (MPC)** for optimum chiller staging.

**4. Actuator Layer (1.5 marks):**
- **Modulating dampers** for outdoor/return air mixing.
- **Two-way and three-way control valves** on chilled water coils.
- **VFDs** on motors (cubic law energy savings).
- **Contactor-based relays** for lighting and exhaust fan switching.

**5. Feedback Verification (1 mark):**
- KPIs (EUI, kWh/TR, peak kVA) are auto-calculated and trended.
- **FDD engine** flags anomalies (e.g., stuck damper, oversized valve) for corrective action.

**Diagrammatic Reference:** See SECTION 4.1 above for the block architecture.

---

#### Solution to Q-A (b) вҖ” 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- COP = 5.8
- Cooling Load = 800 TR
- Pump power fraction = 12% of chiller power
- VFD reduces pump speed to 75% of full speed
- Operating hours = 4500 h/year
- Electricity cost = вӮ№9/kWh

**Step 1 вҖ” Convert cooling load to kW thermal.**
1 TR = 3.517 kW

$$
Q_{\text{cool}} = 800 \times 3.517 = 2813.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Compute full-load electrical input to chiller.**

$$
W_{\text{chiller, FL}} = \frac{Q_{\text{cool}}}{COP} = \frac{2813.6}{5.8} = 485.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Extract full-load pump power.**

$$
W_{\text{pump, FL}} = 0.12 \times 485.1 = 58.21 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Apply cubic affinity law to compute new pump power.**

$$
W_{\text{pump, NL}} = W_{\text{pump, FL}} \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 58.21 \times (0.75)^3
$$

$$
(0.75)^3 = 0.421875
$$

$$
W_{\text{pump, NL}} = 58.21 \times 0.421875 = 24.56 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Compute new total chiller system power (compressor unchanged, pump reduced).**

$$
W_{\text{total, NL}} = (W_{\text{chiller}} - W_{\text{pump,FL}}) + W_{\text{pump,NL}}
$$

$$
W_{\text{total, NL}} = (485.1 - 58.21) + 24.56 = 426.89 + 24.56 = 451.45 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Compute power saved per hour.**

$$
\Delta W = 485.1 - 451.45 = 33.65 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Compute annual energy saved.**

$$
E_{\text{saved}} = 33.65 \times 4500 = 1{,}51{,}425 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 8 вҖ” Compute annual monetary savings.**

$$
\text{Cost Saved} = 1{,}51{,}425 \times 9 = вӮ№13{,}62{,}825/\text{year} \approx вӮ№13.63 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- Full-load power: **485.1 kW**
- New power (after VFD): **451.45 kW**
- Annual savings: **вӮ№13.63 lakh/year** *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 distribution)*

---

### Question B (Option 2 вҖ” Alternative Choice) вҖ” 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **seven pillars of Building Efficiency Improvement** as applied to a commercial office complex. For each pillar, cite at least one *contemporary technology* that enables it. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A building's energy audit reveals the following loads and annual consumptions:
>   - Lighting: 1,80,000 kWh/year
>   - AHU Fans (constant speed): 2,40,000 kWh/year
>   - Chilled Water Pumps (constant speed): 1,20,000 kWh/year
>
>   Propose a **three-stage retrofit plan** with the following targets:
>   1. Stage 1: LED lighting retrofit (saves 60% of lighting kWh)
>   2. Stage 2: VFD on AHU fans at 70% speed during average occupancy
>   3. Stage 3: VFD on pumps at 80% speed
>
>   Calculate the **total annual energy saved (kWh)** and the **percentage reduction in total building energy consumption**. Electricity cost = **вӮ№8.5/kWh**. Compute the **annual monetary savings** in вӮ№. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q-B (a) вҖ” 7 Marks

**The Seven Pillars of Building Efficiency Improvement:**

**1. Energy Auditing & Benchmarking (1 mark):**
A Level 1, 2, or 3 audit (per ASHRAE Standard 211) establishes baseline EUI, identifies waste, and prioritizes retrofits. *Tool:* **EnergyCAP, RETScreen, or custom EMS dashboards.**

**2. HVAC Optimization (1 mark):**
Largest energy consumer (~50вҖ“60%). *Technologies:* **VFDs, magnetic-bearing chillers, EC fans, geothermal heat pumps, radiant cooling, chilled beams.**

**3. Lighting Control (1 mark):**
*Technologies:* **LED luminaires (90+ CRI), DALI-2 or 0-10V dimming, Bluetooth Mesh occupancy sensors, KNX-based scene controllers.**

**4. Building Envelope Upgrade (1 mark):**
*Technologies:* **Double-glazed low-E units, vacuum insulated panels, cool-roof coatings, phase-change material (PCM) wallboards, aerogel insulation.**

**5. Renewable Integration (1 mark):**
*Technologies:* **Rooftop solar PV with net-metering, building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV), solar thermal for DHW, micro-wind turbines.**

**6. Smart Metering & Submetering (1 mark):**
*Technologies:* **IoT-enabled cloud meters (Schneider PM5000, Honeywell E-Mon), LoRaWAN/NB-IoT wireless submetering, AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure).**

**7. Continuous Commissioning (CCx) & FDD (1 mark):**
*Technologies:* **SkySpark, KMC Commander, DGLux, EcoStruxure, Fault Detection algorithms using ASHRAE Guideline 36 rule sets.**

**Conclusion (included for 1 extra):** An integrated BMS with *cross-domain analytics* ties all seven pillars into a self-correcting efficiency ecosystem.

---

#### Solution to Q-B (b) вҖ” 7 Marks

**Given Loads:**
- Lighting: 1,80,000 kWh/yr
- AHU Fans: 2,40,000 kWh/yr
- CHW Pumps: 1,20,000 kWh/yr
- **Total baseline consumption:** 1,80,000 + 2,40,000 + 1,20,000 = **5,40,000 kWh/yr**

**Stage 1 вҖ” LED Retrofit (60% saving on lighting):**

$$
E_{\text{saved,L}} = 1{,}80{,}000 \times 0.60 = 1{,}08{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Stage 2 вҖ” VFD on AHU Fans at 70% speed:**

$$
E_{\text{saved,F}} = 2{,}40{,}000 \times \left[1 - (0.7)^3\right] = 2{,}40{,}000 \times [1 - 0.343] = 2{,}40{,}000 \times 0.657 = 1{,}57{,}680 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Stage 3 вҖ” VFD on Pumps at 80% speed:**

$$
E_{\text{saved,P}} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times \left[1 - (0.8)^3\right] = 1{,}20{,}000 \times [1 - 0.512] = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.488 = 58{,}560 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Total Energy Saved:**

$$
E_{\text{total saved}} = 1{,}08{,}000 + 1{,}57{,}680 + 58{,}560 = 3{,}24{,}240 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{3{,}24{,}240}{5{,}40{,}000} \times 100 = 60.04\%
$$

**Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$
\text{Cost Saved} = 3{,}24{,}240 \times 8.5 = вӮ№27{,}56{,}040/\text{year} \approx вӮ№27.56 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- Total energy saved: **3,24,240 kWh/yr**
- Percentage reduction: **~60%**
- Annual monetary savings: **вӮ№27.56 lakh/year** *(7 marks: 1+1+1+1+1+1+1 distributed across stages and final calculation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout вҖ” Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state kWh/yr, kW, вӮ№, TR. Mark deduction = 0.5 per missing unit.
> 2. **Wrong affinity exponent:** Students frequently use $(N_2/N_1)^2$ (pressure) instead of $(N_2/N_1)^3$ (power). **Power = SpeedВі** is the *most-asked* relationship.
> 3. **Mixing up COP and EER:** $1 \text{ EER} = 0.293 \text{ COP}$ (or $COP = 3.412 \times EER$). Examiners *expect* you to know the conversion.
> 4. **Ignoring chiller part-load:** At 50% load, real chiller efficiency is *better* than at full load (IPLV > full-load COP). Always cite this for kWh/ton-hour calculations.
> 5. **In 14-mark answers, missing the diagram:** A block diagram of BEMS architecture carries **2вҖ“3 easy marks** вҖ” *never skip it*.
> 6. **Annualization errors:** Operating hours for fans (5000вҖ“6000 h), pumps (4500вҖ“5000 h), and chillers (3000вҖ“4000 h) differ. Use the *correct* operating hour profile.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Building Efficiency Improvement (Module 4):**
> - вң… **EUI = kWh/mВІ/year** is the *universal benchmark metric*.
> - вң… **COP = Q/W (dimensionless)**; **EER uses BTU/W**; **1 COP = 3.412 EER**.
> - вң… **Affinity law: Power вҲқ SpeedВі** вҶ’ 30% speed reduction вҮ’ ~66% power reduction.
> - вң… **HVAC = 50вҖ“60%** of building energy; **Lighting = 20вҖ“25%**; **Plug loads = 10вҖ“15%**.
> - вң… **VFDs** are the *highest-ROI* retrofit for fans and pumps (payback 2вҖ“4 years).
> - вң… **LED retrofits** save 50вҖ“70% lighting kWh; combined with occupancy sensors, up to 80%.
> - вң… **Chilled water reset** saves 2вҖ“4% compressor energy per В°C of CHWS temperature increase.
> - вң… **Economizers** (enthalpy-based preferred in humid climates like Kerala).
> - вң… **Simple Payback = Investment / Annual Savings**; **SPP < 5 years** is *good*; **< 3 years is excellent**.
> - вң… **ECBC, ASHRAE 90.1, ISO 50001, LEED v4.1** are the *key compliance standards*.
> - вң… **FDD** detects stuck dampers, leaking valves, simultaneous heating-cooling, oversized equipment.
> - вң… **Continuous Commissioning (CCx)** delivers sustained 10вҖ“20% savings *annually* without new capital.
> - вң… **BEM tools:** EnergyPlus, eQuest, IES VE, DesignBuilder, OpenStudio.
> - вң… **Demand Factor, Diversity Factor, Load Factor** influence kVA demand and electricity bills.
> - вң… **PF correction to 0.95+** avoids utility penalties and reduces line losses.
> - вң… **Net-metering** (KSERC regulations in Kerala) enables ROI on rooftop solar PV.
> - вң… **Kerala-specific tip:** High humidity drives latent load вҖ” *enthalpy-based economizer* + *dehumidification control* are critical.

> **End of Module 4 Notes вҖ” Building Efficiency Improvement**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) | Outcome-Based Education | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy: L3 (Apply) вҶ’ L5 (Evaluate)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Green Building Concept and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEIT526 - BUILDING AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Green Building Concept and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Green Building** (also called a *Sustainable Building* or *High-Performance Building*) is a structure that is designed, constructed, operated, renovated, or repurposed in an environmentally responsible and resource-efficient manner throughout its entire life cycle. It integrates sustainable site planning, energy efficiency, water conservation, material optimisation, indoor environmental quality (IEQ), and waste minimisation to reduce its overall ecological footprint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):** A green building is one which uses less water, optimises energy efficiency, conserves natural resources, generates less waste, and provides healthier spaces for occupants when compared to a conventional building of similar typology, as evaluated through a certified rating framework such as **LEED, GRIHA, BREEAM, WELL, or IGBC**.

Mathematically, the **Green-ness (G)** of a building can be conceptually expressed as:

$$ G = \frac{E_{saved} + W_{saved} + C_{reduced} + R_{reused}}{LCC} $$

where $E_{saved}$ = energy saved, $W_{saved}$ = water saved, $C_{reduced}$ = carbon reduction, $R_{reused}$ = recycled content, and $LCC$ = Life Cycle Cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Living Tree" Model

> [!NOTE]
> рҹҢі **Intuition for a First-Time Reader:**
> Think of a green building as a **mechanical tree** вҖ” a living organism embedded in the city.
>
> - **Roots** вҶ’ Strong, sustainable *foundation* and *site integration* with the soil.
> - **Trunk** вҶ’ The *structural skeleton*, built using recycled steel, fly-ash bricks, or bamboo.
> - **Leaves** вҶ’ **Solar PV panels** on the roof вҖ” capturing sunlight (energy harvesting).
> - **Stomata (pores)** вҶ’ **Smart ventilation louvers** and operable windows вҖ” passive cooling.
> - **Sap flow** вҶ’ **Plumbing network** recycling rainwater and greywater.
> - **Oxygen output** вҶ’ Net-zero carbon emissions and healthy indoor air.
> - **Mulch around the tree** вҶ’ **Permeable pavements and landscape** that absorb storm-water.
> - **Birds nesting** вҶ’ **Happy occupants** with abundant daylight and fresh air.
>
> A *conventional building* is a sealed concrete box that consumes resources and expels waste; a *green building* behaves like a **tree** вҖ” it draws what it needs, recycles internally, and gives back to its environment.

## 1.3 Need for Green Buildings вҖ” Why This Topic Matters

| Conventional Building (Status Quo) | Green Building (Target) |
|---|---|
| Consumes **40%** of global energy | Targets **net-zero operational energy** |
| Emits **~30%** of global COвӮӮ | Reduces embodied & operational carbon |
| Uses **drinking-quality water** for flushing | Uses **rainwater + greywater** for non-potable needs |
| Generates **construction waste** in landfills | Diverts **75вҖ“95%** waste from landfill |
| Artificial lighting 24/7 | Maximises **daylight harvesting** |
| Indoor COвӮӮ often >1000 ppm | Maintains **COвӮӮ < 800 ppm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sustainability Statistics (Bold for Memory):**
> - Buildings account for **~40%** of global energy consumption.
> - Buildings account for **~30%** of global greenhouse-gas (GHG) emissions.
> - Buildings consume **~16%** of the world's freshwater withdrawal.
> - Green buildings reduce energy consumption by **~30вҖ“50%** over conventional baselines.
> - Green buildings reduce water consumption by **~40%** over conventional baselines.

---

## 1.4 Key Differences Between Green, Smart, and Net-Zero Buildings

| Attribute | Green Building | Smart Building | Net-Zero Building |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Resource efficiency | Automation & user comfort | Self-sustaining energy |
| Core Focus | Materials, energy, water | IoT, sensors, controls | Renewable generation |
| Tech Stack | Passive + some active | Heavy on ICT/BMS | High renewables + storage |
| Certification | LEED, GRIHA, BREEAM | WiredScore, SmartScore | Net-Zero certification |

> A green building may or may not be *smart*; a smart building may or may not be *green*. **Net-zero is a stricter sub-set of green** where annual on-site renewable generation equals annual consumption.

---

## 1.5 The Six Pillars of Green Building (KTU Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> Every green building is judged on **six performance pillars** as per the KTU 2024 Building Automation syllabus:
>
> 1. **Sustainable Site (SS)** вҖ” Location, connectivity, ecology preservation.
> 2. **Water Efficiency (WE)** вҖ” Rainwater harvesting, low-flow fixtures, recycling.
> 3. **Energy & Atmosphere (EA)** вҖ” Energy-efficient HVAC, lighting, on-site renewables.
> 4. **Materials & Resources (MR)** вҖ” Recycled content, regional sourcing, waste diversion.
> 5. **Indoor Environmental Quality (EQ)** вҖ” Daylight, thermal comfort, low-VOC materials.
> 6. **Innovation in Design (ID)** вҖ” Exemplary performance, LEED-accredited professionals.

These six pillars directly map to the **LEED v4.1 BD+C** categories and are the same pillars tested in KTU end-semester questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework вҖ” The Green Building Design Philosophy

The design philosophy rests on **three concentric layers** of intervention, from passive (free) to active (technology-driven):

### Layer 1 вҖ” Passive Design (Highest Priority)
- Building **orientation** (long axis East-West to minimise heat gain).
- **Building envelope** insulation, shading devices, double-glazed low-E windows.
- **Daylight harvesting** through light shelves, skylights, and atrium design.
- **Natural ventilation** via stack effect, cross-ventilation, wind towers.
- **Thermal mass** for night-flush cooling in tropical climates like Kerala.

### Layer 2 вҖ” Efficient Active Systems
- **HVAC** with Variable Refrigerant Flow (VRF) / chilled-beam technology.
- **LED lighting** with daylight + occupancy sensors.
- **High-efficiency plumbing fixtures** (dual-flush WC, aerated taps, drip irrigation).
- **Energy Recovery Ventilators (ERVs)** for fresh-air energy recovery.

### Layer 3 вҖ” On-site Renewables + Smart Controls
- Rooftop **Solar PV** or **Solar Water Heaters**.
- **Building Management System (BMS)** with AI-based load prediction.
- **Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)**.
- **Smart meters** and IoT-driven demand response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** Always answer green-building questions in the order **Passive вҶ’ Efficient вҶ’ Renewable**. This is the energy-efficiency hierarchy followed in IGBC and GRIHA manuals and is the **examiner-preferred answer sequence**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only numerical toolkit** you will need for this topic. Memorise every row.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | Typical Green-Building Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy Use Intensity | $EUI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | kWh / mВІ / yr | $\leq 90$ (GRIHA-5) |
| 2 | Water Use Intensity | $WUI = \dfrac{W_{annual}}{A_{floor}}$ | kL / mВІ / yr | $\leq 4$ |
| 3 | Lighting Power Density | $LPD = \dfrac{P_{lighting}}{A_{floor}}$ | W / mВІ | 5вҖ“8 (office) |
| 4 | Overall U-value of wall | $U_{wall} = \dfrac{1}{R_{si} + \Sigma \dfrac{d_i}{k_i} + R_{se}}$ | W/mВІВ·K | $\leq 0.35$ for tropical |
| 5 | Window-to-Wall Ratio | $WWR = \dfrac{A_{window}}{A_{wall}}$ | Dimensionless | 0.20вҖ“0.40 (tropical) |
| 6 | Daylight Factor | $DF = \dfrac{E_{interior}}{E_{exterior}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 2$ for office |
| 7 | Simple Payback | $SPB = \dfrac{C_{initial}}{A_{savings}}$ | Years | $\leq 7$ ideal |
| 8 | Life Cycle Cost | $LCC = C_{initial} + \Sigma \dfrac{O_t}{(1+i)^t}$ | Currency | Minimum LCC wins |
| 9 | Embodied Carbon | $EC = \Sigma (M_i \times EF_i)$ | kgCOвӮӮe / mВІ | $\leq 400$ |
| 10 | Carbon Reduction % | $\Delta C = \dfrac{C_{baseline} - C_{green}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | 30вҖ“50% target |
| 11 | On-site Renewable Fraction | $ORF = \dfrac{E_{renewable}}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | 100% = Net Zero |
| 12 | Waste Diversion Rate | $WDR = \dfrac{W_{recycled} + W_{reused}}{W_{total}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 75$ for Platinum |
| 13 | Storm-water Runoff Coeff. | $C = \dfrac{Q_{runoff}}{Q_{rainfall}}$ | Dimensionless | $\leq 0.5$ post-development |
| 14 | Heat Gain through wall | $Q = U \times A \times \Delta T$ | W | Lower is better |
| 15 | Thermal Transmittance | $U = \dfrac{1}{\Sigma R}$ | W/mВІВ·K | Lower = better insulation |

> **Critical formatting note for students:** All unit symbols use `В·` (middle dot) or `/` вҖ” never the pipe character `|` вҖ” to maintain valid markdown tables in KTU digital portal uploads.

---

## 2.3 Green Building Rating Systems вҖ” Comparison Matrix

### 2.3.1 Global Systems

| Rating System | Country of Origin | Year | Certifier | Levels (Low вҶ’ Highest) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LEED** (Leadership in Energy & Environmental Design) | USA | 1998 | USGBC | Certified вҶ’ Silver вҶ’ Gold вҶ’ Platinum |
| **BREEAM** (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method) | UK | 1990 | BRE | Pass вҶ’ Good вҶ’ Very Good вҶ’ Excellent вҶ’ Outstanding |
| **WELL** | USA | 2014 | IWBI | Bronze вҶ’ Silver вҶ’ Gold вҶ’ Platinum |
| **DGNB** | Germany | 2007 | DGNB | Bronze вҶ’ Silver вҶ’ Gold вҶ’ Platinum |
| **CASBEE** | Japan | 2001 | JSBC | C вҶ’ BвҲ’ вҶ’ B+ вҶ’ A вҶ’ S |

### 2.3.2 Indian Systems

| Rating System | Country | Custodian | Levels (Low вҶ’ Highest) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GRIHA** (Green Rating for Integrated Habitat Assessment) | India | TERI + MNRE | 1 Star вҶ’ 5 Star |
| **IGBC** (Indian Green Building Council) | India | CII | Certified вҶ’ Silver вҶ’ Gold вҶ’ Platinum |
| **EDGE** | IFC (World Bank) | IFC | Certified |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** GRIHA is the Indian government's own rating system (endorsed by MNRE); IGBC is industry-driven (CII). **LEED is the most globally accepted certification** and the most commonly appearing rating system in KTU questions.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Rating Categories

The six LEED categories with their **maximum point allocations** are:

| LEED Category | Abbrev. | Max Points (BD+C v4.1) |
|---|---|---|
| Location & Transportation | LT | 16 |
| Sustainable Sites | SS | 10 |
| Water Efficiency | WE | 11 |
| Energy & Atmosphere | EA | 33 |
| Materials & Resources | MR | 13 |
| Indoor Environmental Quality | EQ | 16 |
| Innovation | IN | 6 |
| Regional Priority | RP | 4 |
| **Total** | вҖ” | **110** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Certification thresholds for LEED v4.1 BD+C:**
> - **Certified:** 40вҖ“49 points
> - **Silver:** 50вҖ“59 points
> - **Gold:** 60вҖ“79 points
> - **Platinum:** 80+ points

The **GRIHA** system has 31 criteria spread across 7 sections, with a 100-point base score; **5-Star = 90+ points**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Green-building principles directly translate into:

1. **Reduced Operational Expenditure (OpEx):** A LEED-Gold certified office saves ~20% on energy bills.
2. **Higher Asset Value:** Green-certified buildings command **7вҖ“17% rental premium**.
3. **Occupant Productivity:** Studies (LEED & WELL joint research) show **+11% productivity** in WELL-certified spaces.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** ECBC+ (Energy Conservation Building Code) mandates energy performance in India.
5. **Disaster Resilience:** Green buildings with passive cooling perform better during grid outages.

---

## 2.6 The 7 Principles of Sustainable Design (KTU Favourite)

1. **Optimise Site Potential** вҖ” orient building, preserve topsoil, use brownfield.
2. **Optimise Energy Use** вҖ” passive design + efficient systems + renewables.
3. **Protect & Conserve Water** вҖ” rainwater harvesting, recycling, xeriscaping.
4. **Optimise Building Space & Material Use** вҖ” modular construction, recycled content.
5. **Enhance Indoor Environmental Quality** вҖ” daylight, fresh air, low VOC.
6. **Optimise Operational & Maintenance Practices** вҖ” commissioning, BMS, IAQ monitoring.
7. **Consider the Surrounding Community** вҖ” connectivity, walkability, mixed-use.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 вҖ” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive worked-out numerical derivations** of the most important green-building metrics that the KTU examiner tests. Since this topic is concept-heavy, the "derivations" are quantitative worked examples showing every algebraic step.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 вҖ” Energy Use Intensity (EUI) Calculation

### Problem Statement

A proposed green office building has a total conditioned floor area of **5,000 mВІ**. The annual electrical energy consumption from HVAC, lighting, and plug loads is **350,000 kWh**, and the annual solar thermal contribution (renewable on-site generation) is **50,000 kWh** equivalent. Calculate:

- (i) Net Energy Use Intensity (EUI)
- (ii) On-site Renewable Fraction (ORF)

### Given Data
- Total floor area: $A_{floor} = 5000 \text{ m}^2$
- Annual electricity consumed: $E_{consumed} = 350{,}000 \text{ kWh}$
- Annual renewable generated on-site: $E_{renewable} = 50{,}000 \text{ kWh}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 вҖ” Calculate Net Energy Used**

The net energy used by the building = Total energy consumed вҲ’ Energy generated on-site.

$$
E_{net} = E_{consumed} - E_{renewable}
$$

$$
E_{net} = 350{,}000 - 50{,}000 = 300{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Calculate Net EUI**

By the formula from Section 2.2:

$$
EUI_{net} = \frac{E_{net}}{A_{floor}}
$$

$$
EUI_{net} = \frac{300{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{5{,}000 \text{ m}^2} = 60 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Calculate On-site Renewable Fraction (ORF)**

$$
ORF = \frac{E_{renewable}}{E_{consumed}} \times 100
$$

$$
ORF = \frac{50{,}000}{350{,}000} \times 100 = 14.29\%
$$

### Interpretation

- $EUI_{net} = 60 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}$ is well within the **GRIHA-5 target** of $\leq 90 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}$.
- $ORF = 14.29\%$ means the building still depends on the grid for ~85% of its energy. To achieve **Net Zero**, ORF must reach **100%**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 вҖ” Overall U-value of a Composite Wall

### Problem Statement

A composite wall of a green building in Kerala consists of the following layers (interior to exterior):

| Layer | Thickness $d_i$ (m) | Thermal Conductivity $k_i$ (W/mВ·K) |
|---|---|---|
| Cement plaster | 0.012 | 0.72 |
| AAC block | 0.200 | 0.16 |
| EPS insulation | 0.050 | 0.035 |
| Sand-cement plaster | 0.012 | 0.72 |

The internal surface resistance $R_{si} = 0.13 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$ and external surface resistance $R_{se} = 0.04 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}$. Calculate the **Overall U-value** and the **heat gain per mВІ** when the temperature difference is $\Delta T = 12 \text{ K}$.

### Step 1 вҖ” Calculate Thermal Resistance of Each Layer

For each layer, $R_i = \dfrac{d_i}{k_i}$.

$$
R_{cement1} = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{AAC} = \frac{0.200}{0.16} = 1.25000 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{EPS} = \frac{0.050}{0.035} = 1.42857 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{cement2} = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Sum All Resistances

$$
R_{total} = R_{si} + R_{cement1} + R_{AAC} + R_{EPS} + R_{cement2} + R_{se}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 0.13 + 0.01667 + 1.25 + 1.42857 + 0.01667 + 0.04
$$

$$
R_{total} = 2.88191 \text{ m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Compute Overall U-value

$$
U = \frac{1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1}{2.88191} = 0.3470 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Heat Gain per Square Metre

$$
Q = U \times A \times \Delta T
$$

For 1 mВІ of wall area:

$$
q = 0.3470 \times 1 \times 12 = 4.164 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

### Interpretation

$U = 0.347 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ is below the ECBC+ tropical wall limit of 0.35 W/mВІВ·K. **Without the 50 mm EPS layer, U would be ~1.18 W/mВІВ·K вҖ” proving insulation contributes ~70% reduction in heat gain.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 вҖ” Carbon Reduction & Payback Period

### Problem Statement

A conventional building emits **8,500 kgCOвӮӮe/month**. After green retrofit, emissions reduce to **5,500 kgCOвӮӮe/month**. The retrofit cost is **вӮ№24,00,000**, and each kgCOвӮӮe avoided saves **вӮ№3.5** in carbon-tax and energy costs. Calculate:

- (i) Monthly carbon savings in kgCOвӮӮe.
- (ii) Annual monetary savings.
- (iii) Simple Payback Period.

### Step 1 вҖ” Monthly Carbon Savings

$$
\Delta C_{monthly} = 8{,}500 - 5{,}500 = 3{,}000 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e/month}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Annual Carbon Savings

$$
\Delta C_{annual} = 3{,}000 \times 12 = 36{,}000 \text{ kgCO}_2\text{e/yr}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Annual Monetary Savings

$$
A_{savings} = 36{,}000 \times 3.5 = \text{вӮ№ }1{,}26{,}000 \text{ / yr}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Simple Payback Period

$$
SPB = \frac{C_{initial}}{A_{savings}}
$$

$$
SPB = \frac{24{,}00{,}000}{1{,}26{,}000} = 19.05 \text{ years}
$$

### Interpretation

A **19-year payback is too long** for a typical project (target вүӨ 7 years). The student must recommend either: (a) increasing the carbon price assumed, (b) including health-productivity savings (~2Г— the energy savings), or (c) seeking an IGBC Platinum subsidy.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 вҖ” Waste Diversion Rate

### Problem Statement

During construction of a 10,000 mВІ green building, total waste generated = **1,200 tonnes**. Recycled waste = **420 tonnes**, Reused on-site waste = **380 tonnes**, and Landfilled = **400 tonnes**. Calculate the **Waste Diversion Rate (WDR)** and comment on LEED Platinum compliance.

### Solution

$$
WDR = \frac{W_{recycled} + W_{reused}}{W_{total}} \times 100
$$

$$
WDR = \frac{420 + 380}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \frac{800}{1{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
WDR = 66.67\%
$$

### Interpretation

$WDR = 66.67\%$ вҖ” **falls short** of the LEED Platinum target of **75%**. The contractor must re-route additional ~100 tonnes from landfill (e.g., concrete crushing for sub-base, gypsum recycling).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 вҖ” Storm-water Runoff Coefficient

### Problem Statement

A site of 2 hectares (20,000 mВІ) has pre-development runoff coefficient $C_{pre} = 0.20$ (forest). Post-development with impermeable pavement, $C_{post} = 0.85$. Annual rainfall = **2,500 mm**. Calculate:

- (i) Pre-development annual runoff volume.
- (ii) Post-development annual runoff volume.
- (iii) Increase in runoff volume that must be captured by rainwater harvesting.

### Step 1 вҖ” Pre-development Runoff

$$
Q_{pre} = C_{pre} \times A \times P
$$

$$
Q_{pre} = 0.20 \times 20{,}000 \times 2.5 = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Post-development Runoff

$$
Q_{post} = 0.85 \times 20{,}000 \times 2.5 = 42{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Required Capture Volume

$$
\Delta Q = Q_{post} - Q_{pre} = 42{,}500 - 10{,}000 = 32{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Equivalent Daily Litres per mВІ

$$
\text{Daily excess} = \frac{32{,}500}{365 \times 20{,}000} \times 1000 = 4.45 \text{ L/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To match pre-development hydrology (zero-discharge), the building must provide storage capacity to detain ~**32,500 mВі/yr** in detention ponds, recharge pits, or permeable pavements.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix вҖ” Certification Requirements

| Parameter | LEED Certified | LEED Silver | LEED Gold | LEED Platinum |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Points | 40вҖ“49 | 50вҖ“59 | 60вҖ“79 | 80+ |
| Energy Savings vs ASHRAE 90.1 | 10% | 15% | 25% | 40%+ |
| Water Reduction | 20% | 30% | 40% | 50%+ |
| Waste Diversion | 50% | 60% | 70% | 75%+ |
| Daylight Factor (offices) | 1.5% | 2.0% | 2.5% | 3.0%+ |
| Ventilation Effectiveness | 0.8 | 1.0 | 1.2 | 1.5+ |

This matrix is the **highest-yield revision table** for any 14-mark question on rating systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 вҖ” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Green Building Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **six pillars of green design** to their **inputs, active/passive interventions, and outputs**. Every node uses alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters to satisfy Mermaid v10 safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Site Context and Climate Analysis] --> nodeB[Passive Design Layer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Energy Efficient Systems]
    nodeC --> nodeD[On Site Renewables and BMS]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Indoor Environmental Quality]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Water and Waste Loops]

    subgraph P1[SUSTAINABLE SITE]
        nodeB1[Orientation E-W] --> nodeB
```

## рҹ“қ PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 вҖ” `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a Green Building. List any four sustainable features incorporated in green buildings.**

#### Model Answer

A **green building** is a structure designed, constructed, and operated to minimise its environmental impact while maximising occupant comfort and resource efficiency throughout its life cycle.

**Four sustainable features:**

1. **Energy efficiency** вҖ” High-performance envelope, LED lighting, VRF HVAC.
2. **Water conservation** вҖ” Rainwater harvesting, low-flow fixtures, greywater recycling.
3. **Sustainable materials** вҖ” Recycled steel, fly-ash bricks, low-VOC paints.
4. **Indoor environmental quality** вҖ” Abundant daylight, fresh-air ventilation, low COвӮӮ levels.

**(3 marks вҖ” 1 for definition + 0.5 each for features)**

---

## рҹ“қ PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question B (Module Internal Choice вҖ” Choose ONE)

---

### вңҚпёҸ Question B-A вҖ” `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **six categories of LEED rating system** with their maximum points. Describe the certification thresholds for each level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6,000 mВІ office building consumes 480,000 kWh/yr. After green retrofit, consumption drops to 312,000 kWh/yr. The retrofit cost is вӮ№30 lakhs and electricity tariff is вӮ№8/kWh. Calculate the annual monetary savings, payback period, and percentage reduction in EUI.

---

#### Model Answer to B-A (a)

**LEED v4.1 BD+C Six Categories:**

1. **Location and Transportation (LT) вҖ” 16 points**
   - Proximity to public transit, bicycle facilities, EV charging, parking capacity.
2. **Sustainable Sites (SS) вҖ” 10 points**
   - Site assessment, heat-island reduction, light-pollution reduction.
3. **Water Efficiency (WE) вҖ” 11 points**
   - Indoor water-use reduction, outdoor water-use reduction, cooling-tower water.
4. **Energy and Atmosphere (EA) вҖ” 33 points**
   - Fundamental & enhanced commissioning, energy performance, demand response, renewables, refrigerant management.
5. **Materials and Resources (MR) вҖ” 13 points**
   - Construction & demolition waste management, recycled content, regional materials, material disclosure.
6. **Indoor Environmental Quality (EQ) вҖ” 16 points**
   - Daylight, views, low-emitting materials, thermal comfort, acoustic performance, ventilation.

Additional: **Innovation (6) + Regional Priority (4) = 110 points total.**

**Certification Thresholds:**

| Level | Points |
|---|---|
| Certified | 40вҖ“49 |
| Silver | 50вҖ“59 |
| Gold | 60вҖ“79 |
| Platinum | 80+ |

**(7 marks: 5 categories Г— 1 mark + thresholds 2 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to B-A (b)

**Given Data**

- $A_{floor} = 6{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- $E_{before} = 480{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$
- $E_{after} = 312{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}$
- Retrofit cost $C_{initial} = вӮ№30{,}00{,}000$
- Tariff = вӮ№8 / kWh

**Step 1 вҖ” Annual Energy Savings**

$$
\Delta E = 480{,}000 - 312{,}000 = 168{,}000 \text{ kWh/yr}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Annual Monetary Savings**

$$
A_{savings} = 168{,}000 \times 8 = вӮ№13{,}44{,}000 / \text{yr}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Simple Payback Period**

$$
SPB = \frac{C_{initial}}{A_{savings}} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{13{,}44{,}000} = 2.23 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Percentage Reduction in EUI**

Pre-retrofit EUI:

$$
EUI_{before} = \frac{480{,}000}{6{,}000} = 80 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}
$$

Post-retrofit EUI:

$$
EUI_{after} = \frac{312{,}000}{6{,}000} = 52 \text{ kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}
$$

Percentage reduction:

$$
\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{80 - 52}{80} \times 100 = 35\%
$$

**Inference:** EUI falls from 80 вҶ’ 52 kWh/mВІ/yr (a 35% reduction), well within LEED Gold energy thresholds. A payback of ~2.23 years is excellent and easily clears the 7-year target. **(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)**

---

### вңҚпёҸ Question B-B вҖ” `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** List and explain the **seven principles of sustainable design** as per the KTU syllabus. Provide one real-world example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the **energy-efficiency intervention hierarchy** in green buildings. Justify why passive design is prioritised over renewable generation.

---

#### Model Answer to B-B (a)

**The Seven Principles of Sustainable Design (with examples):**

1. **Optimise Site Potential** вҖ” Orient the building East-West to minimise heat gain. *Example: CII-Godrej GBC, Hyderabad (long axis E-W).*

2. **Optimise Energy Use** вҖ” Combine passive design with high-efficiency HVAC. *Example: Infosys Pocharam campus uses radiant cooling + VRF.*

3. **Protect & Conserve Water** вҖ” Harvest rainwater; recycle greywater for landscape. *Example: ITC Royal Bengal (Kolkata) saves 35% water.*

4. **Optimise Building Space & Material Use** вҖ” Use modular construction and recycled steel. *Example: Suzlon One Earth uses fly-ash bricks + recycled-content steel.*

5. **Enhance Indoor Environmental Quality** вҖ” Provide daylight, low-VOC paints, fresh-air ventilation. *Example: Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology (RMIT).*

6. **Optimise Operational & Maintenance Practices** вҖ” Implement BMS + IAQ monitoring + commissioning. *Example: Edge Olympic, Amsterdam вҖ” uses sensors + AI to optimise spaces.*

7. **Consider the Surrounding Community** вҖ” Promote walkability, mixed-use zoning. *Example: Auroville, India вҖ” pedestrian-first master planning.*

**(7 marks: 1 mark per principle; 0.5 if example is missing)**

---

#### Model Answer to B-B (b)

**Energy-Efficiency Intervention Hierarchy (Schematic + Explanation)**

The hierarchy prioritises interventions based on **cost-to-impact ratio** and **life-cycle effectiveness:**

| Priority | Intervention | Cost | Payback | Embodied Carbon |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Passive Design (orientation, insulation, daylight, NV) | Low | < 2 yr | None |
| **P2** | Efficient Systems (VRF, LED, low-flow) | Medium | 3вҖ“6 yr | Medium |
| **P3** | Renewables + BMS (PV, BESS, IoT) | High | 6вҖ“10 yr | High |

**Schematic Flow:**

```
PASSIVE DESIGN  в”Җв”Җв–ә  EFFICIENT SYSTEMS  в”Җв”Җв–ә  RENEWABLES + SMART
   (cheapest,           (mid-cost,              (expensive, but
    highest leverage)    second leverage)         offsets grid carbon)
```

**Why Passive Design is Prioritised (3 Justifications):**

1. **First-Law Thermodynamics:** Reducing load before supplying it is always more efficient than generating to meet it (i.e., *saving 1 kWh at the wall > generating 1 kWh via PV*).
2. **Zero Embodied Carbon:** Passive measures have no operational emissions and minimal manufacturing footprint, unlike PV panels that take ~2вҖ“3 years to "pay back" their embodied energy.
3. **Resilience:** Passive design (cross-ventilation, daylight) continues to function during power outages вҖ” essential for tropical climates like Kerala with grid disruptions during monsoons.

**(7 marks: 3 for hierarchy table/schematic + 4 for three justifications)**

---

## вҡ пёҸ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Green Building Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing LEED with BREEAM** вҖ” They are NOT the same system. USGBC owns LEED; BRE owns BREEAM. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for confusing them.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the units** вҖ” Writing EUI as "80" without "kWh/mВІ/yr" loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the calculation steps** вҖ” Even in 14-mark answers, if Part (b) is numerical, show ALL five steps (Given, Formula, Substitution, Solution, Result). Partial marks depend on visible steps.
> 4. **Memorising point allocations wrongly** вҖ” LEED total is **110 points** (not 100). GRIHA total is **100 points**.
> 5. **Using "Green" and "Smart" interchangeably** вҖ” Smart = automation; Green = sustainability. They overlap but are distinct. Examiners will deduct marks if you claim a green building is "smart" without qualification.
> 6. **Ignoring Indian context** вҖ” Kerala-specific climate (tropical, high humidity, monsoon) demands **cross-ventilation + light-coloured roofs + shaded windows**. Always localise your answer to the Indian/Kerala scenario.
> 7. **Not writing the rating acronyms correctly** вҖ” Use **LEED v4.1**, **GRIHA-5**, **IGBC Platinum**. Spelling "GRIHA" as "GRHA" is a 0.5-mark deduction.
